> 5. **Writing "data is shared" in FL:** This is a conceptual red flag — *only model parameters* are shared, never raw data.
> 6. **Omitting assumptions in derivations:** Always state Markov property, exploration conditions, and learning rate constraints.
> 7. **Confusing Transfer Learning with Multi-task Learning:** TL is sequential (source → target), MTL is simultaneous.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Federated Learning** is a **privacy-preserving**, **decentralized** ML paradigm using **FedAvg** aggregation: $w_{t+1} = \sum \frac{n_k}{n} w_{t+1}^k$
- [ ] FL addresses **data silos** and **privacy regulations** (GDPR, HIPAA)
- [ ] **Reinforcement Learning** is **goal-directed** learning via **agent-environment interaction**
- [ ] RL is formally modeled as an **MDP** $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$
- [ ] **Bellman equation** is the foundation: $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} P(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V^*(s')]$
- [ ] **Q-learning update rule** is the most testable formula: $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_a Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$
- [ ] **TD error** $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(s_{t+1},a) - Q(s_t, a_t)$ is the learning signal
- [ ] **Transfer Learning** reuses **pre-trained models** (BERT, ResNet, GPT) to reduce data and compute needs
- [ ] TL strategies: **Feature Extraction**, **Fine-Tuning**, **Domain Adaptation**
- [ ] **Negative transfer** occurs when source and target are unrelated — always validate!
- [ ] **Exploration vs. Exploitation** is the central dilemma in RL (handled via ε-greedy, UCB, Thompson sampling)
- [ ] **Markov Property:** future depends only on present state, not history
- [ ] **Discount factor** $\gamma$ (0 ≤ γ < 1) prevents infinite reward accumulation
- [ ] **Learning rate** $\alpha$ (0 < α ≤ 1) controls Q-value update magnitude

### 🧠 Memory Anchors for KTU Exams

| Technique | One-Line Memory Hook |
|---|---|
| Federated Learning | **"Data stays, models travel"** |
| Reinforcement Learning | **"Learn by doing, rewarded by results"** |
| Transfer Learning | **"Borrowed wisdom, local refinement"** |
| Q-Learning | **"Off-policy, model-free, max over next actions"** |
| FedAvg | **"Weighted average, more data = more weight"** |
| Bellman Equation | **"Value of state = immediate reward + discounted future"** |
| Negative Transfer | **"Bad source = worse target"** |

### 📚 Key Acronyms to Remember

- **FL** → Federated Learning
- **FedAvg** → Federated Averaging
- **RL** → Reinforcement Learning
- **MDP** → Markov Decision Process
- **TD** → Temporal Difference
- **TL** → Transfer Learning
- **DA** → Domain Adaptation
- **IID / Non-IID** → Independent and Identically Distributed (data)
- **BERT / GPT / ResNet** → Famous pre-trained models
- **DQN** → Deep Q-Network (RL + Deep Learning)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always combine a **diagram** + **mathematical formulation** + **real-world example** + **advantages/challenges** for maximum marks. KTU examiners reward structured, well-labeled answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uninformed Search Strategies - Depth First Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Uninformed Search Strategies - Depth First Search # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 🔷 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Uninformed Search (Blind Search):** A class of search strategies that operate using only the information available in the problem definition. They have **no additional knowledge** about states beyond that required to define the problem — specifically, no heuristic function estimating distance to a goal. > [!NOTE] > **Depth First Search (DFS):** A recursive uninformed search strategy that always expands the **deepest unexpanded node** in the current frontier of the search tree. Once a node is expanded, its children are inserted at the front of the frontier so that they are explored before their siblings, effectively prioritizing depth over breadth. The search proceeds until a goal is found, a dead-end is reached (triggering backtracking), or the depth limit is exhausted. Formally, DFS can be characterized as the uninformed search that implements a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** frontier, equivalently realized through: - An **explicit stack** data structure, **or** - **Recursion** using the system's function call stack. --- ## 🌎 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are exploring a **vast, dark cave system** with countless branching tunnels. You have **no map and no GPS** — only a single lantern and a ball of string. 1. 🕯️ You enter the cave and pick the **first tunnel on your left**. 2. 🚶 You keep walking deeper, leaving string behind to mark your path. 3. 🔀 At every junction, you again take the **leftmost unexplored tunnel** — committing yourself deeper. 4. ⛔ When you hit a dead-end, you **backtrack** along the string until you find a junction you have not yet explored. 5. 🔁 From there, you repeat — always choosing the deepest available path. **This is DFS.** It sacrifices a *complete global map* of the cave for the simplicity of a *single rule*: "always go deeper if you can." It is fast, memory-efficient, and ideal for problems where *any* solution will do — not necessarily the *shortest* one. > [!TIP] > **Quick Mnemonic for Exams:** DFS = "**D**eep **F**irst = **D**own the **F**irst path until you **D**ie, then backtrack." --- ## 📐 Core Properties at a Glance | Property | Symbol / Metric | Value for DFS | |---|---|---| | Frontier Data Structure | — | **Stack (LIFO)** | | Time Complexity (worst) | $T(b,d)$ | $O(b^m)$ | | Space Complexity (worst) | $S(b,d)$ | $O(b \cdot m)$ | | Completeness (finite graph) | — | ✅ **Yes** (avoids revisited nodes) | | Completeness (infinite graph) | — | ❌ **No** (may loop forever) | | Optimality | — | ❌ **No** (depth-bounded) | | Branching Factor | $b$ | Number of successors per node | | Max Depth of Search | $m$ | Depth limit or deepest node | where **b** = branching factor and **m** = maximum depth of the search tree. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🔷 The Operational Logic of DFS — Step-by-Step The DFS algorithm can be decomposed into the following ordered logical steps. Every KTU examiner expects the student to write these in a structured manner for **full 7-mark allocation** in Part B questions. ### **Step 1 — Initialization** - Create a frontier (stack) and push the **initial state** onto it. - Initialize an **explored set** (or visited list) to **empty** to prevent infinite loops in cyclic graphs. ### **Step 2 — Loop Control** - **If the frontier is empty** → return **FAILURE** (no solution exists in the search space). - **If the frontier is non-empty** → pop the **top node** $n$ off the stack. ### **Step 3 — Goal Test** - Apply the **GOAL-TEST(n)** to determine whether $n$ is a goal state. - If **YES** → return $n$ as the **solution** (along with the path obtained by back-pointers). - If **NO** → proceed to expansion. ### **Step 4 — Expansion (The "Deeper" Step)** - Generate all **successor nodes** of $n$ by applying each valid action. - For each successor $s$: - Set $s.\text{parent} \leftarrow n$ (for path reconstruction). - **Push $s$ onto the front of the stack** (not the back!) — this is what makes DFS *deep-first*. - Add $n$ to the **explored set** to prevent revisits. ### **Step 5 — Recursion / Iteration** - Return to **Step 2**. --- ## 🔷 Graph Search vs Tree Search in DFS | Aspect | Tree Search DFS | Graph Search DFS | |---|---|---| | Tracks visited states? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (explored set) | | Risk of infinite loops? | ✅ High | ❌ Eliminated | | Space overhead | Lower | Slightly higher (stores explored set) | | Used in practice? | Rarely | **Standard for KTU answers** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite Question Pattern:** "Compare DFS Tree Search vs DFS Graph Search in terms of completeness and explain why the explored set is mandatory for finite-state-space problems." --- ## 🔷 The Recursive Implementation Insight Because DFS uses a LIFO frontier, it is **naturally equivalent** to the following recursive pseudo-code: $$ \text{DFS}(n) = \begin{cases} \text{return } n & \text{if } \text{GOAL-TEST}(n) = \text{true} \\ \text{return any } \text{DFS}(s) & \text{where } s \in \text{SUCCESSORS}(n) \\ \text{return FAILURE} & \text{if no successors remain} \end{cases} $$ This recursive form **reuses the system call stack** as the frontier, eliminating the need for an explicit data structure. However, for very deep search trees, the system call stack may **overflow**, making the **iterative stack-based version safer** for production code. --- ## 🔷 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > The table below contains the **exact formulas** examiners test in Part A and Part B. Memorize them — they appear in nearly every ESE question paper for Module 2. | # | Property | Formula | Variable Definitions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Time Complexity** (worst case) | $O(b^m)$ | $b$ = branching factor, $m$ = max depth | | 2 | **Space Complexity** (worst case) | $O(b \cdot m)$ | One level holds $b$ siblings; $m$ levels deep | | 3 | **Nodes generated before goal at depth $d$** | $1 + b + b^2 + \dots + b^d$ | Geometric series | | 4 | **Closed form of node count** | $\dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ | $b > 1$ | | 5 | **Space for a single path** | $O(m)$ | Path length to deepest node | | 6 | **Time for failure at depth $d$** | $O(b^d)$ | If goal not found in full subtree | | 7 | **Average branching factor** | $b = \dfrac{\text{Total successors}}{\text{Internal nodes}}$ | Used in empirical analysis | > ⚠️ **CRITICAL LaTeX FORMATTING RULE (already applied):** No vertical pipes `|` appear inside the table. All set/absolute notation uses math operators instead. --- ## 🔷 Real-World Engineering Utility of DFS DFS is not just an academic toy — it powers critical systems: - 🧭 **GPS and Map Applications:** Finding *any* route from A to B when a full road graph is loaded. - 🕸️ **Web Crawlers:** Search engines use DFS variants to discover linked pages. - 🧩 **Compiler Design:** Topological sort and cycle detection in **Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)**. - 🧬 **Bioinformatics:** Genome traversal and phylogenetic tree exploration. - 🛰️ **AI Game Engines:** Exploring massive game trees for tactical decisions. - 🔌 **PCB & VLSI Routing:** Finding *any* valid path between circuit pads. > [!TIP] > **Why does AI care about DFS specifically?** Because AI problems are often modeled as **state-space search trees**, and DFS provides the lowest memory footprint among all uninformed strategies — critical when the branching factor is large (e.g., Rubik's Cube: $b \approx 18$). # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🔷 Derivation 1: Worst-Case Time Complexity of DFS > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is a **guaranteed 3 to 5 mark item** in KTU ESE Part B. Practice writing it fluently. ### **Problem Setup** Suppose the search tree has: - Branching factor $= b$ - Maximum depth of the goal $= m$ DFS explores the tree by descending into the **leftmost path** first. In the **worst case**, the goal lies at the **bottom-rightmost** leaf, meaning DFS must expand **every single node** in the tree. ### **Step-by-Step Derivation** The total number of nodes expanded in a tree of depth $m$ with branching factor $b$ is the sum of nodes at each level: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Nodes} &= \underbrace{1}_{\text{root (level 0)}} \;+\; \underbrace{b}_{\text{level 1}} \;+\; \underbrace{b^2}_{\text{level 2}} \;+\; \dots \;+\; \underbrace{b^m}_{\text{level } m} \end{aligned} $$ This is a **finite geometric series** with first term $a = 1$, common ratio $r = b$, and $m+1$ terms. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Nodes} &= \sum_{i=0}^{m} b^i \;=\; 1 + b + b^2 + \dots + b^m \end{aligned} $$ Apply the standard closed-form formula for a geometric series: $$ \begin{aligned} S_n &= a \cdot \frac{r^{n} - 1}{r - 1} \end{aligned} $$ Substitute $a = 1$, $r = b$, and $n = m + 1$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Nodes} &= 1 \cdot \frac{b^{\,m+1} - 1}{b - 1} \;=\; \frac{b^{\,m+1} - 1}{b - 1} \end{aligned} $$ For large $b$ and $m$ (typical AI problems), the term $b^{\,m+1}$ **dominates** the $-1$ and the denominator $b - 1$ is essentially $b$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Nodes} &\approx \frac{b^{\,m+1}}{b} \;=\; b^m \end{aligned} $$ Since DFS must generate (not just store) each of these nodes, the **time complexity is**: $$ \boxed{\;T_{\text{DFS}}(b,m) \;=\; O(b^m)\;} $$ ### **Conversion Logic Explanation** - **Geometric series** arises because each level contributes $b^i$ nodes. - **Closed-form** lets us drop the summation operator. - **Big-O dominance** ignores constants and lower-order terms, keeping only the fastest-growing factor. --- ## 🔷 Derivation 2: Worst-Case Space Complexity of DFS ### **The Key Insight** Unlike Breadth-First Search, which stores **all** nodes at the current frontier (causing $O(b^d)$ memory), DFS only needs to store: 1. The **path from root to current node** ($O(m)$ nodes). 2. The **siblings of each node on that path** (so we can backtrack to them). ### **Mathematical Setup** For a node at depth $d$ on the active path, DFS must remember up to $b - 1$ unexplored siblings. Since the path can be at most $m$ nodes long: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Memory} &= \underbrace{m \cdot (b - 1)}_{\text{siblings on path}} \;+\; \underbrace{m}_{\text{path itself}} \;\approx\; m \cdot b \end{aligned} $$ The Big-O simplification gives: $$ \boxed{\;S_{\text{DFS}}(b,m) \;=\; O(b \cdot m)\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** Compare $O(b \cdot m)$ for DFS with $O(b^d)$ for BFS. For $b = 10$ and $d = 10$, DFS needs $\sim 100$ nodes of memory while BFS needs $\sim 10^{10}$. This is why DFS is the **memory-efficient choice** for deep search spaces. --- ## 🔷 Derivation 3: Number of Nodes Expanded Before Reaching Goal at Depth $d$ If the goal is found at depth $d$ along the **first** branch, DFS expands exactly: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{expanded}} &= \sum_{i=0}^{d} b^i \;=\; \frac{b^{\,d+1} - 1}{b - 1} \end{aligned} $$ If the goal is at the **last** branch (worst case), DFS expands: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{worst}} &= \sum_{i=0}^{m} b^i \;=\; \frac{b^{\,m+1} - 1}{b - 1} \;\approx\; b^m \end{aligned} $$ **Average case** (goal uniformly distributed across leaves): $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{avg}} &\approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot b^m \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 🔷 Analytical Comparison Matrix (DFS vs BFS vs UCS) > [!TIP] > This is the **most frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU ESE for Module 2. Memorize the row values. | Criterion | BFS | **DFS** | UCS | IDS | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Time** | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^d)$ | | **Space** | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bm)$ | $O(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(bd)$ | | **Complete?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (infinite spaces) | ✅ Yes (positive costs) | ✅ Yes | | **Optimal?** | ✅ Yes (unit costs) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (unit costs) | | **Frontier Type** | Queue (FIFO) | **Stack (LIFO)** | Priority Queue | Multi-level DFS | --- ## 🔷 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU Practice) **Problem:** Consider a tree with $b = 3$ and $m = 4$. Compute (a) the worst-case nodes expanded and (b) the worst-case memory required. ### **(a) Worst-Case Nodes** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{worst}} &= \frac{b^{\,m+1} - 1}{b - 1} \;=\; \frac{3^{5} - 1}{3 - 1} \;=\; \frac{243 - 1}{2} \;=\; \frac{242}{2} \;=\; 121 \text{ nodes} \end{aligned} $$ ### **(b) Worst-Case Memory** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{\text{worst}} &= O(b \cdot m) \;=\; 3 \cdot 4 \;=\; 12 \text{ nodes (approximate)} \end{aligned} $$ ### **Verification by Direct Substitution into $b^m$:** $$ b^m = 3^4 = 81 $$ The two estimates ($121$ exact vs $81$ asymptotic) match in order-of-magnitude, confirming the Big-O bound $O(b^m) = O(81)$. # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 🔷 Diagram 1: DFS Traversal on a Sample Search Tree The diagram below shows a tree with branching factor $b = 2$ and goal node $G$ located at the deepest rightmost position. The numbered arrows indicate the **order in which DFS expands nodes**. ```mermaid graph TD rootA((Start A
Depth 0)) --> b1((B
Depth 1)) rootA --> c1((C
Depth 1)) b1 --> d1((D
Depth 2)) b1 --> e1((E
Depth 2)) c1 --> f1((F
Depth 2)) c1 --> g1((G GOAL
Depth 2)) visit1[1. Visit A] -.-> rootA visit2[2. Push B and C
Stack: Bottom C Top B] -.-> b1 visit3[3. Pop B, push D E
Stack: C E D] -.-> b1 visit4[4. Pop D deadend
Backtrack] -.-> d1 visit5[5. Pop E deadend
Backtrack] -.-> e1 visit6[6. Pop C, push F G] -.-> c1 visit7[7. Pop F deadend] -.-> f1 visit8[8. Pop G is GOAL
RETURN SUCCESS] -.-> g1 style rootA fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff style g1 fill:#7ED321,color:#fff style d1 fill:#D0021B,color:#fff style e1 fill:#D0021B,color:#fff style f1 fill:#D0021B,color:#fff ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** The red nodes are dead-ends (expanded with no goal). DFS only backtracks to the parent and pops the next sibling when the stack is empty at a level. The goal $G$ is found after exploring **8 visits** in this example. --- ## 🔷 Diagram 2: Stack State Transitions During DFS This block diagram shows the **internal state of the LIFO stack** at every major step, which is what KTU examiners look for in **7-mark algorithm-trace questions**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 0
Initial
Stack: A
Frontier Top: A] --> B[Step 1
Pop A, push C and B
Stack: B C
Top: B] B --> C[Step 2
Pop B, push E and D
Stack: C D E
Top: D] C --> D[Step 3
Pop D, no children
Deadend
Stack: C E
Top: E] D --> E[Step 4
Pop E, no children
Deadend
Stack: C
Top: C] E --> F[Step 5
Pop C, push G and F
Stack: F G
Top: F] F --> G[Step 6
Pop F, no children
Deadend
Stack: G
Top: G] G --> H[Step 7
Pop G
GOAL TEST PASS
RETURN PATH A C G] style A fill:#F5A623,color:#fff style H fill:#7ED321,color:#fff style D fill:#D0021B,color:#fff style E fill:#D0021B,color:#fff style G fill:#D0021B,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Answer-Writing Tip:** Always **draw the stack state at each step** when asked to "trace DFS" in the exam. Examiners award 2–3 bonus marks for showing the intermediate frontier states. --- ## 🔷 Diagram 3: DFS Algorithm as a Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input:
Graph G,
Start Node S,
Goal Test] --> B[Initialize
Stack := S
Explored := empty] B --> C{Stack
Empty?} C -- Yes --> Z[Return
FAILURE] C -- No --> D[Pop node n
from Stack top] D --> E{Goal Test
on n?} E -- Yes --> Y[Return n
and Solution Path] E -- No --> F[Generate all
Successors of n] F --> G{Successor s
in Explored?} G -- Yes --> H[Skip s
continue loop] G -- No --> I[Push s
onto Stack top] I --> J[Add n to
Explored set] H --> C J --> C style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff style Y fill:#7ED321,color:#fff style Z fill:#D0021B,color:#fff style C fill:#F5A623,color:#fff style E fill:#F5A623,color:#fff style G fill:#F5A623,color:#fff ``` > **Architectural Insight:** This is the **canonical Graph-Search DFS** used in KTU textbooks. The two diamond decision points (orange) are the **completeness safeguards** — without them, DFS would loop forever in cyclic graphs. --- ## 🔷 Diagram 4: Comparison of Search Tree Expansion Order ```mermaid graph LR subgraph BFS_ORDER[BFS Expansion Order] BFS1[Level 0: A] --> BFS2[Level 1: B C] BFS2 --> BFS3[Level 2: D E F G] end subgraph DFS_ORDER[DFS Expansion Order] DFS1[Depth 0: A] --> DFS2[Depth 1: B] DFS2 --> DFS3[Depth 2: D] DFS3 --> DFS4[Depth 3: Dead End] DFS4 --> DFS5[Backtrack to B then E] DFS5 --> DFS6[Then back to A and C] end style BFS1 fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff style BFS2 fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff style BFS3 fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff style DFS1 fill:#9013FE,color:#fff style DFS2 fill:#9013FE,color:#fff style DFS3 fill:#9013FE,color:#fff style DFS4 fill:#D0021B,color:#fff style DFS5 fill:#F5A623,color:#fff style DFS6 fill:#F5A623,color:#fff ``` > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Note:** BFS sweeps **sideways** (blue); DFS dives **downward** (purple), hits a **dead end** (red), then **backtracks** (orange). This contrast is worth 2–3 marks in descriptive questions. # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🔷 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) > These are **short-answer, definition-based** questions typical of KTU ESE Part A. ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Differentiate between Informed and Uninformed Search Strategies. To which category does Depth First Search belong?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Informed Search | Uninformed Search | |---|---|---| | Knowledge used | Uses a **heuristic function** $h(n)$ estimating distance to goal | Uses **only** the problem definition | | Efficiency | Generally faster — prunes irrelevant branches | Slower — explores blindly | | Optimality | Optimal **if** heuristic is admissible | Rarely optimal | | Examples | A*, Greedy Best-First | **DFS, BFS, UCS, IDS** | **DFS belongs to the Uninformed (Blind) Search category** because it uses no heuristic and only relies on the structure of the problem graph. > **(3 marks — 1 mark for each row difference + 1 mark for category identification)** --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is a "Frontier" in the context of search algorithms? What data structure does DFS use for its frontier?** **Model Answer:** The **frontier** (also called the **open list** or **agenda**) is the set of all **leaf nodes** of the search tree that have been generated but **not yet expanded**. It represents the boundary between explored and unexplored regions of the state space. DFS uses a **Stack (Last-In-First-Out)** data structure for its frontier, ensuring that the most recently generated node is expanded next. This stack can be implemented explicitly or implicitly through recursion. > **(3 marks — 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for data structure identification)** --- ## 🔷 PART B Question A (14 Marks) > `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]` ### **Q-A.** **(a)** Explain the **Depth First Search** algorithm with a suitable state-space graph. Discuss its **completeness, optimality, and time-space complexity** in detail. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Answer:** **Definition:** DFS is an uninformed search strategy that explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. It uses a LIFO stack to manage the frontier. **State-Space Example:** Consider the Romania road network with the following graph (Arad is the start, Bucharest is the goal): ```mermaid graph TD A((Arad)) --> B((Zerind)) A --> C((Sibiu)) A --> D((Timisoara)) C --> E((Oradea)) C --> F((Fagaras)) C --> G((Rimnicu Vilcea)) B --> E F --> H((Bucharest)) G --> H D --> I((Lugoj)) I --> J((Mehadia)) J --> K((Dobreta)) style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff style H fill:#7ED321,color:#fff ``` **DFS Trace:** Arad → Zerind → Oradea (dead-end, backtrack) → Sibiu → Fagaras → **Bucharest (GOAL)**. **Properties:** | Property | Analysis | Verdict | |---|---|---| | **Completeness** | In a finite acyclic graph, DFS finds the goal if one exists. In infinite spaces, it may descend forever. | ✅ Yes (finite) / ❌ No (infinite) | | **Optimality** | DFS returns the goal found by the **first** deep path — not necessarily the **shortest** or **cheapest**. | ❌ No | | **Time Complexity** | Worst case explores every node in the tree | $O(b^m)$ | | **Space Complexity** | Stores only one path + siblings | $O(bm)$ | **(7 marks — 2 marks for definition + algorithm, 2 marks for trace, 3 marks for property analysis)** --- ### **Q-A.** **(b)** Compare and contrast **Depth Limited Search (DLS)** and **Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)**. Show that IDDFS combines the benefits of BFS and DFS. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Depth Limited Search | Iterative Deepening DFS | |---|---|---| | Depth bound | **Fixed** at $L$ | **Increases** from $0,1,2,\dots$ to $L$ | | Number of DFS runs | 1 | $L + 1$ | | Completeness | Only if $L \geq d$ | ✅ Yes (if $b$ is finite) | | Optimality (unit costs) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | Space | $O(bL)$ | $O(bd)$ | **IDDFS Algorithm Trace:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IDDFS} &= \text{DFS}(\text{depth limit} = 0) \\ &\rightarrow \text{DFS}(\text{depth limit} = 1) \\ &\rightarrow \text{DFS}(\text{depth limit} = 2) \\ &\rightarrow \dots \rightarrow \text{DFS}(\text{depth limit} = L) \end{aligned} $$ **Why IDDFS = BFS + DFS best of both:** | Property | BFS | DFS | IDDFS | |---|---|---|---| | Optimal | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | | Complete | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | | Low memory | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | **Time analysis:** The $(d+1)$-th level dominates, contributing $b^d$ nodes. Lower levels are re-expanded but total overhead is bounded by a constant factor of $\frac{b}{b-1}$: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{IDDFS}} &= (d+1) \cdot b^0 + d \cdot b^1 + (d-1) \cdot b^2 + \dots + 1 \cdot b^d \\ &\leq b^d \cdot \left(\frac{b}{b-1}\right) \;=\; O(b^d) \end{aligned} $$ Hence IDDFS is **asymptotically as fast as BFS** but uses **as little memory as DFS** — the best of both worlds. **(7 marks — 2 marks for comparison table, 3 marks for IDDFS trace, 2 marks for combined benefit justification)** --- ## 🔷 PART B Question B (14 Marks) > `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]` ### **Q-B.** **(a)** What are the **failure conditions of DFS**? Explain with an example why DFS is **not optimal** even in a finite state space. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Answer:** **Three Failure Conditions of DFS:** 1. **Failure by Depth Exhaustion** — Goal lies beyond the depth limit $L$; DFS returns FAILURE despite a solution existing. 2. **Failure by Infinite Descent** — In graphs with cycles and no visited-check, DFS loops forever exploring the same path. 3. **Failure by Wrong Branch Priority** — The first solution found is not the optimal one. **Counter-Example for Non-Optimality (Even in Finite State Space):** Consider this graph where each edge has a cost, and the goal is $G$: ```mermaid graph LR S((S)) -->|cost 100| A((A)) S -->|cost 1| B((B)) A -->|cost 1| G((G)) B -->|cost 100| G style S fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff style G fill:#7ED321,color:#fff ``` - DFS expands $S$, then pushes $A$ (or $B$) onto the stack first. - Suppose DFS picks the edge to $A$ (cost 100). The path $S \to A \to G$ has cost $101$. - The optimal path $S \to B \to G$ has cost $101$ as well, but if costs differ, DFS would still return a non-minimal path. - **Better counter-example:** Let $S \to A$ cost $100$ and $S \to B$ cost $1$, with $A \to G$ cost $1$ and $B \to G$ cost $100$. The optimal is $S \to A \to G$ (cost $101$), but DFS returns $S \to B \to G$ (cost $101$ again) — for clarity, change $B \to G$ to cost $50$, giving $S \to B \to G = 51$ vs $S \to A \to G = 101$; DFS still returns the more expensive path $S \to A \to G = 101$. **Conclusion:** DFS makes its choice **based on node ordering, not edge cost**, so it cannot guarantee optimality. **(7 marks — 2 marks for failure conditions, 3 marks for counter-example, 2 marks for conclusion)** --- ### **Q-B.** **(b)** Trace **Depth First Search** on the following graph starting from node $S$. Show the **stack and explored set** at each step. Mark the goal $G$. **(7 marks)** **Graph:** $S \to A$, $S \to B$, $A \to C$, $A \to D$, $B \to E$, $B \to F$, $C \to G$ (goal), $D$ is a dead-end, $E$ is a dead-end, $F$ is a dead-end. **Model Trace Table:** | Step | Action | Stack (Top → Bottom) | Explored Set | Note | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | Initialize | $[S]$ | $\emptyset$ | Start | | 1 | Pop $S$, push $A, B$ | $[A, B]$ | $\{S\}$ | Push children | | 2 | Pop $A$, push $C, D$ | $[C, D, B]$ | $\{S, A\}$ | Go deeper from A | | 3 | Pop $C$, push $G$ | $[G, D, B]$ | $\{S, A, C\}$ | One child only | | 4 | Pop $G$ | $[D, B]$ | — | **GOAL FOUND!** | **Final Path Reconstructed via Parent Pointers:** $$ S \rightarrow A \rightarrow C \rightarrow G $$ **Path Cost (assuming unit edges):** $$ \text{Cost} = 3 $$ > **Note on Optimality:** This path has cost 3, but an alternative path $S \to B \to \dots$ might have had a shorter route to $G$ in a different graph. DFS's greediness for depth makes it **non-optimal** in general. **(7 marks — 5 marks for complete trace table with stack states, 2 marks for path reconstruction)** --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in DFS Questions:** > > 1. **❌ Confusing DFS with BFS** — Students often draw the BFS expansion order (level by level) instead of DFS (deepest first). The frontier data structure is the giveaway: stack = DFS, queue = BFS. > > 2. **❌ Writing $O(b^d)$ for space complexity** — This is the BFS space. DFS space is $O(bm)$, NOT $O(b^d)$. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this slip. > > 3. **❌ Forgetting the Explored Set** — In the algorithm trace, students often re-expand already-visited nodes. Always maintain `Explored` and check before pushing. > > 4. **❌ Claiming DFS is Complete in Infinite Graphs** — Wrong. DFS is **NOT complete** in infinite state spaces because it can descend forever. Use **Iterative Deepening DFS** for completeness. > > 5. **❌ Confusing Completeness with Optimality** — These are **two independent properties**. DFS is not complete (in infinite spaces) AND not optimal. Memorize them as separate. > > 6. **❌ Not Drawing the Stack State** — When the question says "trace DFS," always **show the stack contents at every step**. This alone earns 2 bonus marks. --- ## 🔷 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet the night before the KTU exam.** - ✅ DFS = **Uninformed Search** using a **Stack (LIFO)** frontier. - ✅ Recursion is a **natural** alternative to an explicit stack. - ✅ **Time Complexity** = $O(b^m)$ where $b$ = branching factor, $m$ = max depth. - ✅ **Space Complexity** = $O(bm)$ — much better than BFS's $O(b^d)$. - ✅ **Complete** in finite graphs (with visited-check) **but not in infinite graphs**. - ✅ **NOT optimal** — returns the first solution found, not the cheapest. - ✅ **Backtracking** is the core mechanism: hit dead-end → pop → try sibling. - ✅ **Tree Search DFS** may loop forever; **Graph Search DFS** uses an explored set to prevent this. - ✅ **Depth Limited Search (DLS)** adds a depth bound $L$ to prevent infinite descent. - ✅ **Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)** runs DLS with $L = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ until goal found — combines BFS optimality with DFS memory efficiency. - ✅ IDDFS time = $O(b^d)$ (same as BFS), space = $O(bd)$ (same as DFS). - ✅ Use DFS when **memory is the bottleneck** and **any solution** (not necessarily optimal) is acceptable. - ✅ Avoid DFS when the state space has **deep, infinite branches** (e.g., game trees) without a depth bound. - ✅ DFS is the **foundation** for backtracking algorithms, topological sort, and cycle detection in graphs. - ✅ In KTU exams: **always draw the stack states** during traces; **always state completeness and optimality** explicitly. --- > 🎯 **Final KTU Exam Tip:** If a question says "trace DFS and find the path," your answer should contain: (1) the initial state, (2) the **stack at each step**, (3) the **explored set at each step**, (4) the **parent pointers**, and (5) the **final reconstructed path**. A complete answer with all five components scores full marks.

topics.title: Breadth First Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Breadth First Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Breadth First Search (BFS)** is an uninformed, graph-traversal algorithm that systematically explores a graph by visiting all vertices at the present **depth level** before proceeding to vertices at the next depth level. It employs a **First-In-First-Out (FIFO)** queue data structure to manage the frontier of undiscovered nodes and guarantees discovery of the **shortest path** (in terms of number of edges) in an unweighted graph.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, BFS belongs to the family of **systematic search strategies** within the broader domain of *uninformed (blind) search algorithms*. It is the canonical example of a search strategy that expands nodes in the order of their **path cost from the root**, which in the unweighted case is equivalent to expanding in order of their **depth** in the search tree.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary):**
> - **State Space:** The set of all possible configurations reachable from the initial state.
> - **Frontier / Open List:** The set of nodes generated but not yet expanded.
> - **Closed List / Explored Set:** The set of nodes already expanded.
> - **Node Expansion:** Generating all successor states (children) of a given node.
> - **Search Tree:** The tree of nodes reachable from the initial state via the application of operators.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you drop a **pebble into a still pond** of water. The ripples spread outward in **concentric circles**. At time $t=0$, only the center is disturbed. At $t=1$, the immediate neighbors are touched. At $t=2$, the next ring of water particles feels the disturbance, and so on. The water never "skips" a ring; it fills each layer completely before moving outward.

**Breadth First Search behaves identically on a graph:**
- The pebble = the **start (root) node**.
- Each ripple ring = a **depth level** in the search tree.
- The water = the **queue** of nodes waiting to be visited.

> [!TIP]
> Think of BFS as a "**level-order**" exploration. If you are standing in a maze, BFS would explore every corridor and room on your **current floor** before you ever consider taking a **staircase** to the next floor. The **queue** is essentially a "to-do list" where items are processed in the exact order they were added.

### 1.3 Why BFS Matters in Artificial Intelligence

BFS is **not merely an academic exercise**. In the KTU PCITT602 (Advanced AI) syllabus, BFS serves as the conceptual foundation for understanding:
1. **Shortest-path problems** in unweighted state spaces (e.g., minimum-move puzzles).
2. **Bi-directional search** strategies.
3. **Garbage collection algorithms** in programming language runtimes.
4. **Social networking algorithms** (e.g., finding people within $k$ connections).
5. **Web crawlers** that index pages layer by layer from a seed URL.

Standard performance metrics for BFS in an unweighted graph with $V$ vertices and $E$ edges are:
- **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$
- **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(V)$
- **Optimality:** **Yes** (for unweighted graphs, finds the shortest path).
- **Completeness:** **Yes** (will find a solution if one exists, provided the branching factor is finite).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — The "Why" and "How"

The BFS algorithm maintains three core data structures:
1. A **FIFO Queue** (the *frontier*).
2. A **Visited / Closed Set** (to prevent re-processing).
3. A **Parent Map** (to reconstruct the path from goal back to start).

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Initialization:**
  - *Why:* We must establish a known starting point and an empty frontier.
  - *How:* Mark the **initial state** $s_0$ as visited, set $\text{parent}(s_0) = \text{NULL}$, and enqueue $s_0$ into the queue $Q$.

- **Step 2 — Frontier Test (Termination Check):**
  - *Why:* If there are no nodes left to explore, the search is exhausted and has either found the goal or determined that no solution exists.
  - *How:* If $Q = \emptyset$ (queue is empty), then **return FAILURE**.

- **Step 3 — Dequeue (Selection):**
  - *Why:* FIFO ordering is the heart of BFS. We always process the *oldest* unexpanded node.
  - *How:* Pop the front element $u$ from $Q$.

- **Step 4 — Goal Test:**
  - *Why:* Test as early as possible to avoid unnecessary expansions.
  - *How:* If $u$ is the **goal state**, then **reconstruct and return the path** by backtracking through the parent pointers.

- **Step 5 — Expansion (Successor Generation):**
  - *Why:* This is the *generative* step where the algorithm actually "moves" through the state space.
  - *How:* For every successor $v$ of $u$ (i.e., for every edge $(u,v)$ in the graph), if $v$ is **not in the Visited set**:
    - Mark $v$ as visited.
    - Set $\text{parent}(v) = u$.
    - Enqueue $v$ into $Q$.

- **Step 6 — Loop:**
  - *Why:* Search is iterative until either a goal is reached or the frontier is exhausted.
  - *How:* Go back to **Step 2**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains the **high-yield formulas and metrics** that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce verbatim in Part A and Part B answers.

| **Metric / Property** | **Formula / Value** | **Explanation** |
|---|---|---|
| Time Complexity (Adjacency List) | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Each vertex and edge processed exactly once. |
| Time Complexity (Adjacency Matrix) | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^{2})$ | Must scan entire row of length $\vert V \vert$ for each node. |
| Space Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert)$ | Queue, visited array, and parent map all bounded by $\vert V \vert$. |
| Optimal Path Cost Bound | $C^{*} \le C^{\text{BFS}}$ | BFS returns optimal cost $C^{*}$ if the cost is uniform. |
| Maximum Queue Size (Tree Search) | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of shallowest goal. |
| Completeness | Guaranteed (finite $b$) | Will find a solution if one exists. |
| Optimality (Unweighted) | Guaranteed | Always finds the minimum-edges path. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notational Convention Used:** $\vert V \vert$ denotes the cardinality (count) of the vertex set, and $\vert E \vert$ denotes the cardinality of the edge set. This is the standard ISO/KTU mathematical notation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Use Case of BFS** |
|---|---|
| **Network Routing (BGP / OSPF)** | Shortest-path computation in communication networks. |
| **GPS Navigation Systems** | Finding minimum number of road segments between two intersections. |
| **Social Networks (LinkedIn, Facebook)** | Computing "degrees of separation" (e.g., 6-handshakes problem). |
| **Garbage Collection (Cheney Algorithm)** | Copying live objects layer-by-layer in managed runtimes. |
| **Web Crawlers (Googlebot)** | Indexing web pages by ring distance from seed URLs. |
| **Puzzle Solving (Rubik's Cube, 8-Puzzle)** | Finding minimum-move solutions in unweighted state spaces. |
| **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) BitTorrent** | Locating nearby peers within a logical hop distance. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Time Complexity

Let $G = (V, E)$ be an unweighted, undirected graph.

**Total Work Performed by BFS:**

$$
W_{\text{total}} = W_{\text{node processing}} + W_{\text{edge examination}}
$$

**Step 1 — Node Processing Cost:**
Every vertex $v \in V$ is enqueued and dequeued **at most once**. Each enqueue/dequeue operation is a constant-time $\mathcal{O}(1)$ operation on a standard queue.

$$
W_{\text{node processing}} = \sum_{v \in V} \mathcal{O}(1) = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert)
$$

**Step 2 — Edge Examination Cost:**
For each vertex $u$ that is dequeued, BFS examines **every outgoing edge** $(u, v)$ to check whether $v$ has been visited. In an undirected graph, this means each edge $(u, v)$ is examined **exactly twice** (once from $u$'s perspective, once from $v$'s perspective).

$$
W_{\text{edge examination}} = \sum_{(u,v) \in E} 2 \cdot \mathcal{O}(1) = \mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert)
$$

**Step 3 — Total Work:**
$$
W_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert) + \mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert) = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Derived Time Complexity (Adjacency List Representation):** $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
> This is the **board-exam expected answer**.

### 3.2 Worked Example — BFS Trace on a Concrete Graph

**Problem Setup:** Consider the following undirected graph with $\vert V \vert = 7$ and $\vert E \vert = 8$:

$$
V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F, G\}
$$
$$
E = \{(A,B), (A,C), (A,D), (B,E), (C,F), (D,F), (E,G), (F,G)\}
$$

**Source Node:** $A$   |   **Goal Node:** $G$

**Step-by-Step Trace (Exam-Ready Format):**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners expect a **tabular trace**. Use the following format in your exam answer. The "Visited" set is shown as a boolean array, and the "Queue" is shown as a left-to-right list (front on the left).

| **Step** | **Action** | **Queue (front → rear)** | **Visited Set** | **Parent Map** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initialize: enqueue $A$ | $[A]$ | $\{A\}$ | $\text{parent}(A) = \text{NULL}$ |
| 1 | Dequeue $A$, expand → $B, C, D$ | $[B, C, D]$ | $\{A, B, C, D\}$ | $B, C, D \leftarrow A$ |
| 2 | Dequeue $B$, expand → $E$ (since $A$ already visited) | $[C, D, E]$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ | $E \leftarrow B$ |
| 3 | Dequeue $C$, expand → $F$ (since $A$ already visited) | $[D, E, F]$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ | $F \leftarrow C$ |
| 4 | Dequeue $D$, expand → $F$ already visited, skip | $[E, F]$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ | — |
| 5 | Dequeue $E$, expand → $G$ **GOAL FOUND** | $[F, G]$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E, F, G\}$ | $G \leftarrow E$ |
| 6 | Stop & reconstruct path | $[F, G]$ | — | $G \rightarrow E \rightarrow B \rightarrow A$ |

**Reconstructed Shortest Path (Goal-Test-on-Generation variant would stop at step 5 expansion):**

$$
A \rightarrow B \rightarrow E \rightarrow G
$$

**Path Verification:**
$$
\text{Path Length (edges)} = 3
$$
$$
\text{Optimal? } \textbf{Yes} \text{ — there is no path from } A \text{ to } G \text{ with fewer than 3 edges.}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tree Search vs. Graph Search

This is a **favourite KTU question**: *Why is BFS on a graph (using a visited set) different from BFS on a tree?*

| **Property** | **Tree Search (No Visited Set)** | **Graph Search (With Visited Set)** |
|---|---|---|
| Redundant Paths Explored | Yes — every path explored | No — first visit is final |
| Space Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert)$ |
| Time Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |
| Optimality (Uniform Cost) | **Not always** | **Yes** |
| Use Case | Infinite / huge state spaces | Known finite graphs |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Many students wrongly state that BFS is $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ for graphs. The correct answer depends on whether the algorithm is doing a **tree search** or a **graph search**. With a visited set, the bound becomes $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.

### 3.4 Queue State Algebra (Abstract)

Let $Q_t$ denote the queue state at iteration $t$. The BFS dynamics can be described by the recurrence:

$$
Q_{t+1} = \left(Q_t \setminus \{u_t\}\right) \cup \left\{v \in \text{Successors}(u_t) \mid v \notin \text{Visited}_{t+1}\right\}
$$

where $u_t$ is the dequeued node at step $t$, and $\text{Visited}_{t+1}$ is the updated visited set.

$$
\text{Visited}_{t+1} = \text{Visited}_t \cup \{u_t\} \cup \{\text{newly enqueued successors}\}
$$

The algorithm terminates when:
$$
Q_t = \emptyset \quad \text{(exhausted)} \quad \lor \quad u_t = \text{goal} \quad \text{(success)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid BFS Traversal Diagram (Block-Level Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows the **logical flow of the BFS algorithm** itself, including the decision points (goal test, visited test) that KTU examiners love to test in Part B questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START]) --> nodeB[Initialize visited set as empty]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Enqueue source node S into queue Q]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Is queue Q empty?}
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeE([RETURN FAILURE])
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeF[Dequeue front node U from Q]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Is U the GOAL node?}
    nodeG -- YES --> nodeH[Reconstruct path via parent map]
    nodeH --> nodeI([RETURN SUCCESS and path])
    nodeG -- NO --> nodeJ[For each successor V of U do]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{Is V already visited?}
    nodeK -- NO --> nodeL[Mark V as visited]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Set parent of V as U]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Enqueue V into Q]
    nodeK -- YES --> nodeO[Skip V and continue]
    nodeN --> nodeP{More successors?}
    nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeP -- YES --> nodeJ
    nodeP -- NO --> nodeD
```

### 4.2 Mermaid BFS Layered Tree Expansion (Worked Example)

> [!TIP]
> The diagram below is a **schematic of BFS on the example graph from Section 3.2**, showing how the search tree expands in **concentric depth rings** (Level 0, Level 1, Level 2, Level 3). This is exactly the kind of figure KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0A["LEVEL 0: ROOT A"] --> L1B["LEVEL 1: B"]
    L0A --> L1C["LEVEL 1: C"]
    L0A --> L1D["LEVEL 1: D"]
    L1B --> L2E["LEVEL 2: E"]
    L1C --> L2F["LEVEL 2: F"]
    L1D --> L2F2["LEVEL 2: F already visited SKIP"]
    L2E --> L3G["LEVEL 3: G GOAL"]
    L2F --> L3G2["LEVEL 3: G already in tree"]
```

### 4.3 Architecture-Level Block Diagram (BFS as a Module)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram below shows BFS as a **functional block** interacting with its core data structures. Use this in viva-voce or design-pattern questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BFSEngine["BFS ALGORITHM CORE"]
        engineA[Node Selector FIFO]
        engineB[Goal Tester]
        engineC[Successor Generator]
    end
    subgraph DataStructs["DATA STRUCTURES"]
        dsA[Queue Q Frontier]
        dsB[Visited Set Closed]
        dsC[Parent Map backpointers]
    end
    Input[Input Graph G V E and source S] --> engineA
    engineA --> dsA
    engineA --> engineB
    engineB -- FOUND --> Output[Output Shortest Path P]
    engineB -- NOT FOUND --> engineC
    engineC --> dsB
    engineC --> dsC
    engineC -- push new nodes --> dsA
    engineA -- next iteration --> engineA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
> **Define Breadth First Search. Why is it classified as an "uninformed" search strategy?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**BFS Definition (2 marks):** Breadth First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores all nodes at the present depth level of the search tree before moving on to the nodes at the next depth level. It uses a **FIFO queue** to maintain the frontier and guarantees optimality (shortest path) in unweighted graphs.
**Uninformed Justification (1 mark):** BFS is called *uninformed* (or *blind*) because it has **no domain-specific heuristic** to guide its exploration. It treats every node uniformly and expands them in strict FIFO order without any estimate of how close a node is to the goal.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Frequently Asked Variant]**
> **List any three properties of BFS. State its time and space complexity in Big-O notation for a graph with $V$ vertices and $E$ edges.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Three Properties (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Complete:** It will find a solution if one exists (with finite branching).
2. **Optimal:** For unweighted graphs, it returns the shortest path.
3. **Systematic:** Uses FIFO queue; never revisits already-visited nodes.

**Complexities (1.5 marks):**
- **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$
- **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert)$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU 2024 Choice Option 1**
> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the BFS algorithm with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of the FIFO queue and the visited set in ensuring optimality and avoiding redundant work. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Apply BFS on the graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ and $E = \{(1,2), (1,3), (2,4), (2,5), (3,5), (3,6), (4,5), (5,6)\}$. Start from node 1 and find the shortest path to node 6. Show the full state of the queue, visited set, and parent map at every step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Algorithm Statement (2 marks):** BFS begins at the source node $s$, marks it as visited, and enqueues it. It then repeatedly dequeues the front node $u$, tests it for the goal, and enqueues all *unvisited* successors of $u$.
2. **Role of FIFO Queue (2.5 marks):** The FIFO queue is the *mechanism* that enforces level-by-level exploration. Because elements are processed in the exact order they are inserted, nodes at depth $d$ are **all dequeued before** any node at depth $d+1$. This is what makes BFS *systematic* and *optimal* for unweighted graphs.
3. **Role of Visited Set (2.5 marks):** The visited set prevents the algorithm from re-enqueuing a node that has already been reached via a shorter (or equal) path. Without it, BFS would behave as a *tree search* and could explore exponentially many redundant paths in cyclic graphs, blowing up the space complexity to $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$.

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Trace Table (carry this exact table into your answer sheet):**

| **Step** | **Dequeued** | **Queue (front → rear)** | **Visited** | **Parent Updates** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | $[1]$ | $\{1\}$ | $\text{parent}(1) = \text{NULL}$ |
| 1 | $1$ | $[2, 3]$ | $\{1, 2, 3\}$ | $\text{parent}(2) = 1, \text{parent}(3) = 1$ |
| 2 | $2$ | $[3, 4, 5]$ | $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ | $\text{parent}(4) = 2, \text{parent}(5) = 2$ |
| 3 | $3$ | $[4, 5, 6]$ | $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ | $\text{parent}(6) = 3$ **GOAL** |
| 4 | Stop. | $[4, 5, 6]$ | — | — |

**Shortest Path Reconstruction (3 marks for backtracking):**

$$
6 \xrightarrow{\text{parent}} 3 \xrightarrow{\text{parent}} 1
$$

**Final Answer:** The shortest path from node $1$ to node $6$ is:

$$
1 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 6
$$

**Path Length:** 2 edges.
**Verification:** There is no edge $(1, 6)$ in $E$, so the minimum is indeed 2.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — KTU 2024 Choice Option 2**
> **[KTU University Exam – Modified Pattern, Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Compare BFS and DFS (Depth First Search) across the following dimensions: data structure used, completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A robot is in a 3×3 grid maze. The robot can move **up, down, left, or right** into adjacent open cells. The start cell is the **top-left corner** $(0,0)$ and the goal is the **bottom-right corner** $(2,2)$. Using BFS, find the shortest path from start to goal. Represent the grid as a graph. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

| **Dimension** | **BFS** | **DFS** |
|---|---|---|
| Data Structure | **FIFO Queue** (2 marks partial) | **LIFO Stack** (2 marks partial) |
| Completeness | Yes (finite branching) | No (can get stuck in infinite branch) |
| Optimality | **Yes** for unweighted graphs | **No** (finds *a* path, not *shortest*) |
| Time Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |
| Space Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ where $d$ is tree depth (often much smaller) |

*(Full marks: 1.4 marks per row × 5 rows = 7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Graph Modeling (2 marks):** Each of the 9 cells is a node. Two nodes are connected by an edge if the robot can move directly between them. The start is $(0,0)$ and the goal is $(2,2)$. The cell coordinates form the node labels.

**Step 2 — Edgelist:**
$$
E = \{((0,0),(0,1)), ((0,0),(1,0)), \ldots, ((1,2),(2,2))\}
$$

**Step 3 — BFS Trace (3 marks):**
BFS explores by Manhattan distance from $(0,0)$:
- **Level 0 (distance 0):** $\{(0,0)\}$
- **Level 1 (distance 1):** $\{(0,1), (1,0)\}$
- **Level 2 (distance 2):** $\{(0,2), (1,1), (2,0)\}$
- **Level 3 (distance 3):** $\{(1,2), (2,1)\}$
- **Level 4 (distance 4):** $\{(2,2)\}$ **GOAL**

**Step 4 — Shortest Path Reconstruction (2 marks):**

$$
(0,0) \rightarrow (1,0) \rightarrow (2,0) \rightarrow (2,1) \rightarrow (2,2)
$$

**Path Length:** 4 moves.
**Conclusion:** BFS guarantees this is the **minimum-move** solution because it explores by increasing Manhattan distance.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on BFS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Parent Map (–2 marks):** Many students find the goal node but cannot reconstruct the path because they never maintained a `parent` pointer. KTU examiners deduct heavily for this — *path reconstruction is the proof of optimality*.
>
> 2. **Confusing Tree Search with Graph Search (–2 marks):** Stating the time complexity as $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ when the algorithm uses a visited set. The correct answer is $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ for graph search.
>
> 3. **Goal Test Placement (–1 mark):** Failing to specify *when* the goal test is performed. KTU accepts both variants, but you **must state explicitly** whether you are testing on *generation* or on *expansion*. Pick one and stick to it.
>
> 4. **Queue Direction (–1 mark):** Drawing the queue with the rear on the left. Examiners follow the standard convention: **front of the queue is on the LEFT**.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Trivial Cases (–1 mark):** Not mentioning the *failure case* ($Q = \emptyset$) or the *start-is-goal* case. A complete algorithm must handle both.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your revision wall!):**
>
> - **BFS** = **uninformed + level-order + FIFO queue + shortest-path-on-unweighted-graphs**.
> - Data structure = **Queue (FIFO)**. Not a stack. Not a priority queue. **Queue.**
> - Time complexity (graph search) = $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
> - Time complexity (tree search) = $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ — *avoid in KTU answers unless the question explicitly says "tree search".*
> - Space complexity = $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert)$ for graph search, $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ for tree search.
> - **Complete?** **Yes** (for finite branching factor).
> - **Optimal?** **Yes** (for uniform step costs, i.e., unweighted graphs).
> - The **visited set** is what differentiates graph search from tree search — *always mention it in your algorithm*.
> - The **parent map** is what allows you to reconstruct the path — *never skip writing it in the trace table*.
> - Adjacency list $\Rightarrow$ $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. Adjacency matrix $\Rightarrow$ $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^{2})$. Know both.
> - BFS is the **optimal choice** when: the graph is unweighted, the branching factor is small, and the solution is expected to be near the root.
> - BFS is a **poor choice** when: the state space is huge and the goal is deep (memory blows up), or when all you need is *any* solution (use DFS or IDS instead).
> - The "ripple in a pond" analogy is the **single most effective way** to explain BFS in a viva — practice it.
> - The "level-order" property is the **single most testable fact** in KTU Part A — memorize it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Depth Limited Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Depth Limited Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Depth-Limited Search (DLS)** is a state-space uninformed (blind) search strategy that operates as a refined variant of the Depth-First Search (DFS) algorithm by imposing a predetermined **predefined depth limit** $\ell$ (denoted by the Greek letter *ell* or sometimes $L$) on the search tree. The algorithm explores nodes in a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) manner, but any node whose depth exceeds the limit $\ell$ is treated as having no successors, effectively pruning all deeper branches.

Formally, if the depth of a node $n$ in the search tree is denoted as $d(n)$, then DLS treats node $n$ as a leaf node if and only if:

$$d(n) > \ell$$

This single conditional boundary transforms an unbounded DFS (which may fail to terminate in infinite or cyclic state spaces) into a guaranteed-terminating search procedure, at the cost of sacrificing completeness in certain graph topologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Depth-Limited Search is positioned in **Module 2** as a foundational building block for the more sophisticated **Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search (IDDFS)** algorithm. Mastery of DLS is mandatory before progressing to IDDFS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction from DFS:** The fundamental difference is the **termination guarantee**. Pure DFS may explore infinitely deep paths in graphs containing cycles or unbounded depth, whereas DLS provides absolute upper-bound termination after exploring at most $b^{\ell}$ nodes (where $b$ is the branching factor).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a **treasure hunter exploring a vast, dark, multi-story building** looking for a specific gem. The building has an unknown (possibly infinite) number of floors:

- **Pure DFS (no limit):** You start at the top floor and keep taking staircases downward, exploring each hallway to its absolute end before backtracking. If the building has an endless basement, you could theoretically descend forever and never find the gem — or worse, you might get lost in a never-ending loop.

- **Depth-Limited Search:** Before entering the building, you tell yourself: *"I will only descend a maximum of 10 floors below my starting point."* After descending 10 floors, you stop going deeper and backtrack, no matter what you find. You are guaranteed to eventually check the 10th floor of *every possible hallway* because you systematically backtrack.

**Real-world parallel:** DLS behaves like a **robot vacuum cleaner with a battery limit**. The robot performs a deep, systematic pattern but stops at a certain "energy budget" and returns to its dock. The "battery level" here is the depth limit $\ell$.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Symbol Convention

The following notational conventions are standardized across all KTU 2024 Scheme AI examinations:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $b$ | Branching factor (average/maximum successors per node) |
| $\ell$ | Depth limit (user-defined non-negative integer) |
| $d$ | Depth of the shallowest goal node |
| $C^*$ | Cost of optimal solution path |
| $d(n)$ | Depth of node $n$ from root |

> [!NOTE]
> All depth values are measured as **non-negative integers** with the root node at depth **0**. The branching factor $b$ is assumed to be a **finite positive integer** for analytical purposes. Bold values like $b$ and $\ell$ represent the two primary control parameters of the DLS algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The DLS algorithm follows a strict procedural sequence. Each step is logically necessary for the algorithm's correctness.

**Step 1 — Initialization**
- Push the root node $n_0$ onto a stack $S$ (or invoke a recursive function with $n_0$ as argument).
- Record the current depth of $n_0$ as $d(n_0) = 0$.

**Step 2 — Stack/Recursion Inspection**
- If $S$ is empty, return **FAILURE** (entire search space within limit has been exhausted).
- Otherwise, pop the top node $n$ from $S$.

**Step 3 — Goal Test (Early Termination)**
- If $n$ is a goal node, return **SUCCESS** with the solution path.
- This step is critical: it allows DLS to return as soon as a goal is found, even if other shallow goals exist elsewhere.

**Step 4 — Depth Boundary Check**
- If $d(n) \geq \ell$, then **do not expand** $n$. Treat $n$ as a leaf and return to Step 2.

**Step 5 — Successor Generation**
- If $d(n) < \ell$, generate all successors of $n$.
- Push each successor $n'$ onto the stack with $d(n') = d(n) + 1$.
- Go to Step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The depth check is **inclusive of the limit**: at depth exactly $\ell$, the node may be inspected (goal test) but is *not* expanded. This is a common exam pitfall — students often incorrectly assume the limit node itself cannot be tested.

### 2.2 Algorithmic Pseudocode (Algorithm-Form Representation)

The following is a **language-agnostic algorithmic representation** suitable for KTU theory examinations:

```
FUNCTION DLS(node n, integer limit):
    RETURN Recursive-DLS(n, limit, 0)

FUNCTION Recursive-DLS(node n, integer limit, integer depth):
    IF Goal-Test(n) THEN
        RETURN (SUCCESS, path-to-n)
    END IF
    
    IF depth >= limit THEN
        RETURN (CUTOFF, empty-path)
    END IF
    
    FOR EACH successor s OF n DO
        (result, path) <- Recursive-DLS(s, limit, depth + 1)
        IF result = SUCCESS THEN
            RETURN (SUCCESS, prepend(n, path))
        END IF
    END FOR
    
    RETURN (FAILURE, empty-path)
```

The three possible return states are:
- **SUCCESS** — Goal node was located.
- **CUTOFF** — Depth limit was reached at some branch without finding a goal.
- **FAILURE** — Entire reachable space within limit was exhausted.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The table below consolidates all KTU-relevant quantitative metrics for DLS:

| Property | Formula / Value | Engineering Interpretation |
|----------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| Time Complexity (worst case) | $O(b^{\ell})$ | Worst case explores all nodes up to depth $\ell$ |
| Space Complexity | $O(b \cdot \ell)$ | Only one path + sibling stack held in memory |
| Completeness | **Yes, if** $\ell \geq d$ | Complete only if limit is at least goal depth |
| Optimality | **No** (in general) | Not guaranteed to find shallowest goal |
| Optimality (uniform-cost actions) | **Yes, if** $\ell = d$ | Finds optimal only when limit matches goal depth |
| Termination Guarantee | **Yes** | Guaranteed to halt after $b^{\ell}$ expansions |
| Maximum Nodes Expanded | $1 + b + b^2 + \ldots + b^{\ell}$ | Geometric series sum |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical exam insight:** When writing the time complexity, students frequently write $O(b^d)$ instead of $O(b^{\ell})$. The correct form uses the **depth limit $\ell$**, not the goal depth $d$, because the algorithm's resource consumption is bounded by the *user-defined* limit, not the *actual* goal location.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

Depth-Limited Search is deployed across multiple engineering domains where bounded exploration is critical:

1. **Robotics & Autonomous Navigation:** Mobile robots with limited computational resources (e.g., warehouse AGVs) use DLS for path planning in grids with bounded horizon lookahead.
2. **Network Routing Protocols:** Certain constrained routing algorithms limit search depth to prevent route-flap storms in dynamic topologies.
3. **Game AI Engines:** Chess engines use depth-limited search (precursor to alpha-beta pruning) to evaluate positions within a fixed ply depth before time runs out.
4. **VLSI Design Verification:** Bounded model checking tools use depth-limited state exploration to verify hardware properties.
5. **Database Query Optimization:** Cost-based optimizers limit search-tree depth when evaluating join orders.

### 2.5 Relationship to Other Uninformed Searches

| Algorithm | Time | Space | Complete | Optimal |
|-----------|------|-------|----------|---------|
| BFS | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | Yes | Yes (uniform cost) |
| Uniform-Cost | $O(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | Yes | Yes |
| DFS | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ | No | No |
| **DLS** | $O(b^{\ell})$ | $O(b\ell)$ | **Conditional** | **No** |
| IDDFS | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ | Yes | Yes (uniform cost) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** DLS is the **conceptual bridge** between DFS (unbounded) and IDDFS (iterative). Questions often ask students to identify which property of DFS is preserved and which is sacrificed — the answer is: **linear space is preserved, but completeness is sacrificed** unless $\ell \geq d$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Trace of DLS on a Sample Search Tree

Consider the following search tree with branching factor $b = 3$ and depth limit $\ell = 2$. Goal node is **G**. Node labels follow BFS-level ordering, while expansion follows DLS (LIFO) order.

**Initial Tree Structure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level 0:} \quad & A \\
\text{Level 1:} \quad & B, C, D \\
\text{Level 2:} \quad & E, F, G, H, I, J, K, L, M \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume goal $G$ exists as a child of node $C$ at depth 2.

**Step-by-Step DLS Execution (LIFO expansion order):**

| Step | Action | Stack State (top → bottom) | Depth Check | Goal Found? |
|------|--------|----------------------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Push root $A$ | $[A]$ | $d(A)=0 < 2$ ✓ | No |
| 2 | Pop $A$, expand → push $D, C, B$ | $[B, C, D]$ | — | — |
| 3 | Pop $B$ (depth 1) | $[C, D]$ | $1 < 2$ ✓ | No |
| 4 | Pop $B$'s children → push $E, F, G$ | $[G, F, E, C, D]$ | — | — |
| 5 | Pop $G$ (depth 2) | $[F, E, C, D]$ | $2 \geq 2$ ✗ | **No cutoff, but checked** |
| 6 | Pop $F$ (depth 2) | $[E, C, D]$ | $2 \geq 2$ ✗ | No |
| 7 | Pop $E$ (depth 2) | $[C, D]$ | $2 \geq 2$ ✗ | No |
| 8 | Pop $C$ (depth 1) | $[D]$ | $1 < 2$ ✓ | No |
| 9 | Expand $C$ → push $I, H, G'$ | $[G', H, I, D]$ | — | — |
| 10 | Pop $G'$ (depth 2) | $[H, I, D]$ | $2 \geq 2$ ✗ | **YES — Goal Found!** |

**Return path:** $A \rightarrow C \rightarrow G'$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** DLS found the goal at step 10, but it was *not* the first goal in the tree. Pure BFS would have found the leftmost goal (G) at step 5. This empirically demonstrates that DLS is **not optimal** in terms of path length or expansion order.

### 3.2 Derivation of Time Complexity Bound

We derive the worst-case time complexity $O(b^{\ell})$ rigorously.

**Premise:** In the worst case, the algorithm must expand every reachable node whose depth is at most $\ell$.

**Counting nodes at each depth level:**
- At depth $0$: $1$ node (the root) → contributes $b^0 = 1$
- At depth $1$: $b$ nodes → contributes $b^1$
- At depth $2$: $b^2$ nodes → contributes $b^2$
- At depth $k$: $b^k$ nodes
- At depth $\ell$: $b^{\ell}$ nodes (cutoff boundary, may or may not be expanded)

**Total nodes expanded (worst case):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{k=0}^{\ell} b^k \\
&= b^0 + b^1 + b^2 + \ldots + b^{\ell}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Applying the finite geometric series formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= \frac{b^{\ell+1} - 1}{b - 1} \quad \text{for } b \neq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Asymptotic bound:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &\leq \frac{b^{\ell+1}}{b - 1} \\
&= \frac{b}{b-1} \cdot b^{\ell} \\
&= O(b^{\ell})
\end{aligned}
$$

The constant factor $\frac{b}{b-1}$ is absorbed into the Big-O notation, confirming the time complexity is $O(b^{\ell})$.

### 3.3 Derivation of Space Complexity

Unlike BFS, DLS does **not** store the entire frontier. It uses a stack corresponding to the current path plus the siblings of nodes on the current path.

**Space accounting:**
- Path length from root to deepest node explored: at most $\ell$ nodes
- Each node on the path has at most $b - 1$ siblings awaiting exploration
- Maximum siblings stored at any depth: $b - 1$

**Total space:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{DLS}} &= \ell \cdot b \\
&= O(b \cdot \ell)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **defining advantage** of DLS over BFS — **linear space usage** with respect to the path length, which is critical for memory-constrained systems.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Completeness and Optimality Conditions

We construct a truth table for completeness and optimality based on parameter values:

| Condition | Complete? | Optimal? | Explanation |
|-----------|-----------|----------|-------------|
| $\ell < d$ (limit too shallow) | **No** | No | Goal is pruned by cutoff; algorithm returns FAILURE |
| $\ell = d$ (exact match) | **Yes** | **Yes** (uniform cost) | Goal is reachable; shallowest found |
| $\ell > d$ (limit too deep) | **Yes** | **No** | Goal is found, but DLS may return a deeper goal first due to LIFO order |
| $\ell = \infty$ (no limit) | **No** (cyclic) | No | Reduces to DFS; may loop forever in cyclic graphs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Pattern:** A frequently asked 7-mark question is: *"Under what conditions is DLS both complete and optimal?"* The correct answer is: **DLS is complete when $\ell \geq d$ and optimal only when $\ell = d$ with uniform step costs**. Partial credit is awarded for identifying both conditions independently.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A robot search problem has branching factor $b = 5$ and the goal is known to exist at depth $d = 6$. A DLS is run with limit $\ell = 4$.

**Step 1 — Determine completeness:**

$$
\ell = 4 < d = 6
$$

Since the depth limit is shallower than the goal depth, the goal will be **pruned** at the cutoff. The algorithm returns **CUTOFF** → **Incomplete**.

**Step 2 — Maximum nodes expanded in worst case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{max}} &= \sum_{k=0}^{4} 5^k \\
&= 1 + 5 + 25 + 125 + 625 \\
&= 781 \text{ nodes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Memory consumed (space):**

$$
S = b \cdot \ell = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ node-slots}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to BFS for same $d$:**

$$
N_{\text{BFS}} = \sum_{k=0}^{6} 5^k = 1 + 5 + 25 + \ldots + 15625 = 19531 \text{ nodes}
$$

**Conclusion:** DLS with $\ell = 4$ uses only **4%** of the time and a fraction of the memory of BFS, but **fails to find** the goal. This trade-off is the central engineering decision in deploying DLS.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node identifiers below are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node IDs. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text with no markdown formatting inside double quotes.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DLS

The following diagram illustrates the operational flow of the Depth-Limited Search algorithm as a closed-loop control system:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START - INITIALIZE STACK]
    nodeB[STACK EMPTY CHECK]
    nodeC[POP TOP NODE N FROM STACK]
    nodeD[GOAL TEST ON NODE N]
    nodeE[RETURN SUCCESS AND PATH]
    nodeF[DEPTH CHECK - IS DEPTH N GREATER THAN LIMIT]
    nodeG[TREAT N AS LEAF - DO NOT EXPAND]
    nodeH[GENERATE ALL SUCCESSORS OF N]
    nodeI[PUSH SUCCESSORS WITH DEPTH PLUS 1]
    nodeJ[RETURN FAILURE - ENTIRE SPACE EXHAUSTED]
    nodeK[RETURN CUTOFF - DEPTH LIMIT HIT]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -->|YES| nodeJ
    nodeB -->|NO| nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD -->|YES| nodeE
    nodeD -->|NO| nodeF
    nodeF -->|YES| nodeG
    nodeF -->|NO| nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeB
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeB
```

### 4.3 Search Tree Expansion Diagram with Depth Cutoff

The following diagram shows how DLS prunes an entire subtree when the depth limit is reached. Node `goalX` lies beyond the limit and is never explored:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot[ROOT NODE - DEPTH 0]
    nodeL1A[CHILD A - DEPTH 1]
    nodeL1B[CHILD B - DEPTH 1]
    nodeL1C[CHILD C - DEPTH 1]
    nodeL2A1[GRANDCHILD A1 - DEPTH 2 - LIMIT REACHED]
    nodeL2A2[GRANDCHILD A2 - DEPTH 2 - LIMIT REACHED]
    nodeL2B1[GRANDCHILD B1 - DEPTH 2 - LIMIT REACHED]
    nodeL2B2[GRANDCHILD B2 - DEPTH 2 - LIMIT REACHED]
    nodeL2C1[GRANDCHILD C1 - DEPTH 2 - LIMIT REACHED]
    nodeL2C2[GRANDCHILD C2 - DEPTH 2 - LIMIT REACHED]
    nodeCutA[PRUNED SUBTREE - DEPTH 3 AND BEYOND]
    nodeCutB[PRUNED SUBTREE - DEPTH 3 AND BEYOND]
    nodeCutC[PRUNED SUBTREE - DEPTH 3 AND BEYOND]
    nodeGoal[GOAL NODE - DEPTH 5 - NEVER REACHED]

    nodeRoot --> nodeL1A
    nodeRoot --> nodeL1B
    nodeRoot --> nodeL1C
    nodeL1A --> nodeL2A1
    nodeL1A --> nodeL2A2
    nodeL1B --> nodeL2B1
    nodeL1B --> nodeL2B2
    nodeL1C --> nodeL2C1
    nodeL1C --> nodeL2C2
    nodeL2A1 -.-> nodeCutA
    nodeL2B1 -.-> nodeCutB
    nodeL2C1 -.-> nodeCutC
    nodeCutA -.-> nodeGoal
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** Solid arrows represent explored edges; dotted arrows represent **cutoff edges** — branches that exist logically but are never traversed by DLS because their parent node is at the depth boundary. The goal node `nodeGoal` at depth 5 is **invisible** to the algorithm when $\ell = 2$.

### 4.4 Comparison Schematic: DFS vs DLS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDFS[DFS - NO LIMIT - RISK OF INFINITE LOOP]
    nodeDLS[DLS - DEPTH LIMIT L - TERMINATION GUARANTEED]
    nodeIDDFS[IDDFS - ITERATIVE LAYER OF DLS - COMPLETE PLUS OPTIMAL]

    nodeDFS -->|ADD LIMIT L| nodeDLS
    nodeDLS -->|WRAP IN LOOP L FROM 0 TO INFINITY| nodeIDDFS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This schematic visually captures the **pedagogical progression** taught in KTU Module 2: DLS is DFS plus a bound, and IDDFS is DLS plus an outer iteration. Mastery of DLS is therefore the **prerequisite** for understanding IDDFS.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define Depth-Limited Search. Under what condition on the depth limit $\ell$ is DLS guaranteed to be complete?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Depth-Limited Search (DLS) is a variant of Depth-First Search that restricts the exploration of any path to a maximum of $\ell$ levels below the root node. Any node whose depth exceeds the limit is treated as a leaf and is not expanded further.

**Completeness condition:** DLS is complete if and only if the depth limit $\ell$ is at least as large as the depth of the shallowest goal node, i.e., $\ell \geq d$, where $d$ is the depth of the nearest goal.

*(Mark allocation: 1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for completeness condition.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

State the time and space complexity of Depth-Limited Search. Explain why DLS uses less memory than Breadth-First Search.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Time complexity:** $O(b^{\ell})$, where $b$ is the branching factor and $\ell$ is the depth limit.
- **Space complexity:** $O(b \cdot \ell)$.

DLS uses less memory than BFS because BFS must store the **entire frontier** of nodes at all depths up to $d$ in a FIFO queue, requiring $O(b^d)$ memory. In contrast, DLS uses a LIFO stack that holds only the **current path** (length $\ell$) plus the **siblings** of nodes on that path, requiring only $O(b \cdot \ell)$ memory. This linear-space property is the primary advantage of DLS in memory-constrained applications.

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark each for time/space, 1 mark for the memory comparison explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Describe the Depth-Limited Search algorithm with a clear procedural flow. Explain each of the three possible return states — SUCCESS, FAILURE, and CUTOFF.

**(b)** [7 marks] Consider a search tree with branching factor $b = 4$. A Depth-Limited Search is run with limit $\ell = 5$. Compute (i) the maximum number of nodes that can be expanded in the worst case, and (ii) the maximum memory consumed by the algorithm. Compare your result with what BFS would require if the shallowest goal is at depth $d = 5$.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The Depth-Limited Search algorithm proceeds as follows:

**Step 1:** Initialize a stack with the root node. Set its depth to $0$.

**Step 2:** Check if the stack is empty. If yes, return **FAILURE** — this means the entire reachable state space within the depth limit has been exhausted without finding a goal.

**Step 3:** Pop the top node $n$ from the stack. Apply the goal test to $n$.

**Step 4:** If $n$ satisfies the goal test, return **SUCCESS** along with the path from the root to $n$.

**Step 5:** If $d(n) \geq \ell$, do not expand $n$. Discard $n$ and return to Step 2. This condition produces the **CUTOFF** state when a goal is eventually not found despite hitting the limit repeatedly.

**Step 6:** If $d(n) < \ell$, generate all successors of $n$. Push each successor onto the stack with depth $d(n) + 1$. Return to Step 2.

**Three return states explained:**

- **SUCCESS:** Returned when the goal test evaluates to TRUE on some node. The algorithm terminates with the solution path.
- **FAILURE:** Returned when the stack becomes empty. All nodes within the bounded search space have been examined; no goal exists at depth $\leq \ell$.
- **CUTOFF:** Returned (in recursive formulations) when a branch was terminated due to depth limit without confirming a goal. CUTOFF does not directly mean failure — the algorithm may still find a goal in another branch.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $b = 4$, $\ell = 5$, $d = 5$.

**(i) Maximum nodes expanded (worst case) for DLS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{DLS}} &= \sum_{k=0}^{\ell} b^k \\
&= \sum_{k=0}^{5} 4^k \\
&= 1 + 4 + 16 + 64 + 256 + 1024 \\
&= 1365 \text{ nodes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum memory consumed by DLS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{DLS}} &= b \cdot \ell \\
&= 4 \times 5 \\
&= 20 \text{ node-slots}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with BFS for $d = 5$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{BFS}} &= \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k = \sum_{k=0}^{5} 4^k = 1365 \text{ nodes}
\end{aligned}
$$

However, BFS must also store the entire frontier queue, requiring:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{BFS}} &= b^d = 4^5 = 1024 \text{ node-slots}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison table:**

| Metric | DLS ($\ell=5$) | BFS ($d=5$) | Ratio |
|--------|----------------|-------------|-------|
| Time (nodes expanded) | 1365 | 1365 | 1:1 |
| Space (max frontier) | 20 | 1024 | **1 : 51.2** |

**Conclusion:** DLS achieves **identical time performance** to BFS in this case (because $\ell = d$), but uses **~51 times less memory**. This demonstrates the practical superiority of DLS in memory-constrained environments when the goal depth is approximately known.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Compare Depth-Limited Search with pure Depth-First Search. Construct a comparison matrix covering: termination behavior, completeness, space complexity, and applicability to infinite state spaces.

**(b)** [7 marks] Explain with a worked example why DLS is not optimal. Use a tree where two goal nodes exist at different depths and show that DLS may return the deeper goal first.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Comparison Matrix: DFS vs DLS**

| Property | Pure DFS | Depth-Limited Search (DLS) |
|----------|----------|----------------------------|
| Termination | **Not guaranteed** — may loop forever in cyclic or infinite graphs | **Guaranteed** — halts after at most $b^{\ell}$ node expansions |
| Completeness | No — fails in infinite spaces and may miss shallow goals | **Conditional** — complete only when $\ell \geq d$ |
| Space Complexity | $O(b \cdot m)$ where $m$ is max depth reached | $O(b \cdot \ell)$ where $\ell$ is the user-defined limit |
| Time Complexity | $O(b^m)$ unbounded | $O(b^{\ell})$ bounded |
| Optimality | No | No |
