| Applicability to Infinite Spaces | **Unsafe** — may never terminate | **Safe** — bound on depth ensures termination |
| User Parameter Required | None | Depth limit $\ell$ must be specified |

**Detailed discussion:**

Pure DFS is a depth-first exploration with no upper bound on path length. In a state space containing cycles, DFS may revisit nodes infinitely; in an infinite branching graph, DFS may descend forever. DLS addresses both issues by introducing a hard cutoff at depth $\ell$, guaranteeing that the recursion/stack depth never exceeds $\ell$ and that the total number of node expansions is finite.

The trade-off is that DLS sacrifices completeness when $\ell$ is chosen too small — the algorithm will return CUTOFF or FAILURE even when a goal exists deeper in the tree. This makes the choice of $\ell$ a critical engineering decision.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Setup:** Consider a tree with root $R$ and branching factor $b = 2$. There are two goal nodes: $G_1$ at depth 2 and $G_2$ at depth 1. Depth limit is set to $\ell = 2$.

**Tree structure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level 0:} \quad & R \\
\text{Level 1:} \quad & A, B \\
\text{Level 2:} \quad & G_1, C, D, E \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $G_1$ is a child of $A$, and $G_2 = B$ itself is a goal at depth 1.

**DLS Execution Trace:**

| Step | Action | Stack (top → bottom) | Result |
|------|--------|----------------------|--------|
| 1 | Push root $R$ | $[R]$ | — |
| 2 | Pop $R$, expand | $[B, A]$ | — |
| 3 | Pop $A$ (depth 1) | $[B]$ | Goal test: $A \neq G$ |
| 4 | Expand $A$: push children | $[G_1, C, B]$ | — |
| 5 | Pop $G_1$ (depth 2) | $[C, B]$ | **Goal test: YES → SUCCESS** |
| 6 | Return path $R \rightarrow A \rightarrow G_1$ | — | Path length = 2 |

**Analysis:**

The optimal (shortest) path is $R \rightarrow B = G_2$ with length 1. However, DLS found the **deeper goal $G_1$ at depth 2** first. This occurred because DLS expands children in LIFO order, and node $A$ was pushed *before* $B$ in step 2, causing $A$'s subtree to be explored first.

**Conclusion:** DLS is **not optimal** because its LIFO expansion order makes it sensitive to the order in which successors are generated. Even when both $\ell \geq d$ and multiple goals exist, DLS may return a non-optimal goal. Optimality in uninformed search is only guaranteed by BFS (or Uniform-Cost Search for weighted graphs).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in DLS Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\ell$ and $d$ in complexity formulas:** Students frequently write $O(b^d)$ for DLS time complexity. The correct answer is $O(b^{\ell})$. Loss: 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Treating the limit as exclusive:** Students often write "if $d(n) > \ell$, cutoff" instead of "if $d(n) \geq \ell$, cutoff" (or vice versa). The KTU standard accepts the inclusive form $d(n) \geq \ell$ (no expansion) but the goal test at $d(n) = \ell$ is permitted. Loss: 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the FAILURE state:** Students often only mention SUCCESS and CUTOFF. The FAILURE state (entire space exhausted) is a distinct third return state. Loss: 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Stating DLS is complete without qualification:** DLS is **conditionally complete** — the answer must include the condition $\ell \geq d$. Loss: 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Wrong space complexity formula:** Writing $O(b^{\ell})$ instead of $O(b \cdot \ell)$. Loss: 2 marks. The space complexity is **linear** in the depth limit, not exponential.
>
> 6. **Not distinguishing CUTOFF from FAILURE:** These are semantically different. CUTOFF means a branch was truncated; FAILURE means the entire space was exhausted. Examiners award marks for this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Depth-Limited Search**

- **Definition:** DFS variant with a hard depth cutoff at level $\ell$; nodes beyond this depth are treated as leaves without successors.
- **Time complexity:** $O(b^{\ell})$ — bounded exponential in the depth limit.
- **Space complexity:** $O(b \cdot \ell)$ — linear in depth limit; the **key advantage** over BFS.
- **Completeness:** **Conditional** — guaranteed only if $\ell \geq d$ (depth limit at least as deep as the shallowest goal).
- **Optimality:** **No** — not optimal in general; LIFO order may return a deeper goal before a shallower one.
- **Three return states:** SUCCESS, FAILURE, CUTOFF — all three must be mentioned for full credit.
- **Termination:** **Always guaranteed** because at most $b^{\ell}$ nodes can be expanded.
- **Critical formula — geometric sum:** $N_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=0}^{\ell} b^k = \frac{b^{\ell+1} - 1}{b - 1}$.
- **Connection to other algorithms:** DLS = DFS + depth bound. IDDFS = DLS wrapped in an outer loop incrementing $\ell$ from $0$ to $\infty$.
- **Engineering use case:** Choose DLS when memory is severely constrained and the approximate goal depth is known in advance.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Never write $O(b^d)$ for DLS complexity — always use $\ell$, not $d$, because the algorithm's resource consumption is governed by the user-defined limit.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Never claim DLS is unconditionally complete — always pair the statement with the condition $\ell \geq d$.
- **Exam-ready phrase:** *"DLS trades completeness for termination guarantee, preserving the linear space advantage of DFS while bounding the search horizon."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search (IDDFS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search (IDDFS)** is a state-space / graph search strategy that combines the space efficiency of Depth-First Search (DFS) with the **optimality and completeness** of Breadth-First Search (BFS). It works by repeatedly executing a depth-limited DFS with an incrementally increasing depth bound, starting from depth **0**, then **1**, then **2**, and so on, until the goal node is found or the maximum allowable depth is exhausted.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCITT602) terminology, IDDFS is classified as an **uninformed (blind) search algorithm** that systematically explores the search tree in successive, layered sweeps, guaranteeing discovery of the shallowest goal state in an unweighted, finite search graph.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** IDDFS is treated as the *de-facto* optimal blind search strategy when the search space is large, the depth of the solution is unknown, and memory is a critical resource. It is the algorithm of choice in problems like the **15-puzzle, Rubik's cube solvers, chess engines, and recursive puzzle solving**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Treasure Hunter and the Expanding Net"

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a vast, murky lake searching for a sunken treasure chest.

- A **BFS** diver would be like a wide, shallow net dragged horizontally — sweeping the entire surface level by level. It is thorough but extremely expensive in terms of fuel (memory).
- A **DFS** diver would be like a thin, super-long rope lowered straight down at a single point — cheap on memory, but you risk missing the chest if it is not exactly at the chosen spot, and you might run out of rope (depth limit) before finding it.
- An **IDDFS** diver is the smartest of all: he lowers the rope to a depth of **1 meter**, sweeps across the lake, pulls it up. Then lowers it to **2 meters**, sweeps across, pulls it up. Then **3 meters**, **4 meters**, and so on — each time going *just a little deeper* than before.

The first time the net actually catches the chest, you have found the shallowest treasure, with minimal rope usage, and the cost of repeated shallow sweeps is negligible compared to the savings in fuel.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

The following metrics must be memorized verbatim for KTU Part A and Part B questions:

- **Branching factor ($b$):** the maximum number of successors of any node.
- **Depth of the shallowest goal ($d$):** the depth at which the first goal state appears.
- **Maximum depth of the state space ($m$):** the depth of the shallowest *dead-end* or the deepest node.
- **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$
- **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$
- **Complete?:** **Yes** (in finite spaces).
- **Optimal?:** **Yes** (when step costs are uniform).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** When asked *“Why is IDDFS preferred over BFS in practice?”* — the standard model answer is: *“IDDFS requires only $\mathcal{O}(bd)$ memory, while BFS requires $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ memory. For a branching factor of 10 and depth 12, BFS would need roughly 10,000,000,000 nodes in memory, while IDDFS needs only 120.”*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The 4-Phase Logic of IDDFS

1. **Phase 1 — Initialization:** Set the depth bound $\ell = 0$. Maintain a counter for the deepest node explored.
2. **Phase 2 — Depth-Limited DFS:** Run a standard depth-first search, but **prune any branch** whose depth exceeds $\ell$. If the goal is found, terminate and return the solution.
3. **Phase 3 — Bound Increment:** If no goal is found at depth $\ell$, increment $\ell \leftarrow \ell + 1$ and return to Phase 2.
4. **Phase 4 — Termination:** Stop when the goal is found, OR when the depth bound exceeds a pre-defined cutoff $C$ (failure), OR when the entire state space has been exhausted.

### 2.2 Why IDDFS Works — The Two Key Intuitions

- **Intuition A — Optimality is preserved because of layered exploration:** Because IDDFS explores *all* nodes at depth $d$ before *any* node at depth $d+1$, the first time it encounters a goal, that goal is provably the *shallowest* one. This is the same completeness/optimality argument as BFS.
- **Intuition B — Memory is preserved because of the DFS backbone:** Each individual iteration is a strict depth-first search, so the algorithm never needs to store more than a single path from the root to a leaf plus the unexpanded siblings on that path. The recursion stack handles everything.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MANDATORY LaTeX Encoding Rule:** All absolute values and conditionals below use the LaTeX-safe delimiter `\vert` instead of the keyboard pipe `|`, to prevent breaking the markdown table renderer.

| Parameter / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Plain-English Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time Complexity (worst case) | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | Same asymptotic cost as BFS | nodes expanded |
| Space Complexity (worst case) | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ | Only one path + sibling stack | nodes in RAM |
| Nodes generated at iteration $\ell$ | $b^{\ell}$ | Geometric growth per layer | nodes |
| Total nodes generated (up to depth $d$) | $\displaystyle \sum_{i=0}^{d} b^{i} = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ | Geometric sum of generated nodes | nodes |
| Effective nodes from repeat expansion | $(d+1) \cdot 1 \;+\; d \cdot b \;+\; \ldots \;+\; 1 \cdot b^{d}$ | Higher-order nodes are re-generated | nodes |
| Ratio of IDDFS cost to single DFS-to-$d$ cost | $\dfrac{b}{(b-1)^{2}}$ for $b>1$ | Overhead is bounded by a small constant | dimensionless |
| Completeness (finite graphs) | True (with depth cutoff $C \geq d$) | Will find a solution if one exists | boolean |
| Optimality (unit step costs) | True | First goal is always the shallowest | boolean |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of IDDFS

- **Game-Tree Solvers:** The algorithms used in deep chess engines, Checkers solvers, and Shogi engines rely on IDDFS-style depth expansion (e.g., $\alpha$-$\beta$ pruning is frequently embedded inside an IDDFS loop).
- **Automated Planning:** Classical planners (STRIPS, PDDL solvers) use IDDFS to incrementally build plans of length 1, 2, 3, … until a feasible plan is found.
- **Cryptographic Key Search:** Brute-force password crackers use IDDFS to try all 1-character passwords, then all 2-character, then all 3-character, … so that short passwords are found quickly.
- **Hardware Verification:** Model checkers for digital circuits often use IDDFS to find the shortest counterexample to a safety property.
- **Puzzle Solvers:** The standard 15-puzzle and 8-puzzle solvers in AI textbooks use IDDFS as the canonical baseline.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Number of Nodes Generated by IDDFS

**Goal:** Prove that the total number of nodes generated by IDDFS up to finding a goal at depth $d$ is asymptotically $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$, i.e., the same as BFS.

**Step 1 — Set up the layered node count.**

In iteration $\ell$, IDDFS performs a depth-limited DFS to depth $\ell$. In a balanced $b$-ary tree, the number of nodes generated is:

$$
N_{\ell} = 1 + b + b^{2} + \cdots + b^{\ell} = \frac{b^{\ell+1} - 1}{b - 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum across all iterations from $\ell = 0$ to $\ell = d$.**

The total number of node generations across all iterations is the sum of geometric sums:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \sum_{\ell=0}^{d} N_{\ell} = \sum_{\ell=0}^{d} \frac{b^{\ell+1} - 1}{b - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand and separate the sums.**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{b-1} \left[ \sum_{\ell=0}^{d} b^{\ell+1} - \sum_{\ell=0}^{d} 1 \right]
$$

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{b-1} \left[ (b + b^{2} + \cdots + b^{d+1}) - (d+1) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Factor the geometric series.**

$$
b + b^{2} + \cdots + b^{d+1} = b \cdot \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{b-1} \left[ \frac{b (b^{d+1} - 1)}{b - 1} - (d+1) \right] = \frac{b^{d+2} - b}{(b-1)^{2}} - \frac{d+1}{b-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the asymptotic limit.**

As $d \to \infty$, the dominant term is $b^{d+2} / (b-1)^2$, and since the lower-order terms grow at most polynomially in $d$:

$$
N_{\text{total}} \in \mathcal{O}(b^{d})
$$

This proves that the multiplicative overhead of IDDFS over BFS is the constant factor $\dfrac{b}{(b-1)^{2}}$, which for $b=10$ is only $\dfrac{10}{81} \approx 0.123$ of the BFS node count *plus* the constant re-expansion cost — small enough to be ignored in asymptotic analysis.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Example ($b=2, d=3$)

**Search Tree (binary, depth 3):**

| Iteration $\ell$ | Depth Limit | Nodes Generated (Layer Sum) | Running Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | $1$ | $1$ |
| 1 | 1 | $1 + 2 = 3$ | $4$ |
| 2 | 2 | $1 + 2 + 4 = 7$ | $11$ |
| 3 | 3 | $1 + 2 + 4 + 8 = 15$ | $26$ |

**Verification using the closed form:**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \frac{2^{5} - 2}{(2-1)^{2}} - \frac{4}{1} = \frac{32 - 2}{1} - 4 = 30 - 4 = 26
$$

This matches the manual enumeration exactly.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — IDDFS vs BFS vs DFS

| Property | BFS | Uniform-Cost Search | DFS | IDDFS (KTU Focus) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{\lfloor C^{*} / \epsilon \rfloor})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{m})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ |
| Space Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{\lfloor C^{*} / \epsilon \rfloor})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot m)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ |
| Complete? | Yes | Yes (positive step costs) | No (fails on infinite paths) | Yes (with cutoff) |
| Optimal? | Yes (unit costs) | Yes | No | Yes (unit costs) |
| Memory footprint | Very high | Very high | Very low | Very low |
| Re-generates nodes? | No | No | No | Yes (but bounded) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart of the IDDFS Control Loop

The diagram below renders the full control flow of the iterative-deepening algorithm as a Mermaid state machine, suitable for reproduction in KTU answer scripts and lab records.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start IDDFS]) --> initNode["Initialize depth limit ell = 0"]
    initNode --> dlsCall["Call Depth-Limited DFS with bound ell"]
    dlsCall --> goalCheck{"Goal found?"}
    goalCheck -- "Yes" --> successNode([Return solution path ell])
    goalCheck -- "No" --> boundCheck{"ell greater than cutoff C?"}
    boundCheck -- "Yes" --> failureNode([Return FAILURE])
    boundCheck -- "No" --> incrementNode["Increment ell = ell + 1"]
    incrementNode --> dlsCall
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The single rectangular block `dlsCall` represents the inner depth-limited DFS subroutine, which is invoked once per iteration.
- The two diamonds `goalCheck` and `boundCheck` are the only decision points — they determine whether the algorithm terminates successfully, fails, or recurses.

### 4.2 Layered Search-Tree Schematic (Binary Tree, Depth 3)

The following Mermaid block draws the *same* tree explored by IDDFS in Section 3.2, annotated with the iteration index at which each level is explored.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Root Level 0 - Iteration 0] --> childB1[Left Level 1 - Iteration 1]
    rootA --> childB2[Right Level 1 - Iteration 1]
    childB1 --> grandC1[Left-Left Level 2 - Iteration 2]
    childB1 --> grandC2[Left-Right Level 2 - Iteration 2]
    childB2 --> grandC3[Right-Left Level 2 - Iteration 2]
    childB2 --> grandC4[Right-Right Level 2 - Iteration 2]
    grandC1 --> leafD1[LLL Goal Check Iteration 3]
    grandC1 --> leafD2[LLR Goal Check Iteration 3]
    grandC2 --> leafD3[LRL Goal Check Iteration 3]
    grandC2 --> leafD4[LRR Goal Check Iteration 3]
    grandC3 --> leafD5[RLL Goal Check Iteration 3]
    grandC3 --> leafD6[RLR Goal Check Iteration 3]
    grandC4 --> leafD7[RRL Goal Check Iteration 3]
    grandC4 --> leafD8[RRR Goal Check Iteration 3]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Iteration 0 visits only the root.
- Iteration 1 visits the root and its two children.
- Iteration 2 re-visits those plus the four grandchildren.
- Iteration 3 finally touches all 8 leaves and finds the goal.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (IDDFS Engine)

This block diagram maps the runtime interactions of the data structures and sub-routines of an IDDFS engine, which is the standard answer required when KTU asks for an *“algorithmic architecture”* style question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph controlLayer[Control Layer]
        ctrlA[Main IDDFS Loop] --> ctrlB[Depth Bound Manager]
        ctrlB --> ctrlA
    end
    subgraph searchLayer[Search Layer]
        sA[Depth Limited DFS Subroutine] --> sB[Recursive Stack Manager]
        sB --> sA
    end
    subgraph memoryLayer[Memory Layer]
        mA[Open List Path Stack] --> mB[Visited Set Hash Table]
        mB --> mA
    end
    ctrlA -- invokes --> sA
    sA -- reads and writes --> mA
    sA -- updates --> mB
    sB -- pushes and pops --> mA
```

**Reading the architecture:**
- The **Control Layer** owns the iteration counter and depth-bound updates.
- The **Search Layer** performs the actual depth-first expansion and backtracking.
- The **Memory Layer** stores the current path (a stack) and the visited/expanded set.
- Importantly, only the *path stack* grows to depth $d$, giving the $\mathcal{O}(bd)$ space complexity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Policy (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A (3 marks):** Definition, list-based, or direct 2- or 3-line conceptual questions. No choice.
> - **Part B (14 marks):** Two-part questions, typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Each module contains an **internal choice** — the student must attempt *one* full 14-mark question OR attempt both (a) parts of two questions.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search (IDDFS). State its completeness and optimality properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search (IDDFS) is an uninformed search strategy that repeatedly performs a depth-limited DFS with an incrementally increasing depth bound (0, 1, 2, …) until the goal is found. It combines the memory efficiency of DFS with the completeness and optimality of BFS. **Completeness:** IDDFS is complete on finite graphs with a sufficient depth cutoff. **Optimality:** IDDFS is optimal when all step costs are uniform (equal to 1), because the first goal it encounters is provably the shallowest one in the search tree.

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected]`
**Compare the space complexity of BFS, DFS, and IDDFS in terms of branching factor $b$ and depth $d$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Algorithm | Space Complexity | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| BFS | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | Must store the entire frontier at the deepest level. |
| DFS | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot m)$ | Stores one path of length $m$ plus sibling stack. |
| IDDFS | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ | Stores one path of length $d$ plus sibling stack; rerun for each $\ell$. |

Hence IDDFS is the most space-efficient among the three while preserving BFS-style optimality.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse the depth limit $\ell$ with the depth of the search tree $d$. Always clarify that $\ell$ is the *current bound*, while $d$ is the *depth of the shallowest goal*.
> 2. When asked for the *node-generation count*, students often write $b^{d}$ (BFS count) and forget the overhead from re-generating upper-level nodes in IDDFS. The KTU model answer *requires* the geometric-series derivation.
> 3. Do **not** claim IDDFS is optimal for *non-uniform* step costs — that property belongs to Uniform-Cost Search (UCS) / Dijkstra's algorithm. Lose **2 marks** if mis-stated.
> 4. Avoid writing *“IDDFS uses both BFS and DFS”* in vague language. Use the precise phrase *“IDDFS uses depth-limited DFS as its subroutine, repeated with increasing depth bounds, achieving the layered coverage of BFS.”*

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Predicted]`

**(a)** Explain the Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search (IDDFS) algorithm in detail, with pseudocode and a clearly labeled diagram. Discuss why it is preferred over plain BFS for large search spaces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a search tree with branching factor $b = 5$ and shallowest goal at depth $d = 4$, compute the total number of nodes generated by IDDFS up to the iteration where the goal is found. Compare this with the number of nodes a plain BFS would generate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Pseudocode of IDDFS (3 marks):**

   ```
   function IDDFS(root, goal):
       for depth_limit = 0 to MAX_DEPTH:
           result = DEPTH_LIMITED_DFS(root, goal, depth_limit)
           if result != FAILURE:
               return result
       return FAILURE

   function DEPTH_LIMITED_DFS(node, goal, limit):
       if node == goal:
           return node
       if limit == 0:
           return FAILURE
       for each child of node:
           result = DEPTH_LIMITED_DFS(child, goal, limit - 1)
           if result != FAILURE:
               return result
       return FAILURE
   ```

2. **Why IDDFS is preferred over BFS (3 marks):**
   BFS must store *all* nodes at depth $d-1$ in its frontier to expand the next layer. For $b=10$ and $d=12$, that is roughly $10^{11}$ nodes, which exhausts typical RAM. IDDFS, by contrast, only ever stores a single path of length $d$ plus a stack of siblings — at most $10 \cdot 12 = 120$ nodes — while still being complete and optimal for uniform step costs.

3. **Diagram (1 mark):** Include the layered search tree schematic (see Section 4.2) annotated with iteration indices.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute IDDFS node-generation count.**

Using the closed-form expression from Section 3.1:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \frac{b^{d+2} - b}{(b-1)^{2}} - \frac{d+1}{b-1}
$$

Substituting $b=5$ and $d=4$:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \frac{5^{6} - 5}{4^{2}} - \frac{5}{4} = \frac{15625 - 5}{16} - 1.25 = \frac{15620}{16} - 1.25
$$

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 976.25 - 1.25 = 975
$$

**Step 2 — Compute BFS node-generation count.**

$$
N_{\text{BFS}} = 1 + b + b^{2} + b^{3} + b^{4} = \frac{5^{5} - 1}{5 - 1} = \frac{3124}{4} = 781
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison.**

IDDFS generates $975$ nodes, BFS generates $781$ nodes. The *overhead* is $975 - 781 = 194$ extra nodes, which is a **24.8%** overhead. In exchange, IDDFS uses only $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d) = 20$ memory slots versus BFS's $\mathcal{O}(b^{d}) = 625$ slots — a **96.8%** memory reduction. This tradeoff is what justifies the choice of IDDFS in memory-constrained AI systems.

**Final Answer:** IDDFS = **975 nodes**; BFS = **781 nodes**; IDDFS memory is **20 slots**; BFS memory is **625 slots**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Differentiate between Depth-First Search, Breadth-First Search, and Iterative Deepening DFS using a comparison table based on time complexity, space complexity, completeness, and optimality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a real-world example why IDDFS is the algorithm of choice in classical game-tree search and puzzle solving. State two engineering applications where IDDFS is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

| Property | BFS | DFS | IDDFS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{m})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ |
| Space Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot m)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ |
| Complete? | Yes | No | Yes (with cutoff) |
| Optimal? | Yes (unit step costs) | No | Yes (unit step costs) |
| Implementation | Queue (FIFO) | Stack (LIFO) / Recursion | Recursive with depth limit |
| Re-generates nodes? | No | No | Yes (bounded overhead) |

The key trade-off is that **BFS and IDDFS share time and optimality, but IDDFS drastically reduces memory**. DFS uses the least memory but is neither complete nor optimal. Therefore, **IDDFS is the practical sweet spot** in tree search.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Real-World Example (4 marks):**
Consider a **15-puzzle** solver. The branching factor is $b = 2$ to $3$, and the shallowest solution is often at depth $d = 15$ to $30$. A BFS solver would need to store up to $3^{30} \approx 2 \times 10^{14}$ frontier nodes — completely infeasible. A DFS solver would dive into one path forever and might never return. IDDFS incrementally searches for solutions of length 1, 2, 3, …, guaranteeing that the first solution found is the shortest, while using only $3 \times 30 = 90$ memory slots.

**Engineering Applications (3 marks — 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Chess / Checkers / Shogi Engines:** Iterative deepening lets $\alpha$-$\beta$ pruning progressively evaluate deeper positions, with shallower depths providing strong move-ordering hints for deeper iterations.
2. **Cryptographic Brute-Force Crackers:** Password crackers iterate over all 1-character passwords, then 2-character, then 3-character, finding short passwords almost instantly and long passwords eventually.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2**

- **IDDFS = Depth-Limited DFS repeated with increasing depth bound $\ell = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$**
- **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(b^{d})$ — asymptotically identical to BFS.
- **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ — asymptotically identical to DFS.
- **Complete?** Yes, for finite state spaces with a sufficient depth cutoff.
- **Optimal?** Yes, when step costs are uniform (i.e., all edges have cost 1). NOT optimal for variable step costs.
- **Closed-form total node count:** $N_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{b^{d+2} - b}{(b-1)^{2}} - \dfrac{d+1}{b-1}$.
- **Overhead vs BFS:** The asymptotic ratio is bounded by the constant $\dfrac{b}{(b-1)^{2}}$.
- **Advantages over BFS:** Drastically lower memory footprint — the *only* reason it exists as a separate strategy.
- **Advantages over DFS:** Guaranteed completeness and optimality; no infinite-loop risk.
- **Key KTU keywords to memorize:** *uninformed search, blind search, layered exploration, depth limit, repeated expansion, geometric series, path stack, frontier set.*
- **Common KTU pitfall:** Confusing *depth of the search tree ($m$)* with *depth of the shallowest goal ($d$)*. Always write $d$ for IDDFS time complexity.
- **Engineering applications to mention in answers:** 15-puzzle solver, game-tree search with $\alpha$-$\beta$ pruning, password brute-forcers, model checkers, classical STRIPS planners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Informed Search Strategies - Hill Climbing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Informed Search Strategies - Hill Climbing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hill Climbing** is a **heuristic-based local search algorithm** belonging to the family of **Informed Search Strategies**. It iteratively moves from the current state toward a neighboring state that has a **higher value of the evaluation function** (typically denoted by a heuristic $h(n)$ or an objective function $f(n)$). The algorithm terminates when it reaches a state where **no neighbor has a higher evaluation value**, implying a local optimum.

In formal mathematical terms, given the current state $n_{current}$, the algorithm selects a successor $n_{next}$ such that:

$$f(n_{next}) > f(n_{current})$$

where $f(n)$ is the heuristic evaluation function. The search continues until no such $n_{next}$ exists in the neighborhood of $n_{current}$.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (Aligned with KTU 2024 Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Heuristic Function $h(n)$** | An estimated cost or value from node $n$ to the goal. |
| **Evaluation Function $f(n)$** | Function used to rank candidate nodes. |
| **State Space Landscape** | A conceptual topographic map where altitude represents $f(n)$. |
| **Local Maximum** | A state better than all neighbors but not the global optimum. |
| **Global Maximum** | The absolute best state in the entire state space. |
| **Plateau** | A flat region in the landscape where neighboring states have equal $f(n)$. |
| **Ridge** | A region where movement in all 4-connected directions goes downhill. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: Climbing a Mountain in Dense Fog** ⛰️🌫️
>
> Imagine you are dropped onto a hilly terrain at **night** with zero visibility. You can only feel the ground beneath your feet and the slope in the four directions around you. Your strategy is simple: **always step in the direction that takes you highest uphill**. You keep doing this until every direction around you is either flat or goes downhill. At that point, you stop — you have reached a **local peak**.
>
> - You **cannot see the entire mountain range**, so you never know if the true summit (global maximum) is just over a hidden valley.
> - If you climb a small foothill and get stuck, you are at a **local maximum**.
> - If the area around you is perfectly flat, you are on a **plateau** and you might wander aimlessly.
> - The fundamental problem is that **Hill Climbing is a greedy, myopic, local search** — it never looks ahead or backtracks.

## 1.4 Why Hill Climbing Falls Under "Informed Search"

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike **Uninformed Search** (BFS, DFS, UCS) which uses no domain knowledge beyond the problem definition, Hill Climbing uses a **heuristic** $h(n)$ to estimate how close a state is to the goal. This domain knowledge is what makes it an **Informed (Heuristic) Search** technique.

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly classifies Hill Climbing under the "heuristic function-based search" category, distinct from adversarial search and optimization-based search methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — Structured Logic Steps

The Hill Climbing algorithm executes the following logical sequence. Each step is broken down to its core rationale:

1. **Initialize Current State**:
   Begin at an arbitrarily selected (or heuristically chosen) starting node $n_0$ in the state space. This is the "drop point" on the mountain landscape.

2. **Generate Successor Set $N(n_{current})$**:
   Enumerate all possible next states reachable from the current state using the problem's defined operators (moves/actions). In the 8-Queens problem, this means moving one queen at a time.

3. **Evaluate Each Successor**:
   Compute the heuristic value $h(n_i)$ for every $n_i \in N(n_{current})$. This is the "feel the slope around you" step.

4. **Greedy Selection**:
   Select the successor $n_{next}$ with the **best heuristic value**:
   $$n_{next} = \arg\max_{n_i \in N(n_{current})} h(n_i)$$
   If $h(n_{next}) \leq h(n_{current})$, terminate. Otherwise, set $n_{current} = n_{next}$ and **loop back to Step 2**.

5. **Termination Condition**:
   Algorithm halts when no neighbor improves upon the current state. The reached state is reported as the **solution candidate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind the Greedy Move**:
> Hill Climbing **does not maintain a search tree** in memory. It only remembers the current state and its immediate neighbors. This makes it **memory-efficient** but also **incomplete** (it may not find a solution even if one exists) and **non-optimal** (it returns a local optimum, not necessarily a global one).

## 2.2 Variants of Hill Climbing (High-Yield for KTU Board Exams)

| Variant | Behavior | Use Case |
|---------|----------|----------|
| **Simple Hill Climbing** | Evaluates one random neighbor at a time; moves if it improves. | Slow, low memory. |
| **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** | Evaluates **all** neighbors; moves to the **best** neighbor. | KTU favorite variant. |
| **Stochastic Hill Climbing** | Chooses a random neighbor among those that improve $h(n)$ with some probability. | Avoids determinism. |
| **Random-Restart Hill Climbing** | Conducts multiple hill climbs from random initial points; returns the best. | Recommended in Russell & Norvig for overcoming local maxima. |

## 2.3 The Three Classic Failure Modes (HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU EXAMS)

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask about these failure modes. Memorize the names and examples.

### 2.3.1 Local Maxima
A **local maximum** is a state that is better than every neighbor but is **not the global optimum**. The algorithm is "trapped" because every step it considers leads downhill or stays flat.

**Example**: In the 8-Queens problem, a configuration with $h=2$ (only 2 attacking pairs remaining) might be a local maximum — the algorithm cannot reduce the number of attacks further, but a global solution with $h=0$ exists.

### 2.3.2 Plateaus
A **plateau** is a flat region in the state space where neighboring states have the **same heuristic value**. The algorithm cannot determine the best direction by slope alone. 

**Resolution strategies**:
- Take a **random sideways move** (with a limit, say 100 moves) to escape.
- Use a stochastic variant.

### 2.3.3 Ridges
A **ridge** is a sequence of local maxima that is not traversable by single-step moves in the standard 4-connected neighborhood. From any position on the ridge, all four compass directions lead downhill.

**Resolution strategies**:
- Use **two-move lookahead** (operator pairs).
- Move in directions **perpendicular** to the ridge line.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| Greedy move condition | $f(n_{next}) > f(n_{current})$ | Move only if improvement exists. |
| Steepest-ascent selection | $n_{next} = \arg\max_{n_i} f(n_i)$ | Pick the best neighbor. |
| Sideways move allowance | $\text{consecutive sideways} \leq 100$ | Prevents infinite plateau wandering. |
| 8-Queens heuristic | $h(n) = \text{number of attacking queen pairs}$ | Lower is better; goal $h=0$. |
| Random-restart probability | $P(\text{success after } k \text{ restarts}) = 1 - (1-p)^k$ | $p$ = success probability of one run. |
| Termination criterion | $\forall n_i \in N(n_{current}), f(n_i) \leq f(n_{current})$ | No improving move exists. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**:
> Hill Climbing (and its variants) power **neural network weight optimization**, **VLSI chip layout**, **job-shop scheduling**, **robot path planning**, and **hyperparameter tuning** in machine learning pipelines. It is a foundational local search paradigm taught before advanced metaheuristics like Simulated Annealing, Genetic Algorithms, and Tabu Search.

## 2.5 Comparison with Other Informed Searches (KTU Board Favourite)

| Property | Hill Climbing | Best-First Search | A* Search |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Memory** | Constant $O(1)$ | Stores frontier | Stores open + closed lists |
| **Backtracking** | No | No | No (but can be modified) |
| **Optimality** | Not guaranteed | Not guaranteed | Guaranteed (with admissible $h$) |
| **Completeness** | Not guaranteed | Yes (in finite graphs) | Yes (in finite graphs) |
| **Heuristic usage** | Local $h(n)$ only | $f(n) = h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: 8-Queens via Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8-Queens problem is the **canonical KTU exam example** for Hill Climbing. Be prepared to trace the algorithm step-by-step.

### Problem Setup
Place 8 queens on an 8×8 chessboard such that **no two queens attack each other**. The heuristic is:
$$h(n) = \text{number of pairs of queens that attack each other}$$

A solution is reached when $h(n) = 0$.

### Step 1: Initial State
Let the initial state have queens on columns: `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]` (queen $i$ on row $i$, column $i$).
This is the **worst possible** configuration. Compute $h(n_0)$:

Each queen on the diagonal attacks 2 others. Total pairs on the main diagonal:
$$\binom{8}{2} = 28 \text{ attacking pairs}$$

So $h(n_0) = 28$.

### Step 2: Generate All Successors
Move **one queen at a time** within its column to any other row. This generates:
$$\text{Successors} = 8 \text{ queens} \times 7 \text{ alternative rows} = 56 \text{ states}$$

### Step 3: Evaluate Each Successor (exhaustive for exam)
For each of the 56 successors, count the number of attacking pairs. Identify the state with the **minimum** $h$ (steepest descent in attacking pairs, since lower is better).

Suppose the best successor has $h = 23$. Since $23 < 28$, we move.

### Step 4: Iterative Progression
The algorithm continues:

| Iteration | Best $h$ Found | Action |
|-----------|----------------|--------|
| 0 | 28 | Initialize |
| 1 | 23 | Move (improvement of 5) |
| 2 | 18 | Move (improvement of 5) |
| 3 | 12 | Move (improvement of 6) |
| 4 | 6 | Move (improvement of 6) |
| 5 | 2 | Move (improvement of 4) |
| 6 | 2 | **Local maximum reached — TERMINATE** |

### Step 5: Failure Analysis
At $h = 2$, **no single-queen move reduces the attacking pairs further**. The algorithm halts at a local maximum. The true global optimum $h = 0$ is **never reached**.

**Probability of Success**: Russell & Norvig (a standard KTU reference framework) estimate that Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing solves **only about 14%** of 8-Queens instances. With **Random-Restart Hill Climbing**, success probability becomes:
$$P(\text{success after } k \text{ restarts}) = 1 - (1 - 0.14)^k$$

To achieve **95% success**:
$$1 - (0.86)^k \geq 0.95$$
$$(0.86)^k \leq 0.05$$
$$k \cdot \ln(0.86) \leq \ln(0.05)$$
$$k \geq \frac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.86)} = \frac{-2.996}{-0.151} \approx 19.85$$

So **$k = 20$ restarts** are needed for 95% success.

## 3.2 Mathematical Foundation of the Heuristic

For the 8-Queens problem, the heuristic $h$ is precisely the number of **non-attacking pair violations**. Mathematically, for queens at positions $(r_i, c_i)$:

$$h(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{7} \sum_{j=i+1}^{8} \mathbb{1}[\text{queens } i \text{ and } j \text{ attack each other}]$$

where the attack condition holds if:

$$(c_i = c_j) \lor (r_i = r_j) \lor (\vert r_i - r_j \vert = \vert c_i - c_j \vert)$$

## 3.3 Proof: Hill Climbing is Neither Complete Nor Optimal

> **Claim 1 (Incompleteness)**:
> Hill Climbing is **not complete** — it may fail to find a goal state even when one exists.

**Reasoning**:
A complete algorithm must find a solution if one exists. Hill Climbing terminates at a **local maximum** that may not be a goal. Since the algorithm does not backtrack and does not explore the full state space, it can get trapped indefinitely. Therefore, there exist solvable problem instances for which Hill Climbing returns "failure" — it is **incomplete**.

> **Claim 2 (Non-Optimality)**:
> Hill Climbing is **not optimal** — it does not guarantee the best solution.

**Reasoning**:
Optimality requires returning the **lowest-cost path** or **best state**. Since Hill Climbing stops at the first local maximum encountered, and a local maximum is by definition not the global maximum, the algorithm returns a sub-optimal state. The search is purely local and ignores global structure.

$$\therefore \text{Hill Climbing is } \boxed{\text{neither complete nor optimal}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Variant

| Problem Characteristic | Recommended Hill Climbing Variant |
|------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Small state space, deterministic | **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** |
| Large state space with many local maxima | **Random-Restart Hill Climbing** |
| Need to escape plateaus | **Stochastic Hill Climbing with sideways moves** |
| Continuous optimization | **Continuous / Gradient-Ascent Hill Climbing** |
| Real-time, memory-constrained | **Simple Hill Climbing** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 State Space Landscape (Mermaid Conceptual Model)

The state space is often visualized as a topographic surface where the **height represents the heuristic value** $f(n)$. The algorithm climbs "uphill" toward local peaks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Current State S0, f=10] --> B{Evaluate All Neighbors}
    B --> C[Neighbor N1, f=15]
    B --> D[Neighbor N2, f=8]
    B --> E[Neighbor N3, f=12]
    B --> F[Neighbor N4, f=10]
    C --> G{Max of Neighbors: N1 with f=15}
    G --> H[Move to N1, New Current, f=15]
    H --> I[Repeat: Evaluate Neighbors of N1]
    I --> J[All Neighbors f <= 15]
    J --> K[STOP: Local Maximum Reached]
    K --> L[Return Current State as Solution]
```

## 4.2 Failure Mode Visualization (Conceptual Topography)

This block diagram maps the three classic failure modes onto a single functional architecture to help students internalize the difference.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LM[Local Maximum Failure]
        LM1[Current State] --> LM2[Climb Up Slope]
        LM2 --> LM3[Peak Found]
        LM3 --> LM4[STOP: Global Optimum Hidden Beyond Valley]
    end
    
    subgraph PL[Plateau Failure]
        PL1[Current State] --> PL2[All Neighbors Equal f Value]
        PL2 --> PL3[No Directional Gradient]
        PL3 --> PL4[Sideways Move Needed]
    end
    
    subgraph RG[Ridge Failure]
        RG1[Current State on Ridge] --> RG2[4-Connected Neighbors All Lower]
        RG2 --> RG3[Diagonal Move Would Climb]
        RG3 --> RG4[Requires 2-Move Lookahead]
    end
```

## 4.3 Algorithm Flowchart (Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start] --> S1[Initialize: Current = Initial State]
    S1 --> S2[Generate ALL Successors of Current]
    S2 --> S3[Evaluate f for Each Successor]
    S3 --> S4{Best Successor f greater than Current f?}
    S4 -->|No| S5[RETURN Current as Final State]
    S4 -->|Yes| S6[Current = Best Successor]
    S6 --> S2
    S5 --> S7[End]
```

## 4.4 Random-Restart Hill Climbing — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Random Restart Loop] --> B[Generate Random Initial State]
    B --> C[Run Steepest Ascent Hill Climbing]
    C --> D{Goal Reached?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Return Solution]
    D -->|No| F[Record Local Maximum]
    F --> G{More Restarts Remaining?}
    G -->|Yes| A
    G -->|No| H[Return Best Local Maximum Found]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Block Diagram: Search Strategy Family

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Informed Search Strategies] --> R1[Hill Climbing Local Search]
    R --> R2[Best First Search]
    R --> R3[A Star Search]
    R --> R4[Simulated Annealing]
    R --> R5[Genetic Algorithms]
    
    R1 --> R1a[Simple]
    R1 --> R1b[Steepest Ascent]
    R1 --> R1c[Stochastic]
    R1 --> R1d[Random Restart]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define Hill Climbing search. State **any two** limitations of Hill Climbing.

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
Hill Climbing is a heuristic-based local search algorithm that iteratively moves to a neighboring state with a higher value of the evaluation function, terminating when no neighbor offers an improvement. **[1 mark]**

Limitations: **[1 mark each, any two]**
- **Local Maxima**: Algorithm gets trapped at a sub-optimal peak.
- **Plateaus**: Flat regions provide no gradient to direct the search.
- **Ridges**: Diagonal ascents are missed by 4-connected moves.
- **Incompleteness and Non-optimality**: It cannot guarantee finding a solution or the best one.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between **Simple Hill Climbing** and **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing**.

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

| Aspect | Simple Hill Climbing | Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing |
|--------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| Successor evaluation | One at a time (random) | All successors evaluated |
| Move decision | First improvement accepted | Best of all improvements |
| Speed | Faster per iteration | Slower per iteration |
| Quality | May get stuck earlier | Better local optima |

**[1 mark for correct definition of each, 1 mark for the table]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 2 Internal Choice Set A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** algorithm with its pseudocode. Discuss how it differs from Simple Hill Climbing. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Apply Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing to solve a **4-Queens problem** starting from the initial state where queens are placed at positions $(1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (4,4)$. Show at least **3 iterations** and identify whether the algorithm reaches the goal. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** is a variant of local search where the algorithm evaluates **every** successor of the current state and moves to the one with the **highest heuristic value** $f(n)$. **[1 mark]**

**Pseudocode**: **[3 marks]**

```
ALGORITHM: Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing
INPUT: Initial state S0, heuristic function h
OUTPUT: Local maximum state or goal

1.  Current ← S0
2.  LOOP:
3.      Generate all successors N(Current) of Current
4.      Best ← arg max_{n ∈ N(Current)} h(n)
5.      IF h(Best) ≤ h(Current) THEN
6.          RETURN Current  // Local maximum
7.      END IF
8.      Current ← Best
9.  END LOOP
```

**Differences from Simple Hill Climbing**: **[3 marks]**
- Simple HC picks a **random neighbor** and accepts it if it improves.
- Steepest-Ascent HC **exhaustively evaluates all neighbors** and picks the best.
- Steepest-Ascent is **slower per step** but generally reaches **better local optima**.
- Simple HC uses less memory and is faster overall in some cases.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Initial State** for 4-Queens: queens at $(1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (4,4)$. This is the main diagonal.

**Compute $h_0$**: 
- Queen 1 at $(1,1)$ attacks Queen 2 at $(2,2)$ (diagonal), Queen 3 at $(3,3)$ (diagonal), Queen 4 at $(4,4)$ (diagonal) → **3 attacks**
- Queen 2 at $(2,2)$ attacks Queen 3, Queen 4 → 2 more
- Queen 3 at $(3,3)$ attacks Queen 4 → 1 more
- **Total** $h_0 = 3 + 2 + 1 = \mathbf{6}$ attacking pairs **[1 mark]**

**Iteration 1**: 
Move each queen to all other rows in its column. The 12 successors have various $h$ values. The best successor has $h = 3$ (achieved by moving Queen 4 from row 4 to row 1, for example). Move to this state. **[2 marks]**

**Iteration 2**: 
From the new state with $h=3$, evaluate successors. Best found: $h = 1$. Move. **[2 marks]**

**Iteration 3**: 
From $h=1$, evaluate successors. The configuration has 1 attacking pair. All single-queen moves either keep $h=1$ or increase it. **Local maximum reached**. **[2 marks]**

**Conclusion**: The algorithm halts at a local maximum with $h=1$. The true goal of $h=0$ is **not reached** — demonstrating the **local maxima problem** of Hill Climbing.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 2 Internal Choice Set B]**

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss the **three failure modes** of Hill Climbing: local maxima, plateaus, and ridges. Suggest **at least one mitigation strategy** for each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain **Random-Restart Hill Climbing** with a suitable example. Show mathematically that the probability of success approaches 1 as the number of restarts increases. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Failure Mode 1: Local Maxima** **[2 marks]**
- A local maximum is a state better than all its neighbors but not the global optimum.
- The algorithm halts because no single move improves $f(n)$.
- *Example*: 8-Queens at $h=2$ with no improving move.
- **Mitigation**: Use **Random-Restart Hill Climbing** — restart from a new random state when stuck.

**Failure Mode 2: Plateaus** **[2 marks]**
- A plateau is a flat region where neighbors have equal $f(n)$.
- The algorithm cannot determine a gradient direction.
- **Mitigation**: Allow a **limited number of sideways moves** (typically ≤100) to traverse the plateau.

**Failure Mode 3: Ridges** **[2 marks]**
- A ridge is a region where the only way to ascend is via diagonal moves, but standard moves are axis-aligned.
- **Mitigation**: Use **2-move lookahead** (consider pairs of operators) or move perpendicular to the ridge.

**Conclusion**: **[1 mark]** These failure modes make plain Hill Climbing incomplete; hence variants like Random-Restart and Stochastic HC are used in practice.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Random-Restart Hill Climbing** conducts a series of hill-climbing searches from **randomly generated initial states**. It keeps track of the best solution found across all restarts. **[2 marks]**

**Mathematical Proof of Convergence**: **[5 marks]**

Let $p$ be the probability that a single hill-climbing run finds the goal. For 8-Queens, $p \approx 0.14$.

Each run is **independent** (random initial state). The probability that a single run **fails** to find the goal is $(1-p)$.

After $k$ independent restarts, the probability that **all** runs fail is:
$$P(\text{all fail after } k \text{ restarts}) = (1-p)^k$$

The probability that **at least one** restart succeeds is:
$$P(\text{success after } k \text{ restarts}) = 1 - (1-p)^k$$

As $k \to \infty$:
$$\lim_{k \to \infty} (1-p)^k = 0 \quad \text{(since } 0 < (1-p) < 1\text{)}$$

$$\therefore \lim_{k \to \infty} P(\text{success}) = 1 - 0 = 1$$

Hence, Random-Restart Hill Climbing is **complete with probability 1** as the number of restarts approaches infinity. It is also **optimal in expectation** because it samples the state space broadly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert** ⚠️
> - Students commonly **lose 2 marks** for confusing **Simple HC** with **Steepest-Ascent HC** in pseudocode questions. Always state explicitly whether you evaluate one neighbor or all neighbors.
> - In 8-Queens trace questions, students **forget to count diagonal attacks** in the heuristic. The attack condition is $\vert r_i - r_j \vert = \vert c_i - c_j \vert$, **not just same row/column**.
> - For Random-Restart derivations, students **incorrectly write** $P(\text{success}) = (1-p)^k$ instead of $1 - (1-p)^k$. Memorize the **complement** form.
> - Do **not** confuse **completeness with optimality**. Hill Climbing is incomplete (no solution) and non-optimal (sub-optimal solution). Random-Restart fixes completeness but does not guarantee global optimality in finite restarts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize These Points**

- ✅ Hill Climbing is a **greedy, local, heuristic-based search**.
- ✅ It uses an **evaluation function** $f(n)$ or heuristic $h(n)$ to rank neighbors.
- ✅ **Steepest-Ascent HC** evaluates **all** neighbors and picks the best.
- ✅ **Simple HC** evaluates one neighbor at a time.
- ✅ **Stochastic HC** picks randomly among improving neighbors.
- ✅ **Random-Restart HC** repeats from multiple random starts — **the most practical variant**.
- ✅ **Three failure modes**: Local Maxima, Plateaus, Ridges — all are board-exam favorites.
- ✅ **Completeness**: NOT guaranteed (plain HC) → GUARANTEED with probability 1 (Random-Restart, $k \to \infty$).
- ✅ **Optimality**: NOT guaranteed in any variant (local optima problem).
- ✅ **Time complexity**: $O(\infty)$ in worst case (can loop on plateaus).
- ✅ **Space complexity**: $O(1)$ — only current state is stored.
- ✅ **8-Queens heuristic**: number of attacking pairs; goal is $h=0$.
- ✅ Random-Restart success formula: $P = 1 - (1-p)^k$.
- ✅ Hill Climbing does **not** maintain a search tree, frontier, or closed list.
- ✅ Hill Climbing is **faster but less reliable** than A* or BFS.
- ✅ Real-world applications: VLSI layout, neural network training, job scheduling, robot motion planning.
- ✅ Sideways move limit (typically 100) is used to escape plateaus without infinite loops.
- ✅ The algorithm **terminates** when no neighbor has $f(n) > f(\text{current})$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Best First Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIA GENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Best First Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Best First Search (BFS)** is a heuristic-driven, node-expansion search algorithm that combines the depth-first and breadth-first strategies by using an **evaluation function** $f(n) = h(n)$ to prioritize the expansion of the most "promising" node in the search frontier. The node with the **lowest heuristic value** (estimated cost to the goal) is expanded first.

Formally stated:

$$f(n) = h(n)$$

where:
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate of the cost from node $n$ to the nearest goal state.
- $f(n)$ = the priority score that dictates the order of node expansion in the **OPEN list** (a priority queue ordered ascending by $h$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Safe):** *Best-First Search is a graph/search algorithm in which the node selected for expansion is the one with the lowest value of the heuristic function $h(n)$, i.e., the node estimated to be closest to the goal.*

The algorithm operates on two lists:
- **OPEN List (Frontier):** Contains all generated but unexpanded nodes, kept sorted by $f(n)=h(n)$.
- **CLOSED List (Explored/Visited):** Contains all nodes that have already been expanded.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hiking Guide"

Imagine you are hiking in foggy mountains and want to reach the **summit** (the goal). You cannot see the whole map, but you have a compass that gives an estimate of how far each adjacent peak seems from the summit (this is your **heuristic** $h$).

- A **Breadth-First Search** hiker would systematically check *every* neighboring peak in concentric circles — slow but exhaustive.
- A **Depth-First Search** hiker would just keep climbing whichever path seemed forward — fast, but often ends in a dead-end cliff.
- A **Best-First Search** hiker looks at the compass readings from each visible peak and **always walks toward the peak that looks closest to the summit** based on the estimate — greedy, intuitive, and fast, but risks being misled if the estimate is wrong (you might walk into a valley that *looked* close but actually isn't).

This "compass-guided greedy walker" is exactly how **Best First Search** explores the state space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Heuristic Function (h):** A function that estimates the cost of the cheapest path from a given state $n$ to a goal state. It is problem-specific (e.g., Euclidean distance for maps, Manhattan distance for grids, misplaced tiles for the 8-puzzle).

---

## 1.3 Key Characteristics at a Glance

| Property | Value for Best First Search |
|---|---|
| Strategy Type | **Greedy / Informed / Heuristic** |
| Evaluation Function | $f(n) = h(n)$ |
| Data Structure for OPEN | **Priority Queue (min-heap)** |
| Optimal? | **No** (unless heuristic is perfect) |
| Complete? | **No** (can loop infinitely without cycle detection) |
| Time Complexity | $O(b^m)$ in worst case, $b$ = branching factor, $m$ = max depth |
| Space Complexity | $O(b^m)$ — keeps all nodes in memory |

---

## 1.4 Distinguishing from Related Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do NOT confuse with Breadth-First Search (BFS-graph traversal).** The "BFS" in Best-First Search refers to *choosing the best node first*, NOT breadth-first graph traversal. KTU questions may test this distinction.

| Algorithm | $f(n)$ | Uses Path Cost? | Uses Heuristic? | Optimal? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform Cost Search | $g(n)$ | Yes | No | Yes (for variable costs) |
| Greedy Best First Search | $h(n)$ | No | Yes | No |
| A* Search | $g(n) + h(n)$ | Yes | Yes | Yes (with admissible $h$) |

> **Bold constants / standard metrics used:**
> - **Branching factor $b$** — average number of successors per node.
> - **Heuristic $h$** — must be non-negative, $h(\text{goal}) = 0$ in standard definitions.
> - **Depth $m$** — depth of the shallowest goal node.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Operating Principle

Best First Search operates on the principle of **expanding the locally-best option first**. The term "locally-best" is critical — the algorithm commits to the node that *appears* best according to $h$, without considering the cost already incurred to reach it.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Initialize** the OPEN list with the **start node** $S$. Compute $h(S)$. Initialize the CLOSED list as empty.
2. **Loop** until OPEN is empty *or* the goal is found:
   - **Step 2a:** Select node $n$ from OPEN with the **minimum** $f(n) = h(n)$ (the "best" node).
   - **Step 2b:** **Remove** $n$ from OPEN. **Add** $n$ to CLOSED.
   - **Step 2c:** If $n$ is the **goal node**, return success and the path reconstructed via parent pointers.
   - **Step 2d:** **Expand** $n$: for every successor $n_i$ of $n$:
     - Compute $h(n_i)$.
     - If $n_i$ is **not in OPEN and not in CLOSED**, insert it into OPEN with parent pointer set to $n$.
     - If $n_i$ is **already in OPEN** with a *higher* $h$ cost, replace it (lower $h$ is better).
     - If $n_i$ is **already in CLOSED** with a *higher* $h$ cost, move it back to OPEN (graph-search version).
3. **Failure:** If OPEN becomes empty without finding the goal, return failure.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 2a (Best selection):** This is the greedy heart. By always picking the lowest $h$, we bias search toward nodes estimated to be near the goal.
- **Step 2b (Move to CLOSED):** Prevents re-expansion of the same node, providing cycle protection (without this, the algorithm could oscillate forever).
- **Step 2d (Heuristic recomputation):** Since $h$ is local, re-checking OPEN/CLOSED members allows path-improvement if a shorter heuristic route is discovered.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Notes for KTU Board Exam |
|---|---|---|
| $f(n) = h(n)$ | Evaluation function of Best First Search | **Must write this explicitly** when asked to identify the algorithm |
| $h(n)$ | Heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal | Non-negative real number |
| $h^*(n)$ | True cost from $n$ to nearest goal | Used in admissibility: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ |
| $g(n)$ | Cost from start to $n$ | **Not used** in Best First Search |
| $b$ | Effective branching factor | Avg. number of children per node |
| $m$ | Maximum depth of search space | Determines worst-case complexity |
| $T$ | Total generated nodes | $T = b + b^2 + \dots + b^m = O(b^m)$ |
| Admissibility | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ | Required for A\*, not strictly for Greedy BFS |
| Monotonicity | $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ | Triangle inequality on estimates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Students frequently write $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ in Best First Search questions. That is the **A\*** evaluation function. For Best First Search, **only $h(n)$** is used.

---

## 2.3 Failure Modes (Why Best First Search is Sub-Optimal)

Best First Search has three classic failure modes frequently asked in KTU exams:

1. **Local Optimum / Dead-End Trap:** The algorithm commits to a path that *looks* close to the goal (low $h$) but is actually a dead-end or very long. Example: in a maze, the heuristic may point down a corridor that ends in a wall.

2. **Incompleteness (Infinite Loops):** In a graph with cycles and no CLOSED list, the algorithm can revisit the same node indefinitely. With a CLOSED list, it is complete for **finite graphs**.

3. **Sub-Optimality:** Even if the goal is reached, the path taken may not be the shortest. A classic 8-puzzle counter-example shows Greedy BFS taking 18 moves when optimal is 12.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Best First Search |
|---|---|
| **GPS Navigation** | Early versions used greedy heuristic routing (e.g., "as the crow flies" distance) to pick next road. |
| **Robotic Path Planning** | Initial greedy motion toward detected goal in unknown terrain. |
| **Network Routing** | OSPF-style local-cost preference routing in computer networks. |
| **Puzzle Solvers (8-puzzle, 15-puzzle)** | Educational benchmark for testing heuristic quality. |
| **Game AI (early chess engines)** | Greedy piece-value-based move selection. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern industry, **A\* Search (Module 2 next topic in KTU syllabus)** almost always supersedes Best First Search because it adds $g(n)$ to ensure optimality. Best First Search is taught as a **conceptual stepping stone** to A\*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Working & Analytical Trace

> [!NOTE]
> Since Best First Search is an **algorithmic procedure** (not a physics formula), the "derivation" in this section is the **exhaustive step-by-step trace** of the algorithm on a graph problem — the standard KTU 14-mark question format.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example — 8-Puzzle Trace (Classic KTU Question)

**Problem State:** Given the following 8-puzzle initial and goal states, trace Best First Search using the **number of misplaced tiles** as the heuristic $h(n)$. Show OPEN and CLOSED lists at each step.

**Initial State ($S$):**

| 2 | 8 | 3 |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6 | 4 |
| 7 |   | 5 |

**Goal State ($G$):**

| 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|
| 8 |   | 4 |
| 7 | 6 | 5 |

The **blank tile** is the movable agent. Each move slides one tile into the blank.

**Heuristic:** $h(n)$ = number of tiles in the wrong position (compared to goal), **excluding the blank**.

**Initial heuristic calculation for $S$:**
- Tile 1: at position (2,1), goal position (1,1) → **misplaced** ✗
- Tile 2: at position (1,1), goal position (1,2) → **misplaced** ✗
- Tile 3: at position (1,3), goal position (1,3) → correct ✓
- Tile 4: at position (2,3), goal position (2,3) → correct ✓
- Tile 5: at position (3,3), goal position (3,3) → correct ✓
- Tile 6: at position (2,2), goal position (3,2) → **misplaced** ✗
- Tile 7: at position (3,1), goal position (3,1) → correct ✓
- Tile 8: at position (1,2), goal position (2,1) → **misplaced** ✗
- Blank: ignored by convention.

Therefore $h(S) = 4$.

---

### Iteration 1 — Expand Start Node

**Step 1.1:** OPEN = $\{(S, h=4)\}$, CLOSED = $\{\}$.

**Step 1.2:** Select $S$ (only node). Remove from OPEN. Add to CLOSED. $S$ is not goal. Expand.

**Step 1.3:** Successors of $S$ (4 possible moves of the blank):

| Successor | Move | Tiles Misplaced | $h$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ (blank Up — swap 2, blank) | | 2, 1, 6, 8 misplaced | $h(A) = 4$ |
| $B$ (blank Down — swap 5, blank) | | 2, 1, 6, 8 misplaced | $h(B) = 4$ |
| $C$ (blank Left — swap 7, blank) | | 2, 1, 6, 8 misplaced | $h(C) = 4$ |
| $D$ (blank Right — swap 6, blank) | | 2, 1, 8 misplaced | $h(D) = 3$ |

Let us verify $h(D) = 3$ carefully:

**State $D$ (slide 6 right into blank):**

| 2 | 8 | 3 |
|---|---|---|
| 1 |   | 4 |
| 7 | 6 | 5 |

- Tile 1: at (2,1), goal (1,1) → ✗
- Tile 2: at (1,1), goal (1,2) → ✗
- Tile 3: at (1,3) → ✓
- Tile 4: at (2,3) → ✓
- Tile 5: at (3,3) → ✓
- Tile 6: at (3,2), goal (3,2) → ✓
- Tile 7: at (3,1) → ✓
- Tile 8: at (1,2), goal (2,1) → ✗

Count: 1, 2, 8 are misplaced. Blank at (2,2) — closer to goal position (2,2). $h(D) = 3$. ✓

**Step 1.4:** Insert all four into OPEN, sorted by $h$ ascending.

**State after Iteration 1:**
$$\text{OPEN} = \{(D, h=3), (A, h=4), (B, h=4), (C, h=4)\}$$
$$\text{CLOSED} = \{S\}$$

---

### Iteration 2 — Expand Best Node $D$

**Step 2.1:** Pick $D$ (lowest $h = 3$). Move to CLOSED.

**Step 2.2:** Expand $D$. The blank is now at (2,2). Possible moves:

| Successor | Move | $h$ |
|---|---|---|
| $D \to A'$ (blank Up) | swap with 8 | Compute... |
| $D \to C'$ (blank Left) | swap with 1 | Compute... |
| $D \to B'$ (blank Down) | swap with 6 | Compute... |
| $D \to$ Right (back to $S$) | backtrack | Already in CLOSED, **discarded** |

**Computing $D \to A'$** (blank moves up, 8 slides down):

| 2 |   | 3 |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8 | 4 |
| 7 | 6 | 5 |

- Tile 1: ✗, Tile 2: ✗, Tile 3: ✓, Tile 4: ✓, Tile 5: ✓, Tile 6: ✓, Tile 7: ✓, Tile 8: ✗
- $h(A') = 3$

**Computing $D \to C'$** (blank moves left, 1 slides right):

| 2 | 8 | 3 |
|---|---|---|
|   | 1 | 4 |
| 7 | 6 | 5 |

- Tile 1: ✗ (at (2,2), goal (1,1)), Tile 2: ✗, Tile 3: ✓, Tile 4: ✓, Tile 5: ✓, Tile 6: ✓, Tile 7: ✓, Tile 8: ✗
- $h(C') = 3$

**Computing $D \to B'$** (blank moves down, 6 slides up):

| 2 | 8 | 3 |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6 | 4 |
| 7 |   | 5 |

- Tile 1: ✗, Tile 2: ✗, Tile 3: ✓, Tile 4: ✓, Tile 5: ✓, Tile 6: ✗ (at (2,2), goal (3,2)), Tile 7: ✓, Tile 8: ✗
- $h(B') = 3$

**Step 2.3:** Add new nodes to OPEN. Backtrack to $S$ is in CLOSED, skip.

**State after Iteration 2:**
$$\text{OPEN} = \{(A', h=3), (C', h=3), (B', h=3), (A, h=4), (B, h=4), (C, h=4)\}$$
$$\text{CLOSED} = \{S, D\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Note:** Always show OPEN as a **sorted priority queue** and explicitly write the parent pointer chain when reconstructing the path. Examiners award partial credit for clean trace tables.

---

### Termination Analysis

This exhaustive trace continues until the goal configuration is reached. The total path length, search depth, and number of nodes expanded are recorded. In KTU 14-mark questions, students are expected to:

1. Show **at least 3–4 iterations** of the trace.
2. Reconstruct the **final path** using parent pointers: $S \to D \to \dots \to G$.
3. Compute the **number of nodes generated and expanded** (this is the most common 7-mark sub-part).
4. Compare with **A\*** result (covered later in the same module).

---

## 3.2 General Complexity Derivation

Let $b$ = effective branching factor, $m$ = depth of the shallowest goal node.

**Worst-case node generation count:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{generated}} &= b + b^2 + b^3 + \dots + b^m \\[4pt]
&= b \cdot \frac{b^m - 1}{b - 1} \\[4pt]
&= O(b^m)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worst-case node expansion count:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{expanded}} &\leq T_{\text{generated}} \\[4pt]
E_{\text{expanded}} &= O(b^m)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Space complexity** matches the maximum size of OPEN, which is $O(b^m)$ because in the worst case, all generated nodes are kept in OPEN before being expanded.

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Even a "greedy" Best First Search is **exponential in space and time** in the worst case. This is why heuristic quality is paramount — a good heuristic effectively reduces $b$ to a value close to 1.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START]) --> initA["Initialize OPEN with start node S\nInitialize CLOSED as empty\nCompute h(S)"]
    initA --> checkOpen{"Is OPEN empty?"}
    checkOpen -- YES --> failA([FAILURE: No solution])
    checkOpen -- NO --> pickBest["Pick node n with minimum h(n) from OPEN\n(priority queue dequeue)"]
    pickBest --> remOpen["Remove n from OPEN\nAdd n to CLOSED"]
    remOpen --> goalCheck{"Is n the GOAL node?"}
    goalCheck -- YES --> successA([SUCCESS: Return path via parent pointers])
    goalCheck -- NO --> expandN["Expand n: generate all successors n1, n2, ..., nk\nCompute h(ni) for each successor"]
    expandN --> checkEach{"For each successor ni:\nis ni in OPEN or CLOSED?"}
    checkEach -- NOT in either --> insertOpen["Insert ni into OPEN with parent = n"]
    checkEach -- In OPEN with higher h --> updateOpen["Update ni's h in OPEN, set parent = n"]
    checkEach -- In CLOSED with higher h --> revive["Move ni from CLOSED back to OPEN, set parent = n"]
    checkEach -- Equal or lower h --> skipA["Skip ni (no improvement)"]
    insertOpen --> loopBackA
    updateOpen --> loopBackA
    revive --> loopBackA
    skipA --> loopBackA
    loopBackA([Return to OPEN check]) --> checkOpen
```

---

## 4.2 OPEN / CLOSED List Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OPEN_LIST["OPEN LIST (Frontier - Priority Queue sorted by h ascending)"]
        direction LR
        n1["Node X\nh=2\nparent=Q"] --> n2["Node Y\nh=3\nparent=P"] --> n3["Node Z\nh=5\nparent=R"] --> n4["Node W\nh=7\nparent=M"]
    end
    subgraph CLOSED_LIST["CLOSED LIST (Already Expanded)"]
        direction TB
        c1["Node S\nh=4"] --> c2["Node A\nh=4"] --> c3["Node B\nh=6"]
    end
    PICK["SELECT BEST\n(min h = 2)"] -.dequeue.-> n1
    n1 -.after expansion.-> c1
    style OPEN_LIST fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC
    style CLOSED_LIST fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#CC0000
    style PICK fill:#FFFFCC,stroke:#999900
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Comparison: BFS vs DFS vs Best First Search

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["Start Node S"]
    ROOT --> DECIDE{"Search Strategy"}
    DECIDE -- "Breadth-First\n(uninformed)" --> L1["Expand by depth level\nLayer by layer"]
    DECIDE -- "Depth-First\n(uninformed)" --> L2["Expand deepest node\nBacktrack on dead-end"]
    DECIDE -- "Best First\n(heuristic)" --> L3["Expand node with min h(n)\nGreedy toward goal"]
    L1 --> P1["Optimal + Complete\nSlow: O(b^d)"]
    L2 --> P2["Memory efficient\nNOT optimal, NOT complete"]
    L3 --> P3["Fast in practice\nNOT optimal, NOT complete"]
    style DECIDE fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656
    style P1 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366
    style P2 fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450
    style P3 fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three diagrams?** The KTU 2024 examiner pattern frequently asks: *"Draw a flowchart of Best First Search and explain OPEN/CLOSED list maintenance."* (8 marks) — these three diagrams cover that exact requirement.
