<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define an AND-OR graph. How does it differ from a standard OR-graph used in A\* search?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **AND-OR graph** is a directed graph in which each non-terminal node is either an **OR-node** (offering a choice of successors) or an **AND-node** (whose successors are grouped as a hyper-arc meaning *all* successors must be satisfied). It is used to model **problem-reduction** — a problem is solved by either choosing one of several alternatives (OR) or by decomposing it into sub-problems that must all be solved (AND).

A standard **OR-graph** (used in A*) contains only OR-nodes; the search commits to one successor at each step. AO* extends A* by accommodating AND-decompositions, making it suitable for planning, theorem proving, and game-tree search. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q2. State the cost-revision rule for an AND-node in the AO\* algorithm. Why is the cost computed as a sum (not minimum) over successors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For an AND-node $n$ with successors $n_1, \ldots, n_k$:
$$f(n) = g(n) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \big[ c(n, n_i) + h(n_i) \big]$$

The cost is a **sum** (not minimum) because, by definition of AND-decomposition, *all* sub-problems $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k$ must be solved for the parent problem $n$ to be considered solved. If any sub-problem is infeasible (i.e., $h(n_i) = \infty$), then $f(n) = \infty$. The sum reflects the cumulative effort of satisfying *every* branch. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(AO\* Trace on a given AND-OR graph)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Consider the AND-OR graph below. Each node is labelled with its heuristic $h$-value. Edge costs are 1. **A** is an OR-node, **B** is an AND-node, **C** is an OR-node, **D** is an OR-node, and **E, F, G, H** are goals ($h=0$).

- A → B (cost 1), A → C (cost 1)
- B → D (cost 1), B → E (cost 1)
- C → F (cost 1), C → G (cost 1)
- D → H (cost 1), D → F (cost 1)

Heuristics: $h(A)=4$, $h(B)=2$, $h(C)=2$, $h(D)=2$, $h(E)=h(F)=h(G)=h(H)=0$.

**Trace the AO\* algorithm** step by step. Show all forward and backward passes, and identify the **final optimal solution graph** with its cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **admissibility condition** for the heuristic function $h(n)$ in AO*. State and prove (sketch) the theorem that guarantees AO\* returns an optimal solution when $h$ is admissible. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Initialise:** $G' = \{A\}$, $f(A) = h(A) = 4$. Solution graph: $\{A\}$.

**Step 2 — Expand A (OR-node), generate B, C.**
Assign $f(B)=2$, $f(C)=2$. Backward pass at A:
$$f(A) = 0 + \min[1+2,\ 1+2] = 3$$
Choose A → B (tie). Mark A EXAMINED. Solution graph: $A \to B$.

**Step 3 — Forward pass:** B is UNEXAMINED. Expand B (AND-node), generate D, E.
Assign $f(D)=2$, $f(E)=0$. Backward pass at B (AND):
$$f(B) = 0 + (1+2) + (1+0) = 4$$
This **exceeds** $h(B)=2$, so revise $f(B)=4$. Backpropagate to A:
$$f(A) = 0 + \min[1+4,\ 1+2] = 3$$
Still A → B. Mark B EXAMINED.

**Step 4 — Expand D (OR-node), generate H, F.**
$f(H)=0$, $f(F)=0$. Backward pass at D:
$$f(D) = 0 + \min[1+0,\ 1+0] = 1$$
**B is AND-node:** revise using D's new value. New $f(D) = 1$, and E = 0, so:
$$f(B) = 0 + (1+1) + (1+0) = 3$$
Backpropagate to A:
$$f(A) = 0 + \min[1+3,\ 1+2] = 3$$
A still → B. Mark D SOLVED (its chosen successor F is a goal).

**Step 5 — Now explore alternative branch C from A.**
Forward pass: go A → C. Expand C (OR-node), generate F, G.
$f(F)=0$, $f(G)=0$. Backward pass at C:
$$f(C) = 0 + \min[1+0,\ 1+0] = 1$$
**Now backpropagate to A:**
$$f(A) = 0 + \min[1+3,\ 1+1] = 2$$
**Switch!** A's best arc now → C. Mark C SOLVED.

**Final Solution Graph:** $A \to C \to F$ (or $C \to G$, either gives cost 1).

**Optimal cost = 2.** ✓ **(7 marks — full credit if all iterations shown)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Admissibility Condition:** A heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** at node $n$ if it *never overestimates* the true optimal cost to a goal:
$$0 \leq h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall n \in N$$
where $h^*(n)$ is the cost of the cheapest solution subgraph from $n$ to any goal.

**Theorem (Nilsson 1980):** *If $h$ is admissible at every node, then AO\* terminates and returns a minimum-cost solution graph whenever a solution exists.*

**Proof Sketch (induction on depth in solution graph):**

*Base case:* $n$ is a goal $\Rightarrow f(n) = 0 = h^*(n)$. ✓

*Inductive step:* Assume $f(n_i) \leq h^*(n_i)$ for all successors $n_i$ of $n$.

- **OR-node** $n$ with best successor $n_j$:
$$f(n) = g(n) + c(n,n_j) + h(n_j) \leq g(n) + c(n,n_j) + h^*(n_j) \leq h^*(n)$$

- **AND-node** $n$ with successors $n_1,\ldots,n_k$:
$$
f(n) = g(n) + \sum_i [c(n,n_i) + h(n_i)] \leq g(n) + \sum_i [c(n,n_i) + h^*(n_i)] = h^*(n)
$$

Hence at the root $s$: $f(s) \leq h^*(s)$ = optimal cost. AO* halts at $f(s) = $ cost of the *constructed* solution graph, so this equals the optimum. $\blacksquare$

Hence AO* is **optimal** under admissibility. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Comparative + Worked Example Alternative)*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare A\* and AO\* algorithms in detail. Highlight **four major differences** in tabular form and explain the significance of the **AND-OR backpropagation** step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the AO\* algorithm to the following AND-OR graph and show all iterations. Identify the final solution graph.

- **Start:** S (OR), $h(S) = 5$
- **S → A** (cost 2), **S → B** (cost 2)
- **A** is AND with children **A1, A2** (cost 1 each); $h(A) = 3$, $h(A_1) = 2$, $h(A_2) = 2$
- **B** is OR with children **B1, B2** (cost 1 each); $h(B) = 1$, $h(B_1) = 1$, $h(B_2) = 0$
- **A1, A2, B1, B2** are goals with $h = 0$.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

| S.No. | A* Algorithm | AO* Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Searches an **OR-tree** (one path) | Searches an **AND-OR graph** (multiple paths and decompositions) |
| 2 | Combines costs with $f = g + h$ | Combines with $f = g + \min$ (OR) and $f = g + \sum$ (AND) |
| 3 | Open list contains frontier nodes | Maintains a separate **solution graph** structure |
| 4 | Single goal reachable | Requires *all* AND-successors solved for parent solved |
| 5 | Used in shortest-path / route-finding | Used in **planning, theorem proving, game trees** |
| 6 | Admissibility ⇒ optimal path | Admissibility ⇒ **optimal solution graph** |

**Significance of AND-OR backpropagation (2 marks extra credit):**
The backpropagation step ensures that **cost revisions propagate from a newly-expanded node back to ancestors along the current best solution graph**. This is what allows AO* to **switch** an OR-decision at a parent when a cheaper alternative is discovered in a sibling branch — without having to re-explore the entire tree. This is the key efficiency advantage over brute-force AND-OR search.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Iteration 0:** $f(S) = 5$. Solution graph: $\{S\}$.

**Iteration 1 — Expand S (OR):** Generate A, B. $f(A)=3$, $f(B)=1$.
$$f(S) = 0 + \min[2+3,\ 2+1] = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \text{best arc: } S \to B$$
Mark S EXAMINED.

**Iteration 2 — Forward pass: expand B (OR):** Generate B1, B2. $f(B_1)=0$, $f(B_2)=0$.
$$f(B) = 0 + \min[1+0,\ 1+0] = 1$$
Mark B SOLVED. Backpropagate to S:
$$f(S) = 0 + \min[2+3,\ 2+1] = 3$$
No change at S; S still → B. Solution graph: $S \to B \to B_1$ (or $B_2$).

**Iteration 3 — Explore alternative A to verify optimality:**
Forward pass: $S \to A$ (we already know $f(A)=3$). Expand A (AND): generate A1, A2.
$f(A_1) = 0$ (we'll use 0 since goals), $f(A_2) = 0$.

But the heuristic values given are $h(A_1)=2, h(A_2)=2$, so initially:
$f(A) = 0 + (1+2) + (1+2) = 6$? — wait, since A1 and A2 are goal nodes, $h(A_1) = h(A_2) = 0$ **after confirmation**. The given $h$ values are *initial estimates*.

Recomputing properly: After confirming A1, A2 are goals, $h(A_1) = h(A_2) = 0$. Then:
$$f(A) = 0 + (1+0) + (1+0) = 2$$
**Switch!** Backpropagate to S:
$$f(S) = 0 + \min[2+2,\ 2+1] = 3$$
S still → B (3 vs 4). **Solution graph remains** $S \to B \to \{B_1 \text{ or } B_2\}$.

**Final Solution Graph:** $S \to B \to B_1$ with cost $= 2 + 1 + 1 = 4$? Wait — recompute: $f(B) = 1$, $f(S) = 3$.

**Final solution cost = 3** (sum of all arc costs in the solution graph: $S \xrightarrow{2} B \xrightarrow{1} B_1$ gives $2+1=3$, which matches $f(S)$). ✓ **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing OR and AND rules:** Many students apply the $\min$ rule to AND-nodes or the $\sum$ rule to OR-nodes. **Memorise:** OR → min, AND → sum.
> 2. **Forgetting arc cost $c(n, n_i)$:** The cost formula is $c(n, n_i) + h(n_i)$, not $h(n_i)$ alone. Losing 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Not re-evaluating parent after expansion:** AO* is iterative — every expansion may revise ancestors. Students who compute $f$ only once lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Drawing the wrong solution graph:** Only the *minimum-cost* arcs are in the final solution graph; alternate arcs are discarded.
> 5. **Skipping the admissibility discussion:** In 14-mark questions, examiners expect 1–2 lines on admissibility + optimality theorem.
> 6. **Using `h(n) = 0` for goal nodes correctly:** $h(\text{goal}) = 0$ by definition. Students sometimes leave the heuristic value unchanged after confirming a node is a goal.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin on Wall):**

- ⭐ **AO\* = A\* on AND-OR graphs.** It is the ONLY standard algorithm to handle problem-decomposition natively.
- ⭐ **OR-node cost rule:** $f(n) = g(n) + \min_i [c(n,n_i) + h(n_i)]$. *Minimum* because we pick the best alternative.
- ⭐ **AND-node cost rule:** $f(n) = g(n) + \sum_i [c(n,n_i) + h(n_i)]$. *Sum* because all branches must be solved.
- ⭐ **Heuristic $h(n)$ must be admissible** ($h(n) \leq h^*(n)$) for AO* to return the *optimal* solution graph.
- ⭐ **Forward pass:** Descend the current best solution graph to the deepest UNEXAMINED node.
- ⭐ **Backward pass:** Recompute $f$ at the expanded node and propagate updates to ALL ancestors along the current best path; **re-evaluate OR-choices** at each ancestor.
- ⭐ **Three node statuses:** UNEXAMINED, EXAMINED, SOLVED. A node is SOLVED iff all leaves of its solution subgraph are goal nodes.
- ⭐ **Termination:** When the start node $s$ is SOLVED, output the solution graph. If $f(s) = \infty$, report "no solution."
- ⭐ **Comparison trap:** AO* is *not* just A* with AND-nodes. The backpropagation in AO* traverses a **branching solution graph**, not a single path.
- ⭐ **Complexity:** For a graph with branching factor $b$ and depth $d$, AO* is exponential in the worst case, but admissible heuristics can drastically prune the search.
- ⭐ **KTU exam weightage:** Expect a 14-mark question (full AO* trace) **OR** a 7-mark comparison + 7-mark trace split. Part A (3 marks) almost always tests the OR/AND cost rule.
- ⭐ **Key inventor:** Nilsson, in *Principles of Artificial Intelligence* (1980). Cite this in 14-mark answers for extra credit.
- ⭐ **Real-world mapping:** Planning (STRIPS), theorem proving, game trees, robot task planning — all use AO*-like reasoning under the hood.
- ⭐ **Mnemonic:** "**O**R = **O**ne is enough → min. **A**ND = **A**ll → sum."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Game Playing - Minimax Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Game Playing - Minimax Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimax Algorithm (KTU 2024 Definition):** The *Minimax* is a recursive, depth-first, backtracking adversarial search algorithm used in **two-player, zero-sum, perfect-information games**. It computes the optimal move for the player called **MAX**, under the assumption that the opponent (**MIN**) will always respond with the move that minimizes MAX's eventual payoff. The algorithm traverses the game tree down to terminal (leaf) states, applies a static **evaluation function** $E(n)$ to score each leaf, and propagates the scores back upward — MAX nodes pick the **maximum** child value, while MIN nodes pick the **minimum** child value.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Minimax algorithm is the foundational engine of **adversarial search** in classical AI, used in domains such as chess engines, tic-tac-toe solvers, and checkers. It is the theoretical ideal against which all practical game-playing heuristics (alpha-beta pruning, iterative deepening, transposition tables) are benchmarked.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (The "Chess Coach" Analogy):**
> Imagine you are playing chess against a perfectly logical opponent. Before making a move, you mentally **simulate every possible future**. You ask: *"If I move my knight here, what is the worst my opponent can do to punish me? And after that, what is the best I can do to recover?"* You keep going back and forth — alternating between *imagining your best* and *imagining your opponent's worst* — until you reach the end of the game or a position you can judge. Then you **pick the move that guarantees the best outcome even in the worst case**.
>
> That is exactly Minimax: a recursive "what-if" machine that models a rational adversary and chooses the **safest, most optimistic move under pessimistic assumptions**.

### 1.3 Important Terminology Glossary

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **MAX** | $\rightarrow$ | The player who wants to **maximize** the evaluation score (usually the AI agent) |
| **MIN** | $\rightarrow$ | The opponent, who wants to **minimize** MAX's score |
| **Game Tree** | $T$ | A directed graph of all reachable game states from the current position |
| **Terminal State** | $t \in T$ | A leaf node where the game ends (win / loss / draw) |
| **Utility / Payoff** | $u(t)$ | Numerical score of a terminal state for MAX ($+1$, $0$, $-1$ in simple games) |
| **Evaluation Function** | $E(n)$ | Heuristic score assigned to a non-terminal state $n$ |
| **Depth Limit** | $d$ | Maximum number of plies (half-moves) the search explores |
| **Static Board Evaluation** | $S(n)$ | Numerical assessment of how good a position $n$ is for MAX |
| **Ply** | — | One half-move (a single move by one player) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero-Sum Property:** The total payoff in a zero-sum game is constant. If MAX gains $x$, MIN gains $-x$. This is what allows the single-scalar evaluation to represent both players' interests.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Board Evaluation Constants

- **Simple Game Payoffs:** Win = **+1**, Draw = **0**, Loss = **−1**
- **Chess Material Values (standard heuristics):** Pawn = **1**, Knight = **3**, Bishop = **3**, Rook = **5**, Queen = **9**, King = **∞ (mate)**
- **Branching Factor ($b$):** Average number of legal moves per position (Tic-Tac-Toe ≈ 4–5, Chess ≈ 35, Go ≈ 250)
- **Search Depth ($d$):** Number of plies explored ahead

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Minimax Matters in AI:** Minimax is the *theoretical benchmark of optimal play*. While impractical for deep games (e.g., chess) due to exponential blowup, it establishes the upper bound of rational decision-making and is the parent of every practical game-search algorithm (α–β pruning, expectiminimax, Monte Carlo Tree Search).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Structured Step-by-Step

The Minimax algorithm is essentially a **recursive descent-and-ascend** process over a game tree. The operational flow is:

1. **Generate the Game Tree:** From the current state $n_0$, recursively generate all legal successor states. Each level of the tree corresponds to one ply (one player's move). MAX and MIN alternate at successive depths.
2. **Apply a Cutoff (Optional):** In real games the tree is astronomically large. The algorithm is therefore stopped at a predefined **depth limit** $d$, and a static evaluation function $E(n)$ is invoked on those frontier nodes.
3. **Evaluate Leaf / Frontier Nodes:** Each terminal or cutoff node is assigned a numerical score using the utility function $u(t)$ or evaluation function $E(n)$.
4. **Backpropagate the Values:**
   - At a **MAX level**, choose the child with the **highest** value (assuming MAX plays optimally).
   - At a **MIN level**, choose the child with the **lowest** value (assuming MIN plays optimally to hurt MAX).
5. **Select the Root Move:** At the root (current game state), MAX selects the action whose child subtree has the **maximum minimax value** — this is the optimal move under perfect rationality.

### 2.2 The Minimax Recurrence (Foundational Equation)

Let $\text{Minimax}(n, d)$ denote the value of node $n$ at depth $d$ from the current position. The recurrence is:

$$
\text{MinimaxValue}(n) =
\begin{cases}
u(n), & \text{if } n \text{ is a terminal state} \\[4pt]
E(n), & \text{if } n \text{ is a cutoff / frontier node} \\[4pt]
\displaystyle\max_{s \in \text{Successors}(n)} \text{MinimaxValue}(s), & \text{if the player at } n \text{ is MAX} \\[8pt]
\displaystyle\min_{s \in \text{Successors}(n)} \text{MinimaxValue}(s), & \text{if the player at } n \text{ is MIN}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Math:** The recursive structure mirrors the assumption of *alternating rational play*. At every node we ask: *"Given the value of every continuation, what value will the current player — who is trying to optimize for themselves — see from this position?"* The recursion bottoms out at terminal/cutoff states because only there can we assign a definite score without further speculation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimax Value of MAX node** | $V(n) = \max_{s \in \text{children}(n)} V(s)$ | Choose the **largest** child score |
| 2 | **Minimax Value of MIN node** | $V(n) = \min_{s \in \text{children}(n)} V(s)$ | Choose the **smallest** child score |
| 3 | **Zero-Sum Property** | $u_{\text{MAX}}(s) = -u_{\text{MIN}}(s)$ | One scalar represents both |
| 4 | **Terminal Utility (3-valued)** | $u \in \{+1, 0, -1\}$ | Win / Draw / Loss for MAX |
| 5 | **Tree Size (Full Expansion)** | $N = b^{d}$ | $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth |
| 6 | **Time Complexity** | $T(b,d) = O(b^{d})$ | Exponential in depth |
| 7 | **Space Complexity** | $S(b,d) = O(b \cdot d)$ | Depth-first recursion stack |
| 8 | **Static Evaluation (Chess-style)** | $E(n) = w_1 M_1 + w_2 M_2 + \dots + w_k M_k$ | Weighted sum of material / positional terms |
| 9 | **Effective Branching Factor** | $b_{\text{eff}} = \sqrt[\,d\,]{\text{Leaves explored}}$ | For pruned trees |
| 10 | **Optimal Move (Root)** | $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in A} \text{MinimaxValue}(\text{Result}(n_0, a))$ | Tie-breaking by ordering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Note:** When asked to compute Minimax values on a tree, **always** label the depth-parity (MAX/MIN) explicitly, and propagate values level-by-level from leaves to root. Marks are typically awarded for the propagated numbers at each internal node, not just the root.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Game Engines:** Foundation of chess, checkers, othello, and backgammon AI.
- **Decision Theory:** Minimax is the prototype for *robust decision-making under adversarial uncertainty* (e.g., security games, military strategy planning, poker bots).
- **Economics & Auctions:** Used in mechanism design to compute equilibrium strategies in zero-sum auctions.
- **Adversarial Machine Learning:** Defenses against evasion attacks are formulated as minimax optimization problems.
- **Robust Control:** $H_{\infty}$ control minimizes worst-case disturbance gain — directly a minimax formulation.

> [!NOTE]
> The Minimax framework essentially answers the engineering question: *"What is the best I can guarantee, even when nature/opponent is conspiring against me?"*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Complete Minimax Evaluation on a Sample Game Tree

Consider the following game tree where **MAX** plays at the root (depth 0) and **MIN** at depth 1, **MAX** at depth 2. The leaf values (terminal utilities) are given.

```
                          MAX (root)
                       /      |      \
                      a1      a2      a3
                     / \     / \     / \
                  MIN   MIN MIN  MIN MIN MIN
                  / \   / \  / \  / \  / \  / \
                 3   5 2  9  0  1  7  2  4  6  8  5
```

**Step 1 — Identify terminal utilities (depth = 3, leaves):**

| Leaf | $u$ |
|---|---|
| L1 (under a1, left) | 3 |
| L2 (under a1, right) | 5 |
| L3 (under a2, left) | 2 |
| L4 (under a2, right) | 9 |
| L5 (under a3, left) | 0 |
| L6 (under a3, right) | 1 |
| L7 (under a4, left) | 7 |
| L8 (under a4, right) | 2 |
| L9 (under a5, left) | 4 |
| L10 (under a5, right) | 6 |
| L11 (under a6, left) | 8 |
| L12 (under a6, right) | 5 |

**Step 2 — Propagate values from leaves to internal nodes (Depth 2 = MAX level).**

At depth 2, MAX picks the maximum of its two leaf children.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(\text{M2-}a_1) &= \max(3, 5) = 5 \\
V(\text{M2-}a_2) &= \max(2, 9) = 9 \\
V(\text{M2-}a_3) &= \max(0, 1) = 1 \\
V(\text{M2-}a_4) &= \max(7, 2) = 7 \\
V(\text{M2-}a_5) &= \max(4, 6) = 6 \\
V(\text{M2-}a_6) &= \max(8, 5) = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Propagate to depth 1 = MIN level.**

At depth 1, MIN picks the minimum of its two children (which are the depth-2 MAX values).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(\text{M1-}b_1) &= \min(5, 9) = 5 \\
V(\text{M1-}b_2) &= \min(1, 7) = 1 \\
V(\text{M1-}b_3) &= \min(6, 8) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Propagate to root (depth 0 = MAX level).**

At the root, MAX picks the maximum of the depth-1 MIN values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(\text{Root}) &= \max(5, 1, 6) = 6 \\
a^{*} &= \arg\max(5, 1, 6) = a_3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Under perfect play, MAX's optimal opening move is $a_3$, and the resulting game value is **+6** in MAX's favor. Even though MAX would *prefer* to reach leaf value 9, MIN will not allow it because at MIN's node the minimum is chosen.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Node-by-Node Computation

| Node | Depth | Player | Children Values | Operation | $V(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Root | 0 | MAX | 5, 1, 6 | $\max$ | **6** |
| $b_1$ | 1 | MIN | 5, 9 | $\min$ | 5 |
| $b_2$ | 1 | MIN | 1, 7 | $\min$ | 1 |
| $b_3$ | 1 | MIN | 6, 8 | $\min$ | 6 |
| $a_1$ | 2 | MAX | 3, 5 | $\max$ | 5 |
| $a_2$ | 2 | MAX | 2, 9 | $\max$ | 9 |
| $a_3$ | 2 | MAX | 0, 1 | $\max$ | 1 |
| $a_4$ | 2 | MAX | 7, 2 | $\max$ | 7 |
| $a_5$ | 2 | MAX | 4, 6 | $\max$ | 6 |
| $a_6$ | 2 | MAX | 8, 5 | $\max$ | 8 |

### 3.3 Derivation of Complexity Bounds

**Time Complexity Derivation:**

At depth $d = 0$, the root is expanded into $b$ children.
At depth $d = 1$, each of those $b$ nodes expands into $b$ more, giving $b^2$.
…
At depth $d = k$, there are $b^{k}$ nodes.

Summing over all depths:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(b, d) &= \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^{k} \\
&= \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} \quad (\text{geometric series}) \\
&\leq \frac{b^{d+1}}{b - 1} \\
&= O(b^{d}) \quad \text{as } d \to \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Space Complexity Derivation:**

The algorithm is implemented as a depth-first recursion, holding at most one path of $b$-siblings at each depth.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(b, d) &= \sum_{k=0}^{d} b \\
&= b \cdot (d+1) \\
&= O(b \cdot d)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication:** Minimax is **exponential in time** but only **linear in space** (when implemented iteratively). This space profile is what made Minimax feasible on early chess programs.

### 3.4 Limitation Analysis: Why Pure Minimax is Infeasible for Chess

For chess with $b \approx 35$ and a typical search depth of $d = 8$ plies (4 full moves):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{chess}} &\approx 35^{8} \\
&\approx 2.25 \times 10^{12} \text{ nodes}
\end{aligned}
$$

At a rate of $10^{9}$ nodes/second (modern CPU), this would take **~2250 seconds ≈ 37 minutes per move**, which is unacceptable in tournament play. This explosion motivates **alpha-beta pruning** (covered in subsequent topics).

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimal Strategy Without Pruning:** Without alpha-beta, Minimax examines the **entire** tree. This is the *theoretical optimum of decision quality* — alpha-beta only prunes subtrees that **cannot** affect the final decision.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Minimax Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Current Game State"]
    nodeB["Is this a TERMINAL node?"]
    nodeC["Return utility u of state"]
    nodeD["Is DEPTH LIMIT d reached?"]
    nodeE["Apply evaluation function E of n"]
    nodeF["Generate all legal moves successors"]
    nodeG["Is current player MAX?"]
    nodeH["Set value to -infinity and loop over successors"]
    nodeI["Recursive call: Minimax of child"]
    nodeJ["value = max of value and child result"]
    nodeK["Set value to +infinity and loop over successors"]
    nodeL["value = min of value and child result"]
    nodeM["Return value to parent caller"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC --> nodeM
    nodeB -- No --> nodeD
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE --> nodeM
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH --> nodeI --> nodeJ --> nodeH
    nodeG -- No --> nodeK --> nodeI2["Recursive call: Minimax of child"] --> nodeL --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Minimax Game-Playing Agent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["Current Board State B0"]
        A2["Player Identity MAX or MIN"]
        A3["Depth Limit d"]
    end

    subgraph B["GAME ENGINE LAYER"]
        B1["Move Generator: legal successors of B"]
        B2["State Transition Function: Result of B, a"]
        B3["Terminal Test: is game over"]
    end

    subgraph C["SEARCH LAYER"]
        C1["Minimax Recursive Search"]
        C2["Depth-First Tree Traversal"]
        C3["Backpropagation of Values"]
    end

    subgraph D["EVALUATION LAYER"]
        D1["Static Evaluation Function E of n"]
        D2["Material / Positional Heuristics"]
        D3["Terminal Utility Assignment"]
    end

    subgraph E["DECISION LAYER"]
        E1["Argmax over Root Children"]
        E2["Output Best Move a star"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> C1
    A3 --> C1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C2
    C2 --> B3
    B3 -- Game Over --> D3
    B3 -- Continue --> D1
    D3 --> C3
    D2 --> D1
    D1 --> C3
    C3 --> C1
    C1 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Value Propagation in a 2-Ply Game Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["ROOT - MAX - value to be chosen"]

    m1["MIN-1: choose min of children"]
    m2["MIN-2: choose min of children"]
    m3["MIN-3: choose min of children"]

    a1["MAX-A: choose max of children"]
    a2["MAX-B: choose max of children"]
    a3["MAX-C: choose max of children"]
    a4["MAX-D: choose max of children"]
    a5["MAX-E: choose max of children"]
    a6["MAX-F: choose max of children"]

    L1["Leaf: 3"]
    L2["Leaf: 5"]
    L3["Leaf: 2"]
    L4["Leaf: 9"]
    L5["Leaf: 0"]
    L6["Leaf: 1"]
    L7["Leaf: 7"]
    L8["Leaf: 2"]
    L9["Leaf: 4"]
    L10["Leaf: 6"]
    L11["Leaf: 8"]
    L12["Leaf: 5"]

    rootA --> m1
    rootA --> m2
    rootA --> m3

    m1 --> a1
    m1 --> a2
    m2 --> a3
    m2 --> a4
    m3 --> a5
    m3 --> a6

    a1 --> L1
    a1 --> L2
    a2 --> L3
    a2 --> L4
    a3 --> L5
    a3 --> L6
    a4 --> L7
    a4 --> L8
    a5 --> L9
    a5 --> L10
    a6 --> L11
    a6 --> L12
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The tree grows *downward* (generation) and values flow *upward* (backpropagation). At each internal node, the level's player (MAX or MIN) makes the appropriate optimization choice. The root's final value is the *guaranteed game-theoretic outcome* under perfect play from both sides.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Minimax algorithm. Why is it classified as an adversarial search technique?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Minimax** is a recursive, depth-first adversarial search algorithm used in two-player, zero-sum, perfect-information games. It determines the optimal move for the player **MAX** by exploring the game tree to a specified depth, applying a static evaluation function $E(n)$ to leaf/frontier nodes, and backpropagating values — at **MAX** nodes it picks the **maximum** child value, and at **MIN** nodes it picks the **minimum** child value. It is called *adversarial* search because it explicitly models an **opponent** (MIN) who is actively trying to *minimize* the agent's payoff, unlike uninformed/informed search where the environment is passive. The zero-sum property allows a single scalar evaluation to encode both players' interests.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for adversarial explanation, 1 for zero-sum remark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a static evaluation function in game playing? Give one example for chess.*

**Model Answer:**

A **static evaluation function** $E(n)$ is a heuristic that assigns a numerical score to a non-terminal game state $n$, estimating how favourable that position is for the MAX player *without* further lookahead. It is "static" because it depends only on the current configuration of the board (e.g., piece positions, mobility, king safety) and not on the future game tree.

**Example (Chess):**

$$
E(n) = 1 \cdot (\Delta P) + 3 \cdot (\Delta N) + 3 \cdot (\Delta B) + 5 \cdot (\Delta R) + 9 \cdot (\Delta Q)
$$

where $\Delta X$ = (number of MAX's $X$-pieces) − (number of MIN's $X$-pieces). A positive $E(n)$ favours MAX; a negative value favours MIN.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for "static" meaning, 1 for chess example with formula.)*

---

### Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**: Each Part-B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Students answer ONE of the two choices.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** For the game tree shown below, where MAX plays at the root (level 0) and players alternate, compute the **Minimax value at every internal node** and identify the **optimal first move** for MAX. Show all propagation steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

```
                              MAX (root)
                          /        |        \
                        P1         P2         P3
                       /  \       /  \       /  \
                     MIN  MIN   MIN  MIN   MIN  MIN
                     / \  / \   / \  / \   / \  / \
                    3 12  2  5  4  8  6  1  7  9  2 11
```

**(b)** Explain the **time and space complexity** of the Minimax algorithm. Why is the space complexity linear even though the algorithm explores an exponential number of nodes? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Step 1 — Identify levels and players:**
- Level 0 (root) → **MAX**
- Level 1 (P1, P2, P3) → **MIN**
- Level 2 → Terminal leaves (utility values)

**Step 2 — Propagate from leaves upward to Level 1 (MIN):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(\text{P1}) &= \min(\max(3,12),\ \max(2,5)) = \min(12,\ 5) = 5 \\
V(\text{P2}) &= \min(\max(4,8),\ \max(6,1)) = \min(8,\ 6) = 6 \\
V(\text{P3}) &= \min(\max(7,9),\ \max(2,11)) = \min(9,\ 11) = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Propagate to root (MAX):**

$$
V(\text{Root}) = \max(5,\ 6,\ 9) = 9
$$

**Step 4 — Optimal move:**

$$
a^{*} = \arg\max(5,\ 6,\ 9) = P3
$$

**Resulting game value = 9, optimal opening = P3.**

*(7 marks: 1 for level identification, 3 for MIN-level propagation, 2 for MAX propagation and argmax, 1 for the final answer.)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Time Complexity:**

The game tree has branching factor $b$ and is explored to depth $d$. Total number of nodes visited:

$$
T(b,d) = b^{0} + b^{1} + b^{2} + \dots + b^{d} = O(b^{d})
$$

This is **exponential in depth** because every node at every level is expanded. For chess with $b \approx 35$ and $d = 8$, we get $T \approx 2.25 \times 10^{12}$ nodes — a clear infeasibility.

**Space Complexity:**

The implementation uses a **depth-first recursion**:

$$
S(b,d) = O(b \cdot d)
$$

**Why linear?** At any moment the recursion stack holds only:
- The current node,
- Its siblings (at most $b$ of them per level),
- The recursion frames for each level (at most $d$ levels).

So memory scales as $b \times d$, not $b^{d}$. The exponential time cost is "forgotten" after each leaf is processed.

**Engineering Implication:** This asymmetric profile (exponential time, linear space) is what made Minimax practical on early hardware where memory was scarce but time (per move) could be spent — and it directly motivated **alpha-beta pruning** to reduce the time.

*(7 marks: 2 for time complexity derivation, 2 for space complexity derivation, 3 for the explanation of why space is linear and the engineering implication.)*

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Differentiate between the **Minimax algorithm** and **pure random/heuristic move selection** in game playing. Why does Minimax guarantee optimal play (within the search horizon) while heuristic play does not? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2-ply game tree (one MAX move, one MIN reply) with the following leaf utilities:

- If MAX plays $X$, MIN can reply with utility 4 or 7.
- If MAX plays $Y$, MIN can reply with utility 6 or 2.
- If MAX plays $Z$, MIN can reply with utility 5 or 8.

Apply Minimax and determine the optimal move and game value. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

| Aspect | Pure Heuristic Play | Minimax |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis of decision** | Single static evaluation of current state | Full lookahead of future states (to depth $d$) |
| **Opponent modelling** | Ignores opponent's counter-move | Explicitly models opponent choosing the worst reply for MAX |
| **Optimality guarantee** | Not guaranteed (greedy / myopic) | Optimal **within the search horizon** against a perfect opponent |
| **Computational cost** | $O(1)$ per move (very fast) | $O(b^{d})$ per move (exponential) |
| **Use case** | Real-time games with massive branching (Go) | Moderate-branching games (chess, checkers) |

**Why Minimax is Optimal (within horizon):**

Minimax considers **all** continuations up to depth $d$. For every move it evaluates the **worst-case** outcome (because MIN plays optimally to hurt MAX). MAX then chooses the action whose worst-case is the *best* among all worst-cases — this is the **maximin** strategy, provably optimal in zero-sum games against a rational opponent. Heuristic play, by contrast, may pick a move that *looks* good now but leaves a devastating reply one ply later, because the opponent's countermove was never simulated.

> **Caveat:** Optimality is only within the search horizon. With a finite depth $d$ and imperfect evaluation function, true global optimality is not achievable.

*(7 marks: 3 for the comparison table, 2 for opponent-modelling explanation, 2 for optimality argument.)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Step 1 — Identify structure:**

The game is 2-ply: MAX's choice at level 0, MIN's reply at level 1. Leaves are utility values.

| MAX's move | MIN's possible replies | Leaf utilities |
|---|---|---|
| $X$ | $x_1$ | 4 |
| $X$ | $x_2$ | 7 |
| $Y$ | $y_1$ | 6 |
| $Y$ | $y_2$ | 2 |
| $Z$ | $z_1$ | 5 |
| $Z$ | $z_2$ | 8 |

**Step 2 — Apply MIN at level 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(X) &= \min(4, 7) = 4 \\
V(Y) &= \min(6, 2) = 2 \\
V(Z) &= \min(5, 8) = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply MAX at root (level 0):**

$$
V(\text{Root}) = \max(4,\ 2,\ 5) = 5
$$

**Step 4 — Optimal move:**

$$
a^{*} = \arg\max(4, 2, 5) = Z
$$

**Result:** MAX's optimal opening is **Z**, and the guaranteed game value under perfect play is **5**.

*(7 marks: 2 for table setup, 2 for MIN-level computation, 2 for MAX-level argmax, 1 for final answer.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the direction of optimization at each level:** A very common error is taking the maximum at *every* node. Always re-check the level — MAX levels take max, MIN levels take min. (Lose 1–2 marks per error.)
> 2. **Forgetting to alternate MAX/MIN:** Some students treat the root alone as MAX and then propagate with the *same* rule downward. The opponent is MAXIMIZING their own payoff, which is the same as MINIMIZING yours — and that is what the $\min$ operator captures.
> 3. **Not showing intermediate values:** Examiners award partial credit for **each** internal node's value. Always write $V(\cdot) = \dots$ explicitly.
> 4. **Mis-stating the optimal move:** The optimal move is the **action** (P3, X, etc.) leading to the max, not just the value.
> 5. **Confusing Minimax with Expectimax:** In games with chance (dice, cards), the chance node uses a **weighted average**, not min/max. This is a frequent trap in questions involving backgammon.
> 6. **Forgetting zero-sum symmetry:** If asked for MIN's value, remember $u_{\text{MIN}} = -u_{\text{MAX}}$.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Minimax = depth-first adversarial search** for two-player, zero-sum, perfect-information games.
- ✅ **Recurrence rule:** MAX nodes take $\max$ of children; MIN nodes take $\min$ of children; leaves return utility $u$; cutoff nodes return $E(n)$.
- ✅ **Time complexity:** $O(b^{d})$ — exponential in depth.
- ✅ **Space complexity:** $O(b \cdot d)$ — linear, because of depth-first recursion.
- ✅ **Optimality:** Minimax is **optimal** against a perfect opponent, *within the search horizon*. True global optimality is unachievable with finite depth.
- ✅ **Zero-sum property:** $u_{\text{MAX}} = -u_{\text{MIN}}$ — one scalar suffices.
- ✅ **Evaluation function** is the heart of practical Minimax — better $E(n)$ ⇒ better play even at shallow depths.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Pure Minimax is infeasible for high-branching games (chess: $b \approx 35$, Go: $b \approx 250$) without pruning.
- ✅ **Foundation for advanced techniques:** Alpha-beta pruning, iterative deepening, transposition tables, Monte Carlo Tree Search all build upon Minimax.
- ✅ **Real-world analog:** Maximin strategy from decision theory — "best under worst-case adversary."
- ✅ **Exam mantra:** *"Alternate MAX and MIN; propagate bottom-up; root picks the max of MIN-resolved values."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIAENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Alpha Beta Pruning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alpha-Beta Pruning ($\alpha$-$\beta$ Pruning)** is an adversarial search optimization technique that dramatically reduces the number of nodes evaluated in the **Minimax** game-tree search algorithm by *pruning* (cutting off) branches that cannot possibly influence the final decision.

The two parameters, defined for any node $n$ in the search tree, are:

$$
\alpha(n) = \text{The value of the best (highest-value) choice found for MAX along the path to } n
$$

$$
\beta(n) = \text{The value of the best (lowest-value) choice found for MIN along the path to } n
$$

Formally, $\alpha$ is a **lower bound** on the final backed-up value at any MAX node, and $\beta$ is an **upper bound** on the final backed-up value at any MIN node. They are initialized as:

$$
\alpha_{root} = -\infty, \qquad \beta_{root} = +\infty
$$

A **prune** (cut-off) is triggered when:

- At a **MAX node**: $v \geq \beta \Rightarrow$ stop (Beta Cut-off).
- At a **MIN node**: $v \leq \alpha \Rightarrow$ stop (Alpha Cut-off).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** Alpha-Beta pruning is covered under "Optimal Decisions in Games — Adversarial Search". Students must know the cut-off conditions, the trace of values, and the worst-case/best-case complexities.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "Negotiating a Price"**
> Imagine you (the **Buyer**) and a Seller are haggling for a car. The Buyer is *MAX* (wants low price), the Seller is *MIN* (wants high price). The Seller is an agent of a car dealer who can offer 3 cars (each with a hidden minimum acceptable price). 
> 
> The Buyer first asks about *Car 1* and learns its price is **₹5,00,000**. The Seller says "wait, I can offer you *Car 2* which is **₹4,50,000**" — clearly better. So the Seller prunes (stops considering) *Car 3* because even if it were cheaper, the Buyer would still be negotiating against an already known better upper bound. 
> 
> The Buyer, in turn, tracks the *best* offer (α) he has seen and the *worst* offer (β) the dealer could force. The instant a branch is dominated, both parties **prune it** — that is $\alpha$-$\beta$ pruning.

## 1.3 Where It Fits in the KTU Module

- **Module 2** of PCITT602 deals with **Adversarial Search and Reasoning**.
- The hierarchy is: **Game Tree → Minimax Algorithm → Alpha-Beta Pruning → Heuristic Evaluation → Stochastic Games (Expectimax)**.
- Alpha-Beta is the *engine* of every classical board-game AI (Chess, Checkers, Othello, Tic-Tac-Toe).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Step by Step

The Minimax algorithm explores a complete game tree to depth $d$ with effective branching factor $b$. For two-player, zero-sum, perfect-information games:

1. **Initialize** $\alpha = -\infty$ and $\beta = +\infty$ at the root.
2. **Descend recursively**, propagating and updating $\alpha$ and $\beta$ as values are discovered.
3. **At a MAX node**: $v$ is the maximum of the child values so far; update $\alpha = \max(\alpha, v)$.
4. **At a MIN node**: $v$ is the minimum of the child values so far; update $\beta = \min(\beta, v)$.
5. **Test the pruning condition**:
   - At MAX: if $v \geq \beta$, *Beta Cut-off* → stop exploring siblings.
   - At MIN: if $v \leq \alpha$, *Alpha Cut-off* → stop exploring siblings.
6. **Return** the backed-up value up the tree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does pruning work?**
> Pruning never removes a node whose value could change the *root decision*, because by the time the cut-off condition is triggered, the parent node already has a *guaranteed* better alternative that the opponent would never let happen.

## 2.2 The Two Cut-offs — The Heart of KTU Questions

| Cut-off Type | Triggered at | Condition | Meaning |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **$\alpha$ Cut-off** | MIN node | $v \leq \alpha$ | MAX already has a better move higher up; MIN cannot force this branch. |
| **$\beta$ Cut-off** | MAX node | $v \geq \beta$ | MIN already has a better counter-move higher up; MAX cannot force this branch. |

Equivalently, the generic condition in most textbooks is:

$$
\text{PRUNE if } \alpha \geq \beta
$$

at *any* recursive call (after passing the updated bounds to a child).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master this table — almost every KTU 14-mark question on Adversarial Search expects at least one of these formulas.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Condition / Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Minimax complexity (no pruning) | $T = O(b^{d})$ | $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth | Pure exhaustive search. |
| 2 | $\alpha$-$\beta$ Best-case (perfect ordering) | $T_{best} = O\!\left(b^{d/2}\right)$ | $b$ even | Doubles the solvable depth. |
| 3 | $\alpha$-$\beta$ Worst-case (no reordering) | $T_{worst} = O(b^{d})$ | Same as Minimax | Always ≥ Minimax. |
| 4 | Effective branching factor (perfect) | $b_{eff} = \sqrt{b}$ | For best ordering | Doubles search depth. |
| 5 | Alpha initial value | $\alpha_{0} = -\infty$ | Root node | Standard. |
| 6 | Beta initial value | $\beta_{0} = +\infty$ | Root node | Standard. |
| 7 | Generic prune test | $\alpha \geq \beta$ | Any node | Either cut-off. |
| 8 | $\alpha$ update (MAX node) | $\alpha = \max(\alpha, v)$ | After each child | Lower bound moves up. |
| 9 | $\beta$ update (MIN node) | $\beta = \min(\beta, v)$ | After each child | Upper bound moves down. |
| 10 | Move ordering heuristic gain | Effective depth = $2 \times d$ | With perfect ordering | Industry rule of thumb. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use the vertical pipe `\vert x\vert` inside KTU table cells** — it breaks the markdown table. Use `\vert x\vert` typeset in LaTeX as $\vert x \vert$ or simply describe the absolute value in words.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Game-playing engines**: Stockfish (Chess), AlphaZero's classical precursor searchers, and checkers engines use $\alpha$-$\beta$ as the core search primitive.
- **Planning under uncertainty**: Adversarial planning in autonomous driving (worst-case pedestrian behaviour) and military simulations.
- **Decision theory**: Negotiation bots, automated trading with adversarial counterparties.
- **Resource allocation**: Competitive scheduling where one process's best-case must be balanced against another's worst-case.

The **efficiency gain** is so significant that the same hardware running $\alpha$-$\beta$ can search approximately *twice as deep* as raw Minimax, which in turn doubles playing strength.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Game Tree for Trace

The following is a **standard 2-ply, 8-leaf** tree that KTU examiners love. Terminal leaves (utility values) are given at the bottom. We will perform the full $\alpha$-$\beta$ trace.

| Level | Node | Type | Children Values (left → right) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | **A** | MAX | B, C |
| 1 | **B** | MIN | D, E |
| 1 | **C** | MIN | F, G |
| 2 | **D** | MAX | 3, 5 |
| 2 | **E** | MAX | 6, 9 |
| 2 | **F** | MAX | 1, 2 |
| 2 | **G** | MAX | 0, −1 |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Recursive Trace

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every transition below shows **which child is being visited, the value returned, the updated $(\alpha, \beta)$ at that node, and the precise prune condition**. Read sequentially.

### Step 1 — Initial Call
At root **A (MAX)** with $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$.

$$
v_A = \max(v_B, v_C) \quad \text{with } (\alpha, \beta) = (-\infty, +\infty)
$$

### Step 2 — Visit B (MIN) first
Recursive call to B with bounds $(\alpha, \beta) = (-\infty, +\infty)$:

$$
v_B = \min(v_D, v_E) \quad \text{with } (\alpha, \beta) = (-\infty, +\infty)
$$

### Step 3 — Visit D (MAX), first child of B
Call D with $(\alpha, \beta) = (-\infty, +\infty)$:

$$
v_D = \max(3, 5) = 5
$$

After exploring child "3" of D:
- $v = 3$. At a MAX node, $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 3) = 3$. Prune test: $\alpha \geq \beta \Rightarrow 3 \geq +\infty$? **No**, continue.
- Explore child "5" of D: $v = 5$. $\alpha = \max(3, 5) = 5$. Prune test: $5 \geq +\infty$? **No**, continue.
- D returns **5**.

### Step 4 — Return to B
- $v = v_D = 5$. At MIN node B, $\beta = \min(+\infty, 5) = 5$.
- Updated bounds at B: $(\alpha, \beta) = (-\infty, 5)$.

### Step 5 — Visit E (MAX), second child of B
Call E with $(\alpha, \beta) = (-\infty, 5)$:

- Explore "6": $v = 6$. At MAX, $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 6) = 6$. 
- Prune test (at E): $\alpha \geq \beta \Rightarrow 6 \geq 5$? **YES — BETA CUT-OFF!**
- "9" is **never explored**.

$$
v_E = 6 \quad (\text{partial — but irrelevant, B will use min later})
$$

### Step 6 — Back to B, B returns
$$
v_B = \min(v_D, v_E) = \min(5, 6) = 5
$$

### Step 7 — Back to A, A updates
At MAX node A: $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 5) = 5$. 
- Updated bounds at A: $(\alpha, \beta) = (5, +\infty)$.
- A continues to second child C.

### Step 8 — Visit C (MIN)
Call C with $(\alpha, \beta) = (5, +\infty)$:

$$
v_C = \min(v_F, v_G)
$$

### Step 9 — Visit F (MAX), first child of C
Call F with $(\alpha, \beta) = (5, +\infty)$:

- Explore "1": $v = 1$. At MAX, $\alpha = \max(5, 1) = 5$. 
- Prune test (at F): $\alpha \geq \beta \Rightarrow 5 \geq +\infty$? **No**, continue.
- Now check cut-off alternative form: $v \leq \alpha \Rightarrow 1 \leq 5$? **YES — ALPHA CUT-OFF!**
- "2" is **never explored**.

$$
v_F = 1 \quad (\text{partial value, but already dominant for MIN})
$$

### Step 10 — Return to C
At MIN node C: $\beta = \min(+\infty, 1) = 1$.
- Updated bounds at C: $(\alpha, \beta) = (5, 1)$.
- Prune test at C: $\alpha \geq \beta \Rightarrow 5 \geq 1$? **YES — entire subtree of G is pruned without inspection.**

### Step 11 — C returns
$$
v_C = \min(v_F, v_G) = \min(1, 1) = 1
$$

### Step 12 — Final answer at A
$$
v_A = \max(v_B, v_C) = \max(5, 1) = \boxed{5}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Optimal Move at A is to go to B**, because B is the child that returns the higher backed-up utility of 5.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Leaves Pruned in This Example

| Subtree | Leaves Explored | Leaves Pruned | Prune Type | Trigger |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| D | 2 of 2 (3, 5) | 0 | — | — |
| E | 1 of 2 (6) | 1 (the "9") | $\beta$ Cut-off at E | $\alpha=6 \geq \beta=5$ |
| F | 1 of 2 (1) | 1 (the "2") | $\alpha$ Cut-off at F | $v=1 \leq \alpha=5$ |
| G | 0 of 2 | 2 (both) | $\alpha \geq \beta$ at C | $5 \geq 1$ |
| **Total** | **4 of 8** | **4 of 8 (50%)** | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> With this particular tree and ordering, exactly **50 % of leaves are pruned** — the canonical figure cited in KTU answers.

## 3.4 Complexity Derivation (Best Case)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is **frequently asked as a 3-mark sub-question** in KTU Module 2.

Assume perfect move ordering — best child is always explored first.

**Setup**: For a tree of depth $d$ and branching factor $b$, only **half** the children at each MAX level need to be examined in full, because the first child already establishes a tight bound that prunes the rest. A standard inductive argument gives:

$$
T(d) = b \cdot T(d-2) \quad \text{(best case)}
$$

For $b$ even, solving the recurrence:

$$
T(d) = b \cdot b \cdot T(d-4) = b^{k} \cdot T(d-2k)
$$

Let $k = d/2$ (assume $d$ even):

$$
T(d) = b^{d/2} \cdot T(0)
$$

Since $T(0) = O(1)$:

$$
\boxed{T_{best}(d) = O\!\left(b^{d/2}\right)}
$$

Equivalently, with depth $d' = 2d$ the algorithm examines as many nodes as Minimax with depth $d$ — i.e., the search **doubles in effective depth** without any extra computation cost.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram describes the **functional architecture** of the recursive $\alpha$-$\beta$ procedure, including all decision gates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Call ALPHABETA node A depth d alpha negInf beta posInf] --> isMaxA{Is node MAX type}
    isMaxA -- yes --> initVmaxA[v = negInf] --> loopA[For each child c in successors]
    loopA --> recurseA[Call ALPHABETA c depth minus 1 alpha beta]
    recurseA --> updatemaxA[v = max of v and returned value]
    updatemaxA --> updateAlphaA[alpha = max of alpha and v]
    updateAlphaA --> cutoffCheckA{alpha greater than or equal to beta}
    cutoffCheckA -- yes --> betaCutoffA[Return v BETA CUTOFF]
    cutoffCheckA -- no --> moreA{More children}
    moreA -- yes --> loopA
    moreA -- no --> returnA[Return v]
    isMaxA -- no --> initVminA[v = posInf] --> loopB[For each child c in successors]
    loopB --> recurseB[Call ALPHABETA c depth minus 1 alpha beta]
    recurseB --> updateminB[v = min of v and returned value]
    updateminB --> updateBetaB[beta = min of beta and v]
    updateBetaB --> cutoffCheckB{alpha greater than or equal to beta}
    cutoffCheckB -- yes --> alphaCutoffB[Return v ALPHA CUTOFF]
    cutoffCheckB -- no --> moreB{More children}
    moreB -- yes --> loopB
    moreB -- no --> returnB[Return v]
```

## 4.2 Game-Tree Trace (Mermaid Tree with Annotated Cuts)

The same tree from §3.1, with each node tagged by the value returned after $\alpha$-$\beta$ pruning. Pruned edges are marked clearly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["A MAX value 5"]
    nodeB["B MIN value 5"]
    nodeC["C MIN value 1"]
    nodeD["D MAX value 5"]
    nodeE["E MAX partial 6"]
    nodeF["F MAX partial 1"]
    nodeG["G PRUNED"]

    leafD1["Leaf 3"]
    leafD2["Leaf 5"]
    leafE1["Leaf 6"]
    leafE2["Leaf 9 PRUNED"]
    leafF1["Leaf 1"]
    leafF2["Leaf 2 PRUNED"]
    leafG1["Leaf 0 PRUNED"]
    leafG2["Leaf neg1 PRUNED"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC -.->|alpha greater than or equal to beta prune| nodeG

    nodeD --> leafD1
    nodeD --> leafD2
    nodeE --> leafE1
    nodeE -.->|beta cut at alpha 6 greater than or equal to beta 5 prune| leafE2
    nodeF --> leafF1
    nodeF -.->|alpha cut v 1 less than or equal to alpha 5 prune| leafF2
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — How $\alpha$ and $\beta$ "Close In"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    left["alpha lower bound MAX guarantee"]
    mid["Actual minimax value window alpha to beta"]
    right["beta upper bound MIN guarantee"]
    left --> mid --> right
    note["When alpha meets beta or crosses it, the branch is PRUNED"]
    mid -.-> note
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Alpha-Beta pruning. What is the role of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in the algorithm?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> *Definition (1 mark):* Alpha-Beta pruning is an optimization of the Minimax algorithm that eliminates branches in a game tree which cannot influence the final decision, by maintaining two threshold values.
> 
> *Role of $\alpha$ (1 mark):* $\alpha$ is the value of the best move that **MAX** can guarantee so far along the current path; it acts as a **lower bound** on the value of any MAX node.
> 
> *Role of $\beta$ (1 mark):* $\beta$ is the value of the best move that **MIN** can guarantee so far; it acts as an **upper bound** on the value of any MIN node. A node is pruned when $\alpha \geq \beta$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
**State the two conditions under which Alpha-Beta pruning is performed. Briefly explain its worst-case and best-case time complexity.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> *Condition 1 — $\alpha$ Cut-off (1 mark):* At a MIN node, prune the remaining children if the value returned ($v$) satisfies $v \leq \alpha$.
> 
> *Condition 2 — $\beta$ Cut-off (1 mark):* At a MAX node, prune the remaining children if $v \geq \beta$.
> 
> *Complexity (1 mark):* Worst-case (no ordering improvement) is $O(b^d)$, identical to Minimax. Best-case (perfect move ordering) is $O(b^{d/2})$, doubling the search depth.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - **Always label** the current $\alpha$ and $\beta$ at **each node**, not only at the cut-off.
> - **Draw an X** on pruned branches, *and* state the **prune condition** (e.g., "$v=1 \leq \alpha=5$").
> - Do **not** forget to write the final **optimal move** at the root.
> - **Show each recursive call** — partial marks are awarded per step.
> - In the complexity sub-question, do not mix up $O(b^{d/2})$ (best) with $O(b^d)$ (worst).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Apply the $\alpha$-$\beta$ pruning algorithm to the following game tree. Show the values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ at every node, mark the branches that are pruned with the reason, and state the optimal move at the root with its value.

Game tree (MAX at root, alternating MIN and MAX, leaves shown):

| Level | Node | Type | Leaf values (left → right) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | A | MAX | — |
| 1 | B | MIN | — |
| 1 | C | MIN | — |
| 2 | D | MAX | 4, 8 |
| 2 | E | MAX | 5, 6 |
| 2 | F | MAX | 7, 3 |
| 2 | G | MAX | 2, 9 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to the $\alpha$-$\beta$ algorithm, derive the best-case time complexity $O(b^{d/2})$ and explain why good move ordering is essential in practice.

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1.** At A: $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$. Visit B first.

**Step 2.** At B (MIN): $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$. Visit D.

**Step 3.** At D (MAX): explore leaves.
- 4 → $v=4$, $\alpha=4$. No prune.
- 8 → $v=8$, $\alpha=8$. No prune.
- $v_D = 8$. B: $\beta = \min(+\infty, 8) = 8$. Bounds at B: $(-\infty, 8)$.

**Step 4.** At B, visit E with $(\alpha, \beta) = (-\infty, 8)$.
- 5 → $v=5$, $\alpha=5$. Prune test: $\alpha \geq \beta \Rightarrow 5 \geq 8$? No.
- 6 → $v=6$, $\alpha=6$. Prune test: $6 \geq 8$? No.
- $v_E = 6$. B: $\beta = \min(8, 6) = 6$. $v_B = \min(8, 6) = 6$.

**Step 5.** Back to A: $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 6) = 6$. Bounds at A: $(6, +\infty)$.

**Step 6.** At A, visit C with $(\alpha, \beta) = (6, +\infty)$. C (MIN) visits F.
- At F (MAX): leaf 7 → $v=7$, $\alpha=7$. Prune test: $7 \geq +\infty$? No. But **alternative check** $v \leq \alpha_{C} = 6$? $7 \leq 6$? No.
- Leaf 3 → $v=3$, $\alpha=7$ (no change). $v_F = 7$. C: $\beta = \min(+\infty, 7) = 7$.

**Step 7.** At C, visit G with $(\alpha, \beta) = (6, 7)$.
- Leaf 2 → $v=2$. At MAX, $\alpha = 7$. **Prune test**: $\alpha \geq \beta \Rightarrow 7 \geq 7$? **YES — $\beta$ CUT-OFF.**
- Leaf 9 not explored.
- $v_G = 2$. C: $\beta = \min(7, 2) = 2$. $v_C = \min(7, 2) = 2$.

**Step 8.** Final value at A: $v_A = \max(6, 2) = \mathbf{6}$.

**Optimal Move at A → B (left branch).**

> **Pruned branches summary (2 marks):**
> - Leaf 9 (right child of G): pruned at G via $\beta$ cut-off (condition $7 \geq 7$).

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]

**Setup (2 marks):** With perfect move ordering, at any MAX node the **first** child examined is already the optimal one and the other children can be pruned after a *single leaf* evaluation. Hence, the effective number of children examined at a MAX node drops from $b$ to roughly **1** (with the partner MIN layer examining $b$).

**Recurrence (3 marks):**

$$
T_{\text{best}}(d) = b \cdot T_{\text{best}}(d - 2) + O(1)
$$

Solving by unrolling $d/2$ times:

$$
T_{\text{best}}(d) = b^{d/2} \cdot T_{\text{best}}(0) = O\!\left(b^{d/2}\right)
$$

**Significance of move ordering (2 marks):** Good ordering (e.g., best-first via transposition tables, killer-move heuristics, or iterative deepening) reduces the effective branching factor from $b$ to $\sqrt{b}$. Practically, this allows the same time budget to search **twice as deep**, gaining roughly *several hundred Elo points* in competitive chess engines. Without ordering, $\alpha$-$\beta$ degrades back to plain Minimax at $O(b^d)$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the game tree below. Apply the $\alpha$-$\beta$ pruning algorithm from left to right. List all pruned nodes, the type of cut-off in each case, and compute the final minimax value at the root.

Game tree (MAX at root, alternating, leaves left → right):

| Level | Node | Type | Leaf values |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | A | MAX | — |
| 1 | B | MIN | — |
| 1 | C | MIN | — |
| 2 | D | MAX | 6, 4 |
| 2 | E | MAX | 7, 1 |
| 2 | F | MAX | 5, 8 |
| 2 | G | MAX | 2, 3 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Distinguish between **Alpha cut-off** and **Beta cut-off** with one example each. Also explain, with a labelled diagram, why $\alpha$ is a lower bound and $\beta$ is an upper bound in the search.

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]

**Trace (5 marks):**

- A: $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$. Visit B.
- B (MIN): $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$. Visit D.
- D (MAX): 6 → $\alpha=6$. 4 → $\alpha=6$. $v_D=6$. B: $\beta = \min(+\infty, 6) = 6$.
- B visits E with $(\alpha, \beta) = (-\infty, 6)$.
- E (MAX): 7 → $\alpha=7$. Test $7 \geq 6$? **YES — $\beta$ CUT-OFF at E.** Leaf 1 is **not explored**.
- $v_E = 7$. B: $\beta = \min(6, 7) = 6$. $v_B = \min(6, 7) = 6$.
- A: $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 6) = 6$.
- A visits C with $(\alpha, \beta) = (6, +\infty)$.
- C (MIN) visits F with $(6, +\infty)$.
- F (MAX): 5 → $\alpha=6$. Test $v \leq \alpha_C = 6$? $5 \leq 6$? **YES — $\alpha$ CUT-OFF at F.** Leaf 8 not explored.
- $v_F = 5$. C: $\beta = \min(+\infty, 5) = 5$.
- C visits G with $(\alpha, \beta) = (6, 5)$. Test $6 \geq 5$? **YES — entire G pruned (no inspection of leaves 2 or 3).**
- $v_G = 5$. $v_C = \min(5, 5) = 5$.
- A: $v_A = \max(6, 5) = \mathbf{6}$.

**Pruned leaves (1 mark):** 1 (at E — $\beta$ cut-off), 8 (at F — $\alpha$ cut-off), 2 and 3 (at G — generic $\alpha \geq \beta$).

**Optimal move (1 mark):** A → B (left branch), value = 6.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]

**Distinction (4 marks):**

| Feature | $\alpha$ Cut-off | $\beta$ Cut-off |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Where? | At a **MIN** node | At a **MAX** node |
| Trigger | $v \leq \alpha$ | $v \geq \beta$ |
| Reason | MAX already has a strictly better alternative above | MIN already has a strictly better counter-move above |
| Effect | Skip remaining children of the MIN node | Skip remaining children of the MAX node |

**Example — $\alpha$ Cut-off (1.5 marks):** In the trace above, at F the leaf value 5 was ≤ $\alpha_C = 6$, so the second leaf (8) was never examined — a classic $\alpha$ cut-off.

**Example — $\beta$ Cut-off (1.5 marks):** At E, the leaf value 7 was ≥ $\beta_B = 6$, so the second leaf (1) was never examined — a classic $\beta$ cut-off.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Alpha-Beta Pruning)**

- **Core idea:** Eliminate branches of a Minimax tree that cannot affect the root decision.
- **Initialization:** $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$ at the root.
- **Updates:** $\alpha \leftarrow \max(\alpha, v)$ at MAX; $\beta \leftarrow \min(\beta, v)$ at MIN.
- **Prune test:** Stop exploring a node if $\alpha \geq \beta$.
- **Equivalent forms:** $v \geq \beta$ at MAX ($\beta$ cut-off) OR $v \leq \alpha$ at MIN ($\alpha$ cut-off).
- **Order of evaluation matters:** Left-to-right is fine, but *best-first* gives $O(b^{d/2})$.
- **Worst case:** No improvement over Minimax, $O(b^d)$.
- **Best case:** Doubles effective search depth (equivalent to $b \rightarrow \sqrt{b}$).
- **Optimal move:** Always the child of the root that returns the highest backed-up value.
- **Does pruning change the final answer?** No — the root decision is identical to plain Minimax.
- **Common exam trap:** Confusing $\alpha$-cut-off with $\beta$-cut-off location. Remember: $\alpha$ is for MAX, $\beta$ is for MIN? **No!** $\alpha$ protects MAX from MIN, so the **cut-off happens at MIN**. Vice-versa for $\beta$.
- **Engineering use:** Stockfish, GNUChess, Checkers engines, classical game AI modules.
- **Formula to memorize verbatim:** $O(b^{d/2})$ best-case, $O(b^d)$ worst-case.
- **Sanity check while tracing:** If total leaves pruned = total leaves − explored leaves, your trace is consistent only if both add up to the original count.
- **Don't forget to mark pruned subtrees with a clear "PRUNED" label and a reason — KTU award 1 mark for that alone.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constraint Satisfaction Problem - Backtracking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constraint Satisfaction Problem - Backtracking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)

A **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)** is a formal mathematical framework used in Artificial Intelligence to represent and solve problems where the objective is to find a state (or set of values) that satisfies a collection of **constraints**. Formally, a CSP is defined as a triple:

$$
\mathcal{P} \;=\; \langle \mathbf{X}, \mathbf{D}, \mathbf{C} \rangle
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{X} = \{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$ is a finite set of **variables**.
- $\mathbf{D} = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$ is a corresponding set of **domains**, where $D_i$ is the finite set of allowable values for variable $X_i$.
- $\mathbf{C} = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$ is a set of **constraints** that restrict the values the variables may take simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (High-Weight):** A *solution* (or *consistent assignment*) to a CSP is an assignment $\{X_1 = v_1, X_2 = v_2, \ldots, X_n = v_n\}$ such that every $v_i \in D_i$ and **all** constraints in $\mathbf{C}$ are simultaneously satisfied. The empty assignment is trivially consistent.

### 1.2 Classification of Constraints by Arity

| Arity | Name | Example |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Unary** | Involves a single variable | $X_1 \neq \text{red}$ |
| **Binary** | Involves a pair of variables | $X_1 \neq X_2$ |
| **Higher-order** | Involves $\geq 3$ variables | Cryptarithmetic column sums |
| **Global** | Defines a global pattern | AllDifferent$(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ |

### 1.3 Backtracking — Formal Definition

**Backtracking** is a *depth-first* search algorithm augmented with a *constraint-propagation* mechanism, used to systematically explore the space of partial variable assignments for a CSP. It incrementally extends a partial assignment by assigning values to one variable at a time, and **undoes** (backtracks from) an assignment the moment it is discovered to be inconsistent with any constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Backtracking search in CSPs has three orthogonal dimensions of improvement — (i) *variable ordering*, (ii) *value ordering*, and (iii) *inference / propagation*. KTU 2024 questions typically test at least one of these dimensions.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are solving a **Sudoku puzzle** in a coffee shop. You fill in the top-left cell with a number, then move to the next empty cell, and so on. The moment you realise that a number you just wrote **breaks a rule** (e.g., a duplicate in a row), you don't throw the whole puzzle away — you **erase** the conflicting entry and try the next candidate. If no candidate works, you **step back** to the previous cell and change it.

That stepping-back is **backtracking**. The puzzle's row/column/box rules are your **constraints**. The grid cells are your **variables**, each with the domain $\{1,2,\ldots,9\}$. The final solved grid is your **consistent complete assignment**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world CSP examples KTU examiners love:** Map colouring, N-Queens, Sudoku, scheduling/timetabling, graph colouring, warehouse layout, VLSI channel routing, and the 8-puzzle.

### 1.5 Why CSPs Deserve Their Own Framework

Unlike general state-space search, CSPs exploit a critical structural property: **commutativity** — the order in which we assign variables does not matter, only the final set of assignments. This allows the use of generic, domain-independent heuristics (MRV, LCV, degree, forward checking, arc consistency) without any problem-specific knowledge.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of CSP Representation

A CSP is not just a list of equations — it is a **graph-theoretic object**. The **constraint graph** $G = (V, E)$ has:

- A node $v_i \in V$ for every variable $X_i$.
- An edge $(X_i, X_j) \in E$ for every binary constraint $C_k$ that involves both $X_i$ and $X_j$.

For *higher-order* constraints, a **hypergraph** generalisation is used (every constraint becomes a hyper-edge linking all involved variables).

### 2.2 The Standard Backtracking Algorithm — Operational Breakdown

The backtracking search operates as a recursive, depth-first enumeration over variable assignments. The base logic is:

1. **Termination Check:** If every variable in $\mathbf{X}$ is assigned, return the current consistent assignment as a solution.
2. **Variable Selection:** Pick an *unassigned* variable $X_i$ (using either a fixed order or an informed heuristic).
3. **Value Ordering:** Iterate over the values $v \in D_i$ in some chosen order (natural, LCV, etc.).
4. **Constraint Test:** For each candidate value $v$, check whether assigning $X_i = v$ violates any constraint with already-assigned variables.
5. **Recursive Descent:** If consistent, *recursively* call backtrack with $X_i = v$ added to the partial assignment.
6. **Backtrack (Undo):** If the recursive call fails to produce a solution, *undo* the assignment (remove $X_i = v$) and try the next value in $D_i$.
7. **Failure Return:** If all values of $D_i$ are exhausted, return **failure** to the caller.

### 2.3 Heuristic Enhancements (KTU Frequently Asked)

#### 2.3.1 Variable-Ordering Heuristics

- **MRV — Minimum Remaining Values** ("fail-first" heuristic): pick the variable with the **fewest legal values** in its current domain. Rationale: if this variable is doomed to fail, discover it *as early as possible* to prune the search tree aggressively.
- **Degree Heuristic:** break MRV ties by picking the variable that participates in the **largest number of constraints** with unassigned variables. Reduces branching on future steps.

#### 2.3.2 Value-Ordering Heuristics

- **LCV — Least Constraining Value:** prefer the value that **rules out the fewest choices** for the neighbouring variables. Rationale: keep maximum flexibility for the rest of the search.

#### 2.3.3 Inference Heuristics

- **Forward Checking:** Whenever $X_i$ is assigned, delete from the domains of every unassigned neighbour any value that becomes inconsistent with $X_i = v$. If any neighbour's domain becomes empty, the branch is killed immediately.
- **Arc Consistency (AC-3):** A stronger form of propagation that iteratively enforces the property: for every arc $(X_i, X_j)$, for every $v_i \in D_i$ there exists some $v_j \in D_j$ such that the constraint between $X_i$ and $X_j$ is satisfied. Detects failures **earlier** than forward checking.
- **MAC (Maintaining Arc Consistency):** AC-3 applied *after every assignment* — the strongest practical inference for general CSPs.
- **Constraint Propagation** = the generic umbrella term for all such domain-reduction techniques.

### 2.4 Intelligent Backjumping

Standard backtracking *chronologically* returns to the most recent variable. **Backjumping** returns to the **most recent variable among those responsible for the failure**, identified by computing a *conflict set*. Saves enormous work in tightly-constrained problems.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Complexity Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Bound | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | CSP triple | $\mathcal{P} = \langle \mathbf{X}, \mathbf{D}, \mathbf{C} \rangle$ | Foundational definition |
| 2 | Total number of arcs in a binary CSP | $m \leq \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Upper bound for fully-connected graph |
| 3 | Brute-force search size (no inference) | $d^n$ | $d =$ domain size, $n =$ variables |
| 4 | Backtracking worst-case time | $O(d^n)$ | Exponential; no inference |
| 5 | Tree-structured CSP | $O(n \, d^2)$ | Solvable in **linear time in $n$** |
| 6 | Nearly-tree-structured CSP | $O(n \, d^2)$ per cycle cutset branch | Cycle cutset size $c \Rightarrow O(d^c \cdot n d^2)$ |
| 7 | AC-3 time complexity | $O(n^2 \, d^3)$ | $n^2$ arcs, $O(d^2)$ work per arc check |
| 8 | AC-3 space complexity | $O(n^2 + n \cdot d)$ | Queue of arcs + domain storage |
| 9 | Forward checking cost per branch | $O(n \cdot d)$ | Check all unassigned neighbours |
| 10 | Domain-size after one forward-check step | $D_j' = D_j \setminus \{v \in D_j \mid \neg \text{consistent}(v_i, v)\}$ | Removes inconsistent neighbour values |
| 11 | MRV selection criterion | $\arg\min_{X_i \text{ unassigned}} \;\vert D_i \vert$ | Tie-break with degree heuristic |
| 12 | Degree heuristic tie-break | $\arg\max_{X_i \text{ unassigned}} \;\deg(X_i)$ | $\deg(X_i) = $ # of unassigned neighbours |
| 13 | Number of leaves pruned by inference | up to $d^n - O(n \, d^2)$ | Empirical — order-of-magnitude reduction |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorisation tip for KTU 2024 ESE:** The single most-tested complexity bound is **$O(n \, d^2)$ for tree-structured CSPs**. Always derive it on the side: a tree has $n-1$ edges, each edge is processed once with $d^2$ work, total $O(n d^2)$.

### 2.6 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

| Engineering Field | CSP Application |
|:---|:---|
| **VLSI Chip Design** | Channel routing, layer assignment, wire-length minimisation |
| **Operations Research** | Job-shop scheduling, vehicle routing, crew rostering |
| **Bioinformatics** | DNA fragment assembly, haplotype inference |
| **Computer Vision** | Stereo matching, line-labelling (Waltz algorithm) |
| **Robotics / Planning** | Path planning with kinematic constraints, assembly sequencing |
| **Cryptography** | Constraint-based attacks on substitution ciphers |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Brute-Force Search Tree Size is $d^n$

> **Question:** *Why does the backtracking search tree contain at most $d^n$ leaves?*

Consider a CSP with $n$ variables, each with domain size $d$ (worst-case uniform). A complete assignment specifies one value for each of the $n$ variables.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of complete assignments} &= \underbrace{d}_{\text{choices for }X_1} \times \underbrace{d}_{\text{choices for }X_2} \times \cdots \times \underbrace{d}_{\text{choices for }X_n} \\[4pt]
&= d \cdot d \cdot \ldots \cdot d \quad (n \text{ times}) \\[4pt]
&= d^{\,n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus the search tree has at most $d^n$ leaves. Internal nodes are at most $1 + d + d^2 + \ldots + d^n = \dfrac{d^{\,n+1} - 1}{d - 1}$, so the total work is $O(d^n)$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tree-Structured CSP Solves in $O(n d^2)$

> **Question:** *Prove that a CSP whose constraint graph is a tree is solvable in $O(n \, d^2)$ time.*

**Step 1 — Topological assumption.** Let the variables of a tree-structured CSP be numbered $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ in any *topological order* — for each edge $(X_i, X_j)$ with $i < j$, parent $X_i$ is closer to the root.

**Step 2 — Single-edge bound.** Consider a single edge $(X_i, X_j)$ where $X_i$ is already assigned. We must remove from $D_j$ all values $v_j$ such that no value of $X_i$ is consistent with $v_j$. The cost of testing all $d$ values of $D_i$ against a single value $v_j$ is $O(d)$. Iterating over all $d$ candidates in $D_j$ costs $O(d^2)$.

**Step 3 — Tree-edge count.** A tree on $n$ nodes contains exactly $n - 1$ edges.

**Step 4 — Total cost.** Sum the per-edge cost over the entire tree:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= (n - 1) \times O(d^2) + n \times O(d) \\[4pt]
&= O(n \, d^2) + O(n \, d) \\[4pt]
&= O(n \, d^2) \quad \text{(the } n d^2 \text{ term dominates for } d \geq 2\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The extra $O(n d)$ term comes from the final pass that selects a value for each parent. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU 2024 favourite):** This is the single most important *linear-time* result in constraint reasoning — once you eliminate cycles from the constraint graph, the problem becomes tractable. The *cycle cutset* and *tree decomposition* techniques generalise this idea.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — AC-3 Time Complexity $O(n^2 d^3)$

> **Question:** *Show that the AC-3 algorithm runs in $O(n^2 d^3)$.*

**Step 1 — Queue size.** The initial queue contains all directed arcs of the binary CSP, at most $2 \cdot \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = n(n-1) = O(n^2)$ arcs.

**Step 2 — Arc processing cost.** To process one arc $(X_i, X_j)$:

- Iterate over every $v_i \in D_i$ — at most $d$ values.
- For each $v_i$, iterate over $D_j$ to find at least one supporting $v_j$ — at most $d$ values.
- Hence the per-arc cost is $O(d^2)$.

**Step 3 — Re-queue count.** Each time $D_i$ is reduced, every *incoming* arc $(X_k, X_i)$ is re-inserted. $D_i$ can be reduced at most $d$ times (each removal is permanent). So arc $(X_i, X_j)$ enters the queue at most $d$ times.

**Step 4 — Total work.** Number of arc insertions $\leq O(n^2 d)$. Each insertion costs $O(d^2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{AC-3}} &= \underbrace{O(n^2 d)}_{\text{total arc insertions}} \times \underbrace{O(d^2)}_{\text{work per insertion}} \\[4pt]
&= O(n^2 \, d^3) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Heuristic vs. Cost vs. Benefit

| Heuristic | Extra Cost per Step | Branching Reduction | KTU Weight |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| MRV (variable) | $O(n)$ to scan domains | High (early failure detection) | High |
| Degree (variable tie-break) | $O(n)$ to compute degrees | Moderate | Medium |
| LCV (value) | $O(n \, d^2)$ per assignment | Moderate | Medium |
| Forward Checking (inference) | $O(n \, d)$ per assignment | High | Very High |
| AC-3 / MAC (inference) | $O(n \, d^2)$ per assignment | Very High | Very High |
| Backjumping | $O(n)$ to track conflict sets | High in dense problems | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — CSP Triple as a Block Architecture

A high-level block diagram illustrating the structural decomposition of a CSP and how backtracking interacts with each subsystem.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["CSP INPUT\nTriple P = X, D, C"] --> B["Constraint Graph G"]
    A --> C["Domain Set D1...Dn"]
    A --> D["Constraint Set C1...Cm"]

    B --> E["Variable Selector\nMRV + Degree"]
    D --> F["Constraint Test\nFunction Check"]
    C --> F
    E --> G["Backtracking Engine\nDFS Recursion"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Inference Module\nForward Checking\nAC-3 / MAC"]
    H --> I["Domain Pruning\nDk Updated"]
    I --> E
    G --> J["Solution Found"]
    G --> K["Backtrack / Backjump\nUndo Last Assignment"]
    K --> E
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Backtracking Search Tree (Partial, 3-Variable CSP)

The diagram below shows a backtracking search tree for a CSP with $n=3$ variables ($A, B, C$) each with domain $\{1, 2, 3\}$, where the constraint is $A \neq B \neq C$. Solid arrows indicate forward recursion, dashed arrows indicate **backtracking** when a constraint fails.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["ROOT: Empty Assignment"] --> A1["A = 1"]
    R --> A2["A = 2"]
    R --> A3["A = 3"]

    A1 --> B1["B = 1\nCONFLICT with A"]
    A1 --> B2["B = 2\nOK"]
    A1 --> B3["B = 3\nOK"]

    A2 --> B4["B = 1\nOK"]
    A2 --> B5["B = 2\nCONFLICT with A"]
    A2 --> B6["B = 3\nOK"]

    A3 --> B7["B = 1\nOK"]
    A3 --> B8["B = 2\nOK"]
    A3 --> B9["B = 3\nCONFLICT with A"]

    B2 --> C1["C = 1\nOK with A,B"]
    B2 --> C2["C = 2\nOK with A,B"]
    B2 --> C3["C = 3\nCONFLICT with B\nBACKTRACK"]

    B4 --> C4["C = 2\nOK with A,B\nSOLUTION"]
    B4 --> C5["C = 3\nOK with A,B"]
    B4 --> C6["C = 1\nOK with A,B"]

    B7 --> C7["C = 2\nOK with A,B"]
    B7 --> C8["C = 3\nOK with A,B"]
    B7 --> C9["C = 1\nOK with A,B"]

    C1 -.backtrack.-> A1
    C5 -.backtrack.-> A2
    C6 -.backtrack.-> A2
    C7 -.backtrack.-> A3
    C8 -.backtrack.-> A3
    C9 -.backtrack.-> A3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the tree:** The depth represents the number of assigned variables. The dashed edges are the *undo* operations.
