\Omega(g) = \begin{cases} \infty & \text{if } g \text{ uses } > K \text{ features} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Final LIME Objective.**

$$
\boxed{\;\xi(x) = \arg\min_{g \in G}\, L(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g)\;}
$$

*Logic*: The returned explanation is the simplest model $g$ that best approximates $f$ locally. The non-zero coefficients of $g$ identify the *most influential features* for the prediction at $x$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the SHAP Value

**Problem Setup (Cooperative Game Theory).** Treat each feature $i \in \{1, \dots, M\}$ as a "player" in a coalition game whose total "payout" is the model's prediction $f(x)$. The Shapley value $\phi_i$ gives feature $i$'s *fair* marginal contribution.

**Step 1 — Define the Set Function.** For a feature subset $S \subseteq F$:

$$
f_x(S) = \mathbb{E}\bigl[\,f(x) \mid x_S \text{ known}\,\bigr]
$$

*Logic*: The expected prediction when only the features in $S$ are revealed; absent features are marginalised over a background dataset.

**Step 2 — Define the Marginal Contribution.** When feature $i$ joins coalition $S$:

$$
\Delta_i(S) = f_x(S \cup \{i\}) - f_x(S)
$$

**Step 3 — Average Over All Coalitions.** Average $\Delta_i(S)$ over all $2^{M-1}$ possible coalitions $S$ not containing $i$:

$$
\phi_i(f, x) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \binom{M-1}{\vert S \vert}^{-1}\, \Delta_i(S)
$$

**Step 4 — Closed-Form Shapley Expression.**

$$
\boxed{\;\phi_i(f, x) = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \frac{\vert S \vert !\,(M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!}\;\bigl[f_x(S \cup \{i\}) - f_x(S)\bigr]\;}
$$

*Logic*: Each coalition size is weighted by the number of orderings in which $i$ arrives at that coalition — yielding a unique, **fair**, and **additive** attribution:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{M} \phi_i = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(X)]
$$

The sum of SHAP values equals the *deviation* of the prediction from the base value, ensuring **local accuracy** (efficiency axiom).

## 3.3 The Faithfulness–Plausibility Trade-off (Analytical Matrix)

| Property | Definition | How to Measure | Typical Issue |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Faithfulness** | Explanation reflects the model's *actual* reasoning | Remove feature $i$ → measure $\Delta f$ | Computationally expensive |
| **Plausibility** | Explanation is *convincing* to humans | User study, Turing-style test | May be fabricated (rationalisation) |
| **Stability** | Similar inputs → similar explanations | Variance of $E$ over $\epsilon$-neighbourhood | Non-robust to adversarial input |
| **Comprehensibility** | User can understand the explanation | Cognitive load index, time-to-task | Depends on user expertise |
| **Completeness** | All decision factors are mentioned | Coverage ratio of identified features | Hard to verify |
| **Robustness** | Explanation invariant to small model retraining | Variance of $E$ across retrained $f$ | Deep ensembles violate this |
| **Fairness** | No protected attribute dominates | Disparate SHAP impact ratio | Discriminatory attributions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Single Metric is Impossible (KTU Discussion Point)**
> The three **desiderata of a good explanation** from Miller (2019) — *contrastive, selective, and social* — are inherently qualitative. Hence, XAI lacks a single objective loss like accuracy in supervised learning. This **evaluation problem** is the *most cited research challenge* in the XAI literature and is a frequent **14-mark** answer topic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Taxonomy of XAI Methods (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[XAI Methods Taxonomy]
    rootA --> branch1[Intrinsic Self Explaining]
    rootA --> branch2[Post Hoc Explanation]
    branch1 --> leaf1[Linear Regression]
    branch1 --> leaf2[Decision Trees]
    branch1 --> leaf3[Attention Based Networks]
    branch1 --> leaf4[Prototype Networks]
    branch2 --> branch2a[Local Explanations]
    branch2 --> branch2b[Global Explanations]
    branch2a --> limeNode[LIME]
    branch2a --> shapNode[SHAP]
    branch2a --> anchorNode[Anchors]
    branch2a --> gradcamNode[Grad CAM Saliency]
    branch2b --> permNode[Permutation Importance]
    branch2b --> pdpNode[Partial Dependence Plots]
    branch2b --> protoNode[Prototypes and Criticisms]
    branch2b --> treeSurrogateNode[Tree Surrogates]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The XAI Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    rawData[Raw Input Data X] --> dataPre[Preprocessing Layer]
    dataPre --> blackBox[Trained Black Box Model f]
    blackBox --> predOut[Prediction f of x]
    predOut --> xaiCore[XAI Explanation Engine]
    xaiCore --> localExp[Local Explanation e local]
    xaiCore --> globalExp[Global Explanation e global]
    xaiCore --> counterExp[Counterfactual Explanation e cf]
    localExp --> userInterface[Human User Interface]
    globalExp --> userInterface
    counterExp --> userInterface
    userInterface --> trustLoop[Trust Calibration and Feedback]
    trustLoop --> retrain[Model Audit and Retraining]
    retrain --> blackBox
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Black-Box vs. Glass-Box (Explainable) AI Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputNode[Input Feature Vector x] --> bbPath[Black Box Path]
    inputNode --> gbPath[Glass Box Path]
    bbPath --> bbModel[Deep Neural Network or Ensemble]
    bbModel --> bbOut[Output y]
    bbOut --> bbTrust[Low Trust No Reasoning]
    gbPath --> gbModel[Linear Model or Surrogate g]
    gbModel --> gbOut[Output y prime]
    gbOut --> gbReason[Reason Codes Feature Contributions]
    gbReason --> gbTrust[High Trust Auditable]
    bbTrust --> regulatorBlock[Regulator Rejection GDPR Non Compliance]
    gbTrust --> acceptanceBlock[Acceptance and Deployment]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — The Seven Major Challenges of XAI (KTU 2024)

```mermaid
graph TD
    chRoot[Seven XAI Challenges KTU 2024]
    chRoot --> c1[Challenge 1 Accuracy vs Interpretability Trade off]
    chRoot --> c2[Challenge 2 Human Comprehension Gap]
    chRoot --> c3[Challenge 3 Evaluation Metrics Absence]
    chRoot --> c4[Challenge 4 Explanation Stability]
    chRoot --> c5[Challenge 5 Computational Complexity]

```

> - **No reserved Mermaid keywords** (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node identifiers.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Define Explainable AI (XAI). Why is it important in modern machine learning systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Explainable AI (XAI) refers to a collection of techniques and frameworks that enable human users to understand, trust, and interpret the decisions and predictions made by AI/ML models. (1 mark)
XAI is important because: (i) it builds **trust** between users and AI systems, (ii) it satisfies **legal and regulatory requirements** (e.g., GDPR's right to explanation), (iii) it helps in **debugging and bias detection**, and (iv) it enables **accountability** in safety-critical applications such as healthcare, finance, and autonomous vehicles. (2 marks — ½ mark per valid point)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
> **Differentiate between Interpretability and Explainability in AI with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Interpretability** is the inherent property of a model that allows humans to directly understand its internal mechanics. Example: a linear regression model with weights showing the direct effect of each feature. (1½ marks)
**Explainability** is the ability to *post-hoc* justify the output of an opaque model. Example: applying SHAP to explain a black-box neural network's prediction for a specific loan applicant. (1½ marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Estimated]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **importance of Explainable AI (XAI)** in modern AI systems. Discuss its role in at least four real-world domains. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Describe the **major challenges of XAI** with a focus on the **accuracy–interpretability trade-off** and the **evaluation problem**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Importance of XAI (7 marks)

**1. Trust and Adoption (1 mark):** Users are reluctant to delegate critical decisions to systems they cannot understand. In healthcare, a clinician will not administer a drug solely because a model says "high probability of effectiveness" without seeing *which biomarkers* drove that prediction.

**2. Regulatory and Legal Compliance (2 marks):** Laws such as the **EU GDPR (Article 22)** grant citizens the "right to explanation" for automated decisions affecting them. India's **RBI Master Directions on Digital Lending (2022)** mandate that loan-rejection decisions be accompanied by specific reason codes — a direct legal requirement that XAI techniques fulfil.

**3. Bias Detection and Fairness (1 mark):** XAI methods like SHAP can reveal whether a model relies on a protected attribute (e.g., gender, caste, religion) for predictions, enabling audit and remediation.

**4. Debugging and Model Improvement (1 mark):** Explanations expose failure modes. A saliency map may reveal that a "pneumonia detector" is actually looking at the *hospital token* printed on the X-ray — a spurious correlation caught only through XAI.

**5. Accountability in Safety-Critical Systems (2 marks):** In autonomous vehicles, aviation, and medical diagnosis, post-incident analysis requires understanding *why* the AI failed. The **EU AI Act (2024)** classifies such systems as "high-risk" and mandates documentation and explainability.

#### Part (b) — Major Challenges of XAI (7 marks)

**1. Accuracy–Interpretability Trade-off (3 marks):** High-performing models (deep nets, gradient-boosted trees) are inherently opaque, while interpretable models (linear, shallow trees) often sacrifice accuracy. XAI attempts to break this trade-off via post-hoc methods, but the surrogate $g$ can never *perfectly* mimic $f$ in all regions — leading to the **fidelity gap**:

$$
\text{Fidelity Gap} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert f(x_i) - g(x_i) \vert \neq 0
$$

**2. Evaluation Problem (2 marks):** Unlike accuracy in supervised learning, XAI has **no universal loss function**. There is no "ground truth" explanation to compare against. Researchers rely on proxies (fidelity, stability, user studies), each capturing a different dimension. This makes benchmarking XAI methods fundamentally harder than benchmarking classifiers.

**3. Other Notable Challenges (2 marks):**
- *Stability*: Small perturbations in input can produce vastly different explanations, undermining user trust.
- *Computational cost*: Exact Shapley values require evaluating $2^M$ coalitions, infeasible for high-dimensional data.
- *Faithfulness vs. plausibility*: A method may generate a *convincing-sounding* but *unfaithful* explanation.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Estimated — INTERNAL CHOICE]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **taxonomy of XAI methods**. Differentiate between **intrinsic vs post-hoc**, and **local vs global** explanations with examples. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the **LIME objective function** step by step. Show how a local surrogate $g$ approximates the black-box $f$ around a query instance $x$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Taxonomy of XAI Methods (7 marks)

**1. Overview (1 mark):** XAI methods can be classified along two primary axes: (i) **stage of explanation** (intrinsic vs post-hoc) and (ii) **scope of explanation** (local vs global). Refer to *Diagram 1 in Section 4.1* for a complete taxonomy.

**2. Intrinsic vs Post-hoc (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Intrinsic (Ante-hoc) | Post-hoc |
|---|---|---|
| When applied | Built into model design | Applied after training |
| Examples | Linear regression, Decision trees, Attention networks | LIME, SHAP, Grad-CAM |
| Pros | Inherently transparent | Works on any model |
| Cons | Limited model complexity | May not reflect true reasoning |

**3. Local vs Global (3 marks):**
- **Local explanations** describe a *single* prediction. Example: *"This loan application was rejected because the debt-to-income ratio (0.62) exceeded the policy threshold (0.45) and the credit score (580) was below 650."* — generated by LIME/SHAP for one applicant. (1½ marks)
- **Global explanations** describe the *overall* model behaviour. Example: A partial dependence plot showing that, on average, "income" is the most influential feature, followed by "employment length". (1½ marks)

#### Part (b) — LIME Derivation (7 marks)

The full derivation is provided in *Section 3.1* above. The summarised exam-ready steps are:

1. **Perturbation generation:** Sample $z' \sim \mathcal{N}(x, \sigma^2 I)$ around $x$. (1 mark)
2. **Black-box labelling:** $y_i = f(z'_i)$. (1 mark)
3. **Proximity weighting:** $\pi_x(z') = \exp(-\Vert x - z' \Vert_2^2 / \tau^2)$. (1 mark)
4. **Weighted loss:** $L(f, g, \pi_x) = \sum_i \pi_x(z'_i) [f(z'_i) - g(z'_i)]^2$. (2 marks)
5. **Complexity penalty:** $\Omega(g)$ constrains $g$ to use at most $K$ features. (1 mark)
6. **Final LIME equation:** $\xi(x) = \arg\min_g L(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g)$. (1 mark)

The non-zero coefficients of $g$ form the explanation. For instance, if $g$ says *"prediction = 0.8 × feature A − 0.4 × feature B"*, the explanation is "Feature A increased the prediction; Feature B decreased it."

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not confuse "interpretability" with "explainability"** — they are *not synonyms*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if used interchangeably.
> 2. **Don't write LIME/SHAP formulas without defining the variables.** Every symbol ($f$, $g$, $\pi_x$, $\Omega$, $\phi_i$, $M$) must be explained — KTU examiners will not award full marks for unannotated equations.
> 3. **Do not list challenges without justification.** For each of the seven challenges, you must give *why* it is a challenge and *how* it impacts real systems.
> 4. **Avoid one-line answers in 14-mark questions.** A 14-mark answer should span *at least* 1.5–2 handwritten pages with diagrams, sub-headings, and structured points.
> 5. **Do not omit diagrams.** A taxonomy or pipeline diagram is worth 1–2 marks by itself; missing it costs you 10–15% of the question's marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Definition (1-liner):** XAI = AI systems that can *explain* their decisions in *human-understandable* terms.
- ✅ **DARPA 2017** is the standard reference framing — quote it for full marks.
- ✅ **Four pillars of importance:** Trust, Transparency, Accountability, Fairness.
- ✅ **Three axes of taxonomy:** Intrinsic vs Post-hoc; Local vs Global; Model-specific vs Model-agnostic.
- ✅ **LIME Equation:** $\xi(x) = \arg\min_g L(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g)$ — local surrogate approach.
- ✅ **SHAP Equation:** Shapley-value-based fair attribution; satisfies efficiency (local accuracy) axiom: $\sum_i \phi_i = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(X)]$.
- ✅ **Seven Key Challenges:** Trade-off, Comprehension, Evaluation, Stability, Computational cost, Faithfulness vs. Plausibility, Legal/Ethical compliance.
- ✅ **GDPR Article 22** and the **EU AI Act 2024** are the two most-cited legal frameworks.
- ✅ **Domains:** Healthcare, Finance, Autonomous Vehicles, Cybersecurity, Justice System.
- ✅ **Real-world XAI tools:** LIME, SHAP, Grad-CAM, Anchors, Eli5, Alibi, Captum, tf-explain.
- ✅ **Trade-off formula:** $\text{Accuracy} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Interpretability} \downarrow$ (and XAI aims to break this).
- ✅ **Exam tip:** Always draw a **taxonomy diagram** and a **pipeline diagram** for 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Avoid pitfall:** Never say XAI makes a model *"100% interpretable"* — XAI *explains* opaque models; it does not *convert* them into interpretable ones.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of XAI Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview of XAI Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI)** refers to a suite of methods, frameworks, and algorithmic techniques designed to produce **human-interpretable explanations** for the decisions, predictions, and internal reasoning processes of AI/ML systems, particularly those built using complex "black-box" models such as deep neural networks, ensemble methods, and large language models.

According to the **Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA)** XAI program (2017), the goal of XAI is to create *"AI systems whose actions and outputs can be easily understood by human users,"* thereby enabling trust, accountability, transparency, and effective human-machine collaboration.

### Taxonomy at a Glance

XAI techniques are broadly classified along three orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Category 1 | Category 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | **Global Explanations** (entire model behavior) | **Local Explanations** (single prediction) |
| **Model Dependence** | **Model-Agnostic** (any ML model) | **Model-Specific** (a particular model class) |
| **Generation Time** | **Post-hoc** (after training) | **Intrinsic / Ante-hoc** (built into model) |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Courtroom of the Algorithm"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a black-box AI system as a brilliant but secretive **doctor** who diagnoses diseases perfectly but never explains *how* they arrived at the conclusion. You would not blindly accept the prescription — you would want justification, evidence, and reasoning. **XAI is the equivalent of forcing the doctor to show their medical charts, lab reports, and differential diagnosis** before you trust them.

In this analogy:
- **Black-box model** → The secretive doctor (e.g., a deep neural network).
- **Prediction** → The prescription/diagnosis.
- **XAI technique** → The transparent chart of symptoms, weights, and reasoning that the doctor must reveal.
- **Stakeholder** → The patient, regulator, or auditor seeking trust.

This becomes critical in high-stakes domains like **medical diagnosis**, **autonomous driving**, **loan approvals**, and **judicial sentencing**, where opaque decisions can have life-altering consequences.

---

## 1.3 Why XAI Matters — The Three Pillars

1. **Trust**: Users must believe the AI's output is correct and reliable.
2. **Accountability**: Regulations such as the **EU GDPR (Article 22)**, **US Algorithmic Accountability Act**, and **India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA, 2023)** demand a *"right to explanation"* for automated decisions.
3. **Debugging & Bias Detection**: Explanations reveal hidden biases, spurious correlations, and dataset shift problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus places XAI as a **bridge between symbolic AI and connectionist AI**, integrating logical reasoning with statistical learning. Expect questions that test your ability to *contrast* interpretable models with black-box models and *justify* the choice of an XAI technique for a given scenario.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Checklist

- **Interpretability**: The degree to which a human can understand the cause of a decision *without needing additional explanation*.
- **Explainability**: The ability to provide *post-hoc* or *ante-hoc* justifications for model behavior.
- **Fidelity**: How accurately an explanation reflects the true reasoning of the underlying model.
- **Completeness**: How well an explanation covers the full decision logic.
- **Robustness**: Stability of explanations under small input perturbations.
- **Transparency**: Openness about model architecture, training data, and decision processes.

These six metrics form the **evaluation rubric** for any XAI technique, as standardized by **Hoffman et al. (2018)** in their influential *Metrics for Explainable AI* framework.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Grand Families of XAI

### A. Post-hoc Explainability (Retrofit Explanations)

Applied **after** a model has been trained. Does not modify the original model.

**Major Sub-families:**

#### A1. Local Surrogate Models — **LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations)**

> [!NOTE]
> **LIME**, introduced by *Ribeiro et al. (2016)*, explains a single prediction $f(x)$ of any black-box classifier by learning a *simple, interpretable surrogate model* $g$ (typically linear or decision tree) in the **local neighborhood** of $x$.

**Algorithm Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. Select the instance $x$ to be explained.
2. Generate a perturbed dataset $\mathcal{Z} = \{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_N\}$ by randomly sampling around $x$.
3. Obtain black-box predictions $f(z_i)$ for each perturbed sample.
4. Assign weights $w_i = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{D(x, z_i)^2}{\sigma^2}\right)$ based on proximity.
5. Train an interpretable model $g \in G$ (e.g., linear regression) on $\mathcal{Z}$ weighted by $w_i$.
6. Explanation: $\xi(x) = \arg\min_{g \in G} \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g)$

   where $\mathcal{L}$ is the fidelity loss and $\Omega(g)$ penalizes model complexity.

#### A2. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)

Based on **cooperative game theory** (Shapley values, 1953). Assigns each feature an importance value such that the sum of importances equals the model's output deviation from the base value.

$$\phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \frac{\vert S \vert ! \, (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!} \left[ f_{x}(S \cup \{i\}) - f_{x}(S) \right]$$

where $M$ is the total number of features, $S$ is a subset of features, and $f_x(S)$ is the model output using only features in $S$.

**Key Property — Local Accuracy:**
$$f(x) = \phi_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{M} \phi_i(x)$$

where $\phi_0 = E[f(x)]$ is the expected (base) value.

#### A3. Counterfactual Explanations

> [!NOTE]
> **"What minimal change to the input would flip the prediction?"** A counterfactual $x'$ is the closest instance to $x$ such that $f(x') \neq f(x)$.

**Formal Objective:**
$$\min_{x'} \, d(x, x') \quad \text{subject to} \quad f(x') = y_{\text{target}}$$

where $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is typically the $\ell_1$ or $\ell_2$ distance, and the instance must remain valid (e.g., $x'_j \in [\text{min}_j, \text{max}_j]$).

#### A4. Visualization-Based — **Grad-CAM**

For CNN-based image classifiers. Uses **gradient flows** into the final convolutional layer to produce a coarse localization heatmap highlighting important regions.

$$L^c_{\text{Grad-CAM}} = \text{ReLU}\!\left(\sum_k \alpha_k^c \, A^k\right)$$

where $A^k$ is the $k$-th feature map and:
$$\alpha_k^c = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_i \sum_j \frac{\partial Y^c}{\partial A^k_{ij}}$$

is the global-average-pooled gradient of class score $Y^c$ w.r.t. feature map $A^k$.

---

### B. Intrinsic / Ante-hoc Interpretability (Interpretable by Design)

Models that are **transparent by construction**. No separate explanation layer is needed.

**Examples:**
- **Linear / Logistic Regression**: Coefficients directly indicate feature influence.
- **Decision Trees**: Path from root to leaf is the explanation.
- **Rule-Based Systems (e.g., RIPPER, OneR)**: IF-THEN rules.
- **Attention Mechanisms** in Transformers: Attention weights are *sometimes* used as explanations (though this is contested).
- **Generalized Additive Models (GAMs)**: $g(y) = f_1(x_1) + f_2(x_2) + \ldots + f_M(x_M)$ where each $f_i$ is a shape function plotted for visual interpretation.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LIME Objective | $\xi(x) = \arg\min_{g \in G}\, \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g)$ | Local explanation |
| 2 | SHAP (Shapley) | $\phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \dfrac{\vert S \vert !\,(M-\vert S \vert -1)!}{M!}\,[\,f_x(S \cup \{i\}) - f_x(S)\,]$ | Feature attribution |
| 3 | SHAP Local Accuracy | $f(x) = \phi_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{M} \phi_i(x)$ | Sum check |
| 4 | Counterfactual | $\min_{x'}\, d(x,x')$ s.t. $f(x') = y_{\text{target}}$ | Actionable recourse |
| 5 | Grad-CAM heatmap | $L^c_{\text{Grad-CAM}} = \text{ReLU}(\sum_k \alpha_k^c A^k)$ | Image localization |
| 6 | Attention Weight (Transformer) | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | NLP explanation (caveat) |
| 7 | GAM Decomposition | $g(E[y]) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} f_i(x_i)$ | Global additive view |
| 8 | Proximity Kernel (LIME) | $w_i = \exp(-D(x,z_i)^2 / \sigma^2)$ | Sample weighting |
| 9 | Mutual Information (Feature Importance) | $I(X_i; Y) = \sum_{x_i, y} p(x_i, y) \log \dfrac{p(x_i, y)}{p(x_i)\,p(y)}$ | Information-theoretic importance |
| 10 | Faithfulness Score | $\rho = \text{corr}\!\left(f(x) - f(x \setminus i),\, \phi_i(x)\right)$ | Explanation quality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder:** Whenever you use $\vert S \vert$ (cardinality) in an answer, write it as `\vert S \vert` or `\lvert S \rvert` in LaTeX — never as bare pipes, which break Markdown rendering.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where Each Technique Shines

| Technique | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **LIME** | Tabular + text + image, fast prototyping | Instability across runs |
| **SHAP** | Theoretical guarantees, tree models have fast `TreeSHAP` | Computationally expensive ($O(2^M)$) |
| **Counterfactuals** | Loan refusal, medical "what-if" | Hard to generate for non-differentiable models |
| **Grad-CAM** | CNN visual explanations | Coarse, class-discriminative only |
| **Attention** | NLP Transformers | "Attention is NOT Explanation" (Jain & Wallace, 2019) |
| **Decision Trees** | High-stakes, small-data domains | Lower accuracy on complex data |
| **GAMs** | Risk modeling, healthcare | Restricted to additive structure |

---

## 2.4 The Model-Specific vs Model-Agnostic Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Model-Agnostic** techniques (LIME, SHAP, PDP, ICE plots) decouple explanation from the underlying model — they treat it as a black-box and query it. This provides flexibility but may sacrifice fidelity.
>
> **Model-Specific** techniques (TreeSHAP, Grad-CAM, attention weights, influence functions) exploit internal structure — they are more faithful but tied to one model class.

**Engineering Rule of Thumb:** When deploying XAI in regulated industries, prefer **model-specific** methods when the model class permits, because regulators (e.g., **RBI** for credit scoring, **FDA** for medical AI) demand *high-fidelity* justifications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: SHAP Value Computation

We derive the Shapley value from first principles to build deep understanding, a hallmark of KTU's expected answer depth.

**Step 1 — Cooperative Game Setup**

Suppose $M$ players (features) collaborate in a game (prediction). The total payout $v(S)$ is the model's prediction using only the feature subset $S \subseteq \{1, 2, \ldots, M\}$.

**Step 2 — Marginal Contribution of Player $i$ in Coalition $S$**

When player $i$ joins coalition $S$, the marginal gain is:
$$\Delta_i(S) = v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S)$$

**Step 3 — Average Over All Coalitions**

Player $i$'s Shapley value is the *weighted average* of marginal contributions over **all possible orderings** of players. Each coalition $S$ of size $k$ has probability $\frac{k!\,(M-k-1)!}{M!}$ of occurring before $i$.

**Step 4 — Final Closed Form**

Combining weights and contributions:
$$\phi_i(v) = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \frac{\vert S \vert ! \, (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!} \bigl[ v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S) \bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency Axiom Check**

Summing over all players:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{M} \phi_i = v(F) - v(\emptyset)$$

For ML, $v(F) = f(x)$ (full model) and $v(\emptyset) = E[f(x)]$ (base rate), so:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{M} \phi_i = f(x) - E[f(x)]$$

Hence:
$$f(x) = E[f(x)] + \sum_{i=1}^{M} \phi_i(x)$$

This is the **additive feature attribution property** that uniquely identifies SHAP among explanation methods.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: LIME's Local Fidelity Objective

**Step 1 — Define the Domain Transformation**

LIME operates in the *interpretable input space* $x' \in \{0,1\}^M$ (e.g., binary presence/absence of words). A mapping $x = h_x(x')$ reconstructs the original instance.

**Step 2 — Define the Proximity Measure**

$$\pi_x(z') = \exp\!\left(-\frac{D(x, z)^2}{\sigma^2}\right)$$

where $D$ is a distance (cosine for text, Euclidean for images, Gower for tabular) and $\sigma$ is the kernel width.

**Step 3 — Define the Fidelity Loss**

$$\mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) = \sum_{z, z' \in \mathcal{Z}} \pi_x(z')\bigl[f(z) - g(z')\bigr]^2$$

**Step 4 — Combine with Complexity Penalty**

$$\xi(x) = \arg\min_{g \in G}\, \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g)$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

The explanation $g$ is chosen to be **(a)** faithful to $f$ in the local neighborhood (low $\mathcal{L}$) and **(b)** simple (low $\Omega$, e.g., number of non-zero weights). This guarantees that the explanation is **locally accurate but globally simple** — the central design philosophy of LIME.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Grad-CAM Gradient Computation

**Step 1 — Compute Class Score Gradient**

For a CNN with final convolutional feature maps $A^k$ (height $H$, width $W$), and class $c$ score $Y^c$:

$$\frac{\partial Y^c}{\partial A^k_{ij}}$$

This is the gradient flowing back through the network during backpropagation.

**Step 2 — Global Average Pool the Gradient**

We collapse spatial dimensions by averaging:

$$\alpha_k^c = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_{i=1}^{H} \sum_{j=1}^{W} \frac{\partial Y^c}{\partial A^k_{ij}}, \quad Z = H \cdot W$$

This $\alpha_k^c$ represents the *global importance* of feature map $k$ for class $c$.

**Step 3 — Weighted Combination of Feature Maps**

$$L^c_{\text{Grad-CAM}} = \text{ReLU}\!\left(\sum_{k} \alpha_k^c A^k\right)$$

The **ReLU** discards negative influences (we care about what *supports* class $c$, not what opposes it).

**Step 4 — Upsample to Input Resolution**

Bilinear interpolation scales $L^c_{\text{Grad-CAM}}$ to match the input image size, producing a heatmap overlay.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right XAI Technique

| Scenario | Best XAI Choice | Reasoning | KTU Expectation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Regulated finance (loan approval) | **Counterfactual + SHAP** | Actionable recourse + global fidelity | Justify *why* in 7-mark answer |
| Medical image diagnosis (X-ray) | **Grad-CAM** | Visual spatial reasoning | Show heatmap computation |
| NLP sentiment (transformer) | **LIME or Integrated Gradients** | Attention not reliable (Jain & Wallace) | Mention controversy |
| High-stakes small dataset | **Decision Tree / GAM** | Intrinsic transparency | Discuss accuracy-interpretability trade-off |
| Real-time fraud detection | **SHAP (TreeSHAP)** | Fast exact computation on tree ensembles | Mention complexity $O(TLD^2)$ |
| Recommender system | **Counterfactual + attention** | Personalized "why this item?" | Combine techniques |
| Legal / judicial sentencing | **Rule-based (post-hoc rules)** | Auditable, deterministic | Cite *State v. Loomis* (2016) case |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Answer Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always *tabulate the trade-offs* rather than just listing techniques. Examiners reward comparative analysis with **2-3 extra marks** under the "depth of analysis" criterion.

---

## 3.5 Evaluation Metrics for XAI — The Quantification Challenge

Since explanations are qualitative, the community has developed quantitative metrics:

**1. Faithfulness** — Correlation between feature importance and impact on prediction when that feature is removed:

$$\text{Faithfulness} = \text{corr}_i\!\left(f(x) - f(x_{\setminus i}),\, \phi_i(x)\right)$$

**2. Robustness** — Stability of explanation under small input perturbations:

$$\text{Robustness} = \mathbb{E}_{\delta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \epsilon I)} \left[ \Vert \xi(x) - \xi(x + \delta) \Vert_2 \right]$$

**3. Completeness** — Sum of feature attributions equals the total deviation (SHAP satisfies this automatically).

**4. Human-grounded Utility** — User studies where humans rate explanation helpfulness.

**5. Functional Complexity (Došilović et al., 2018):**

$$\Omega(g) = \text{number of non-zero coefficients} + \text{interaction terms}$$

A *lower* $\Omega(g)$ means a *simpler, more interpretable* explanation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*Define Explainable AI (XAI). List any two post-hoc XAI techniques with one-line descriptions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** XAI refers to a collection of techniques that produce human-understandable justifications for the outputs of AI/ML systems, addressing the "black-box" problem inherent in complex models.

**Post-hoc Techniques (2 marks):**

1. **LIME** — Builds a locally faithful linear surrogate model around a single prediction to explain black-box decisions.
2. **SHAP** — Uses Shapley values from cooperative game theory to assign each feature an additive contribution to the prediction.

*(Alternative accepted: Counterfactual, Grad-CAM, PDP.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*Differentiate between intrinsic and post-hoc interpretability with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Intrinsic | Post-hoc |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Model is interpretable by design | Explanation extracted after training |
| **Example** | Decision Tree, Linear Regression | LIME, SHAP, Grad-CAM |
| **Fidelity** | Exact (model = explanation) | Approximate (surrogate may diverge) |
| **Flexibility** | Limited to simple model classes | Works on any black-box |

**Key Distinction (1 mark):** Intrinsic interpretability is *built-in* (ante-hoc), while post-hoc is *retrofit* (after training). The former offers perfect fidelity; the latter offers flexibility.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**
*(a)* Explain the LIME algorithm in detail with its mathematical formulation. Discuss the role of the proximity kernel and the interpretable input space. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Describe the SHAP framework based on Shapley values. Derive the local accuracy property and show why SHAP is considered the *unique* solution satisfying local accuracy, missingness, and consistency. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — LIME (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):**
LIME addresses the black-box problem by learning a simple surrogate model $g \in G$ (e.g., linear) that mimics the complex model $f$ **locally** around a specific instance $x$.

**Step 2 — Interpretable Representation (1 mark):**
LIME converts the original input $x$ into a binary interpretable vector $x' \in \{0, 1\}^M$. For text, $x'_i = 1$ means word $i$ is present; for images, $x'_i = 1$ means super-pixel $i$ is "on."

**Step 3 — Perturbation Generation (1 mark):**
Generate $N$ perturbed samples $z'_i \in \{0,1\}^M$ by randomly toggling bits of $x'$. Map back to original space: $z_i = h_x(z'_i)$.

**Step 4 — Proximity Kernel (1 mark):**

$$\pi_x(z') = \exp\!\left(-\frac{D(x, z)^2}{\sigma^2}\right)$$

This Gaussian kernel ensures that perturbations *closer* to $x$ receive *higher weight*, enforcing locality. The hyperparameter $\sigma$ controls the kernel width.

**Step 5 — Optimization Objective (2 marks):**

$$\xi(x) = \arg\min_{g \in G}\, \underbrace{\mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x)}_{\text{fidelity loss}} + \underbrace{\Omega(g)}_{\text{complexity penalty}}$$

where:
$$\mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_x(z'_i)\bigl[f(z_i) - g(z'_i)\bigr]^2$$

**Step 6 — Final Explanation (1 mark):**
The chosen $g$ (e.g., sparse linear model) yields coefficients $\beta_j$ that quantify each feature's local influence — visualized as a horizontal bar chart.

---

### Part (b) — SHAP (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Game-Theoretic Foundation (1 mark):**
SHAP assigns each feature $i$ a value $\phi_i$ representing its marginal contribution to the prediction, averaged over all coalitions $S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}$.

**Step 2 — Shapley Value Formula (2 marks):**

$$\phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \frac{\vert S \vert ! \, (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!} \bigl[ f_x(S \cup \{i\}) - f_x(S) \bigr]$$

The weighting factor $\frac{\vert S \vert ! (M - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{M!}$ is the probability that coalition $S$ is formed before player $i$ in a random permutation.

**Step 3 — Local Accuracy Derivation (2 marks):**
Summing Shapley values over all features:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{M} \phi_i = v(F) - v(\emptyset) = f(x) - E[f(x)]$$
$$\implies f(x) = \underbrace{E[f(x)]}_{\phi_0} + \sum_{i=1}^{M} \phi_i(x)$$

This is the **additive feature attribution** property.

**Step 4 — Uniqueness (2 marks):**
By the Shapley uniqueness theorem (Shapley, 1953), the Shapley value is the **only** attribution method that simultaneously satisfies:

1. **Local Accuracy**: $f(x) = \phi_0 + \sum \phi_i$
2. **Missingness**: $\phi_i = 0$ if feature $i$ is missing/unobserved
3. **Consistency**: If a model changes so that $i$ contributes more, $\phi_i$ should not decrease

These three axioms together make SHAP the **gold standard** for feature attribution in regulated applications.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**
*(a)* What are counterfactual explanations? Formulate the optimization problem and explain how *diversity* and *proximity* constraints improve actionability. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Describe the Grad-CAM technique for visual explanations in CNNs. Derive the importance weight $\alpha_k^c$ from first principles and explain the role of the ReLU operation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Counterfactual Explanations (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
A counterfactual $x'$ is the *minimum change* to input $x$ such that the model's prediction flips to a desired class $y_{\text{target}}$.

> *"You were denied a loan because your income was ₹4.5 LPA. If it had been ₹5.5 LPA, you would have been approved."*

**Step 2 — Basic Optimization Formulation (2 marks):**

$$\min_{x'} \, d(x, x') \quad \text{subject to} \quad f(x') = y_{\text{target}}$$

where $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is typically $\ell_1$ (sparse, actionable) or $\ell_2$ (Euclidean) distance.

**Step 3 — Validity Constraints (1 mark):**
The instance $x'$ must lie in a valid domain:
$$x'_j \in [L_j, U_j], \quad \forall j \in \{1, \ldots, M\}$$
*(e.g., age cannot be negative; income cannot be infinite.)*

**Step 4 — Diversity (1 mark):**
A single counterfactual may be uninformative. *Diverse Counterfactual Explanations (DiCE)* (Mothilal et al., 2020) generate a set:
$$\min_{X'}\, \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} d(x, x'_i) - \lambda \cdot \text{diversity}(X')$$

This exposes multiple recourse paths, empowering user choice.

**Step 5 — Actionability (2 marks):**
*Proximity* (closeness to $x$) ensures the change is small. *Actionability* further restricts mutable features (e.g., cannot change *race* or *gender*). Formally:

$$\min_{x'}\, \Vert x - x' \Vert_1 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad f(x') = y_{\text{target}}, \, x'_j = x_j \; \forall j \in \mathcal{I}_{\text{immutable}}$$

where $\mathcal{I}_{\text{immutable}}$ is the set of immutable feature indices.

---

### Part (b) — Grad-CAM (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):**
CNNs classify images but offer no insight into *where* in the image the decision originated. Grad-CAM produces a class-discriminative heatmap using gradient information flowing into the last convolutional layer.

**Step 2 — Architecture (1 mark):**
Let $A^k \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W}$ denote the $k$-th feature map of the last conv layer, and $Y^c$ the pre-softmax score for class $c$.

**Step 3 — Gradient Computation (1 mark):**
During backpropagation, compute:
$$\frac{\partial Y^c}{\partial A^k_{ij}}$$

This is the sensitivity of class $c$ score to a small change in activation $A^k_{ij}$.

**Step 4 — Importance Weight Derivation (2 marks):**
Global-average-pool the gradients to get the neuron importance weight:
$$\alpha_k^c = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_{i=1}^{H} \sum_{j=1}^{W} \frac{\partial Y^c}{\partial A^k_{ij}}, \quad Z = H \cdot W$$

**Reasoning:** A high $\alpha_k^c$ means feature map $k$ contains *strong evidence* for class $c$.

**Step 5 — Heatmap Construction (1 mark):**

$$L^c_{\text{Grad-CAM}} = \text{ReLU}\!\left(\sum_{k} \alpha_k^c A^k\right)$$

**Step 6 — Role of ReLU (1 mark):**
ReLU sets all negative values to zero. We are interested only in features that **positively influence** the class score. Negative contributions would highlight regions *against* class $c$, which is misleading in a class-discriminative explanation.

**Step 7 — Final Output (optional bonus):**
Bilinear interpolation upsamples $L^c_{\text{Grad-CAM}}$ to input resolution; overlay on original image yields the saliency map.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing LIME and SHAP origins** — LIME is *local surrogate*, SHAP is *game-theoretic Shapley*. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Omitting the proximity kernel** in LIME answers — the Gaussian weight $w_i = \exp(-D^2/\sigma^2)$ is **mandatory** for full marks (1 mark reserved).
> 3. **Forgetting ReLU in Grad-CAM** — the formula without ReLU is incomplete; you lose 0.5-1 mark.
> 4. **Writing bare pipes** ($\vert x \vert$) inside Markdown tables — KTU expects consistent LaTeX usage with `\vert`.
> 5. **Listing techniques without comparison** — for 7-mark sub-parts, always include a **comparative table or trade-off discussion**.
> 6. **Missing the axioms** (Local Accuracy, Missingness, Consistency) when describing SHAP uniqueness — these are explicitly asked in past papers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Overview of XAI Techniques**

- **XAI** = set of techniques to make AI decisions **human-understandable, trustworthy, and accountable**.
- **Two Grand Families:** Intrinsic (interpretable by design) vs Post-hoc (retrofit explanations).
- **Two Scopes:** Global (entire model) vs Local (single prediction).
- **Two Model Dependencies:** Model-Agnostic (LIME, SHAP) vs Model-Specific (Grad-CAM, TreeSHAP).
- **LIME Objective:** $\xi(x) = \arg\min_{g} \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g)$ — surrogate with proximity-weighted fidelity loss.
- **SHAP Formula:** Shapley-value-weighted average of marginal contributions; satisfies **Local Accuracy, Missingness, Consistency**.
- **SHAP Local Accuracy:** $f(x) = \phi_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{M} \phi_i(x)$ — uniquely identifies SHAP.
- **Counterfactual:** $\min d(x, x')$ subject to $f(x') = y_{\text{target}}$ — provides *actionable recourse*.
- **Grad-CAM Heatmap:** $L^c = \text{ReLU}(\sum_k \alpha_k^c A^k)$ with $\alpha_k^c = \frac{1}{Z}\sum_{i,j}\frac{\partial Y^c}{\partial A^k_{ij}}$.
- **Attention $\neq$ Explanation** (Jain & Wallace, 2019) — common misconception; examiners may test this.
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Faithfulness, Robustness, Completeness, Functional Complexity $\Omega(g)$.
- **Real-world Domains:** Medical (FDA), Finance (RBI, EU GDPR Art. 22), Autonomous Vehicles, Legal (State v. Loomis).
- **Trade-off:** Interpretability ↔ Accuracy — no free lunch; choose by **stakeholder, regulation, and risk**.
- **Exam Strategy:** Always **tabulate** techniques; mention **at least one limitation**; cite **regulatory context** where applicable.
- **Common Pitfalls:** (a) Confusing LIME/SHAP, (b) Missing proximity kernel, (c) Forgetting ReLU in Grad-CAM, (d) Using bare pipes in tables.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — XAI Techniques Overview | PCITT602 Advanced AI | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Interpretable Models — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretable Models (a.k.a. Glass-Box / White-Box Models)** are machine learning models whose internal decision-making logic can be directly inspected, understood, and explained by a human in natural language, without requiring post-hoc approximation tools. They sit in **direct contrast to black-box models** (Deep Neural Networks, Gradient Boosted Ensembles) whose internal representations are opaque.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** curriculum, interpretability is treated as a **first-class engineering requirement**, not an optional add-on. It directly maps to the ethical AI principles of **transparency, accountability, and fairness** mandated under India's National Strategy for AI (NSAI) and the EU AI Act (which KTU case-studies reference).

**Formal Definition (Christopher Molnar, Christoph Molnar's Interpretable ML framework adopted by KTU):**

> An interpretable model $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ is one for which a domain expert can, for any input $x \in \mathcal{X}$ and any prediction $\hat{y} = f(x)$, articulate the *causal* chain of reasoning used to reach $\hat{y}$, using only the parameters and structure of $f$ itself.

### The Three Pillars of Interpretability (KTU Module 3 Scope)

| Pillar | KTU Term | Decision Boundary | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Decision Trees** | Tree-based white-box | Axis-parallel splits | Low–Medium |
| **Linear Models** | Additive white-box | Hyperplane | Low |
| **Rule-Based Models** | Logical white-box | Disjunctive/conjunctive | Variable |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three Doctors"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Analogy — Three Doctors, One Patient:**
> 
> Imagine three doctors diagnosing the same patient. **Doctor Linear** says, *"For every kg you weigh over 60, your risk goes up by 2%; for every year of age, 0.5%."* This is a **Linear Model** — a simple, additive formula.
> 
> **Doctor Tree** says, *"Is your BP > 140? If yes, go left. If left, is your cholesterol > 200? If yes, you are at high risk."* This is a **Decision Tree** — a flowchart of yes/no questions.
> 
> **Doctor Rules** says, *"IF (age > 50) AND (smoker = YES) THEN risk = HIGH. EXCEPT IF (exerciser = YES) THEN risk = MEDIUM."* This is a **Rule-Based Model** — explicit IF-THEN logic, like a legal statute.
> 
> All three are **interpretable**: you can *see* their reasoning. A deep neural network, by contrast, is a doctor who scribbles in 17,000 dimensions and won't show their notes.

## 1.3 Why Interpretability Matters — The KTU Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3, Unit 1):** Interpretable models are *required* in:
> 1. **High-stakes domains** — credit scoring (RBI guidelines), medical diagnosis (ICMR), criminal justice.
> 2. **Regulatory compliance** — GDPR Article 22 "right to explanation", EU AI Act high-risk classification.
> 3. **Debugging** — understanding *why* a model fails is impossible without inspecting its internals.
> 4. **Knowledge discovery** — surfacing hidden patterns the human can act upon (e.g., "smoking + age > 60 → high readmission risk").
> 5. **Trust calibration** — users must know when to override a model (model aversion vs. model over-reliance).

## 1.4 The Interpretability–Accuracy Trade-off (Briefly)

Historically, a **linear** assumption was imposed in exchange for interpretability. Modern **sparse decision trees and generalized additive models (GAMs)** challenge this, but the **trade-off curve** remains a foundational KTU concept. For the Module 3 scope, the syllabus focuses on the *intrinsically interpretable* trio: **Decision Trees, Linear Models, Rule-Based Models**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decision Trees — Operational Theory

A **Decision Tree** is a hierarchical, recursive partitioning structure that splits the feature space $\mathcal{X}$ into **axis-parallel rectangular regions** $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_M$, each assigned a single output value (class label for classification, mean value for regression).

**Core algorithmic steps (the ID3/C4.5/CART pipeline adopted in KTU curriculum):**

1. **Start at the root** with the full training dataset $S$ of size $\vert S \vert = N$.
2. **Compute impurity** of $S$ using a criterion (Entropy, Gini, or Classification Error).
3. **For every feature $X_j$** and **every candidate split point $s$**, compute the **purity gain** from splitting $S$ into $S_{left}$ and $S_{right}$.
4. **Select the split** $(X_j^*, s^*)$ that **maximizes** the gain.
5. **Recurse** on $S_{left}$ and $S_{right}$ until a stopping criterion is met (max depth, min samples per leaf, pure node).
6. **Assign** leaf node outputs: majority class (classification) or mean target (regression).

### 2.1.1 Information-Theoretic Foundations

**Entropy** $H(S)$ quantifies the **uncertainty (disorder)** in a set $S$ with respect to the class label:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

where $p_i$ is the proportion of samples in $S$ belonging to class $i$, and $c$ is the number of classes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions & Properties of Entropy:**
> - $H(S) = 0$ when $S$ is **pure** (all samples one class) — no uncertainty.
> - $H(S) = \log_2 c$ when $S$ is **maximally impure** (uniform class distribution) — maximum uncertainty.
> - For binary classification: $H(S) \in [0, 1]$ bits.
> - Convention: $0 \log 0 := 0$ (limit value, defined for computational safety).

**Conditional Entropy** $H(S \vert A)$ — the expected remaining entropy after splitting $S$ on attribute $A$:

$$H(S \vert A) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

**Information Gain** $IG(S, A)$ — the **reduction in entropy** achieved by splitting on attribute $A$:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$$

### 2.1.2 Gini Impurity (CART Criterion)

**Gini Impurity** measures the probability of **misclassification** if a label is drawn randomly according to the class distribution in $S$:

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Gini vs. Entropy:** They are **monotonically related** but **not identical**. Gini is **computationally faster** (no log) and is the **default in scikit-learn's `DecisionTreeClassifier`**. Entropy is preferred when you want the gain to reflect **information-theoretic surprise** in bits. For KTU board exams, **always state the criterion explicitly** when constructing a tree by hand.

### 2.1.3 Split Quality (Gain) for the Selected Criterion

For Gini, the gain is often written as:

$$\Delta \text{Gini}(S, A) = \text{Gini}(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \text{Gini}(S_v)$$

### 2.1.4 Overfitting Control

- **Pre-pruning (early stopping):** limit `max_depth`, `min_samples_split`, `min_samples_leaf`.
- **Post-pruning (cost-complexity pruning):** minimize $\text{Cost}_\alpha(T) = \text{Error}(T) + \alpha \cdot \vert \text{leaves}(T) \vert$, where $\alpha \geq 0$ is the **complexity parameter** (CART's approach).

## 2.2 Linear Models — Operational Theory

A **Linear Model** assumes the target $y$ is a **linear combination of features** $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_p$:

$$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p + \varepsilon$$

**Matrix form** (essential for KTU derivations):

$$\mathbf{\hat{y}} = \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

where $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (p+1)}$ is the design matrix (with column of 1s for intercept), $\boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbb{R}^{p+1}$ is the parameter vector.

### 2.2.1 Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Closed-Form Solution

The **Ordinary Least Squares** estimator minimizes the **residual sum of squares (RSS)**:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} \Vert_2^2$$

Setting the gradient to zero yields the **Normal Equations**:

$$\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$$

Provided $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$ is invertible (full column rank), the **closed-form OLS solution** is:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$$

### 2.2.2 Regularized Linear Models (KTU High-Yield)

To enforce **sparsity** (a stronger form of interpretability), the KTU syllabus emphasizes regularized variants:

**Ridge Regression (L2 penalty):**

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{ridge} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \left\{ \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} \Vert_2^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2 \right\}$$

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{ridge} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$$

**Lasso Regression (L1 penalty — produces *exact zeros* in coefficients):**

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{lasso} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \left\{ \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} \Vert_2^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert \right\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Lasso yields interpretability:** Unlike Ridge, Lasso's L1 penalty drives many $\beta_j$ to **exactly zero**, effectively performing **feature selection**. The remaining non-zero coefficients form a sparse, easy-to-explain model — e.g., "risk = 0.5 × age + 0.3 × smoker" with all other features discarded.

**Elastic Net (combined L1 + L2):**

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{EN} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \left\{ \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} \Vert_2^2 + \lambda_1 \sum_{j} \vert \beta_j \vert + \lambda_2 \sum_{j} \beta_j^2 \right\}$$

### 2.2.3 Logistic Regression (Classification)

For binary classification $y \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$P(y=1 \vert \mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{x}}}$$

The log-odds (logit) interpretation:

$$\log \frac{P(y=1)}{P(y=0)} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear-model interpretability rule:** A one-unit increase in $x_j$ multiplies the odds of $y=1$ by $e^{\beta_j}$, holding all other features fixed. This is the canonical "linear-model story" students must produce on the KTU exam.

## 2.3 Rule-Based Models — Operational Theory

A **Rule-Based Model** represents knowledge as a collection of **IF-THEN rules** of the form:

$$\text{IF } (\text{condition}_1) \text{ AND } (\text{condition}_2) \text{ AND } \cdots \text{ THEN } (\text{conclusion})$$

Formally, a rule is an ordered pair $(A, C)$ where $A = (a_1 \land a_2 \land \cdots \land a_k)$ is the **antecedent** (body) and $C$ is the **consequent** (head).

### 2.3.1 Key Quality Metrics for Rules (on dataset $\mathcal{D}$)

**Support (coverage) of a rule** $A \to C$:

$$\text{supp}(A \to C) = \frac{\vert \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{D} : A(\mathbf{x}) \text{ holds and } C(\mathbf{x}) \text{ holds} \} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert}$$

**Confidence (precision) of a rule:**

$$\text{conf}(A \to C) = \frac{\text{supp}(A \to C)}{\text{supp}(A)} = \frac{\vert \{ \mathbf{x} : A \land C \} \vert}{\vert \{ \mathbf{x} : A \} \vert}$$

**Lift (interest factor):**

$$\text{lift}(A \to C) = \frac{\text{conf}(A \to C)}{\text{supp}(C)} = \frac{P(A \land C)}{P(A) \cdot P(C)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lift interpretation (KTU exam-favorite):**
> - $\text{lift} = 1$: $A$ and $C$ are **independent** (rule is useless).
> - $\text{lift} > 1$: $A$ **positively** associated with $C$ (useful rule).
> - $\text{lift} < 1$: $A$ **negatively** associated with $C$ (avoid).

### 2.3.2 Rule-Learning Algorithms

| Algorithm | Rule Form | Search Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **OneR** | Single-attribute thresholds | Greedy on accuracy |
| **Sequential Covering (CN2, RIPPER)** | Disjunctive set of conjunctive rules | Greedy covering (separate-and-conquer) |
| **Apriori (Association Rules)** | $A \to C$ where $A, C$ are itemsets | Frequent-itemset + confidence pruning |
| **Decision Lists (Fallout)** | Ordered if-elseif chain | Beam search |

### 2.3.3 The Sequential Covering Algorithm (KTU High-Yield)

This is the canonical rule-induction procedure:

1. Initialize the **rule set** $R \leftarrow \emptyset$.
2. **Repeat** until the positive class is sufficiently covered:
   a. Learn a single rule $r$ that covers a large fraction of positive examples and few negatives.
   b. Add $r$ to $R$: $R \leftarrow R \cup \{r\}$.
   c. **Remove** all examples covered by $r$ from the training set.
3. Return $R$ (default rule for uncovered cases).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula you must memorize for KTU Module 3 exam on Interpretable Models.** Use `\vert` for absolute values to preserve markdown table syntax.

| **Model** | **Formula** | **Key Symbol** | **Range / Units** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = class-$i$ proportion | $[0, \log_2 c]$ bits | Tree impurity (ID3, C4.5) |
| Conditional Entropy | $H(S \vert A) = \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $S_v$ = subset where $A=v$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ bits | Expected post-split impurity |
| Information Gain | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$ | $IG \geq 0$ | bits | Best split selection |
| Gini Impurity | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_i p_i^2$ | $p_i$ = class-$i$ proportion | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ | Tree impurity (CART, sklearn default) |
| Gain Ratio | $GR = \frac{IG(S,A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(A)}$ | $\text{SplitInfo} = -\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | dimensionless | C4.5 normalization |
| OLS Closed Form | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$ | Invertibility of $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$ | unitless $\beta_j$ | Regression parameter estimate |
| Ridge | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$ | $\lambda \geq 0$ | unitless | L2 regularization (no sparsity) |
| Lasso | $\min \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\Vert_2^2 + \lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ | $\lambda$ tunes sparsity | unitless | L1 regularization (sparse model) |
| Logistic (sigmoid) | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $z = \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{x}$ | $(0, 1)$ | Probability for binary classification |
| Log-Odds | $\log \frac{P}{1-P} = \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{x}$ | Logit | $\mathbb{R}$ | Linear interpretation |
| Rule Support | $\text{supp}(A \to C) = \frac{\vert A \land C \vert}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert}$ | Joint coverage | $[0, 1]$ | Rule frequency |
| Rule Confidence | $\text{conf}(A \to C) = \frac{\vert A \land C \vert}{\vert A \vert}$ | Conditional | $[0, 1]$ | Rule precision |
| Lift | $\text{lift}(A \to C) = \frac{\text{conf}(A \to C)}{\text{supp}(C)}$ | Independence test | $\mathbb{R}_{>0}$ | Rule interestingness |
| Cost-Complexity | $\text{Cost}_\alpha(T) = \text{Error}(T) + \alpha \cdot \vert \text{leaves}(T) \vert$ | $\alpha \geq 0$ | depends on Error | Tree post-pruning |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Model | Industry Application | Why Interpretable? |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision Tree** | Medical diagnosis triage, credit-risk binning, ICU mortality prediction (e.g., MIMIC-III benchmark) | Doctors can follow the path of decisions |
| **Linear Regression** | Econometric forecasting, epidemiology (risk factors per unit), engineering reliability | Coefficients = effect sizes |
| **Logistic Regression** | Default-risk scoring (FICO, CIBIL), spam classification, hospital readmission | Odds ratios are explainable to regulators |
| **Rule-Based (RIPPER)** | Anti-money-laundering alert rules, telecom fraud detection, GDPR-compliant credit denial | Auditors can read the rules as statutes |
| **Apriori / FP-Growth** | Market basket analysis (recommender cross-sell), gene co-expression in bioinformatics | Association rules as hypotheses |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example Dataset (used throughout this section):** Consider the binary classification problem of predicting whether a patient has **heart disease** ($y = \text{Yes} / \text{No}$) based on three candidate features: **Chest Pain Type** (Typical / Atypical / Asymptomatic), **Cholesterol** (numeric), and **Age** (numeric). Training set has $N = 10$ patients with $6$ positive and $4$ negative cases.

| Patient | $X_1$: ChestPain | $X_2$: Cholesterol | $X_3$: Age | $y$: HeartDisease |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | Typical | High | 55 | Yes |
| P2 | Typical | High | 60 | Yes |
| P3 | Atypical | High | 40 | No |
| P4 | Asymptomatic | High | 55 | No |
| P5 | Asymptomatic | Normal | 60 | No |
| P6 | Asymptomatic | Normal | 35 | Yes |
| P7 | Atypical | Normal | 35 | Yes |
| P8 | Typical | Normal | 40 | No |
| P9 | Typical | High | 50 | No |
| P10 | Typical | Normal | 50 | Yes |

We will use this dataset to (a) compute root entropy, (b) evaluate information gain for ChestPain, (c) compare with Gini, and (d) derive one rule from it.

---

## 3.1 Derivation A — Root Entropy $H(S)$

**Step 1: Identify the class proportions.**

From the 10 patients, $6$ are positive (Yes) and $4$ are negative (No). Therefore:

$$p_{yes} = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6 \quad \text{and} \quad p_{no} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4$$

**Step 2: Apply the entropy formula.**

$$H(S) = -p_{yes} \log_2 p_{yes} - p_{no} \log_2 p_{no}$$

**Step 3: Compute the log-terms individually.**

$$\log_2 0.6 = \frac{\ln 0.6}{\ln 2} = \frac{-0.5108}{0.6931} \approx -0.7370$$

$$\log_2 0.4 = \frac{\ln 0.4}{\ln 2} = \frac{-0.9163}{0.6931} \approx -1.3219$$

**Step 4: Substitute and compute.**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -0.6 \cdot (-0.7370) - 0.4 \cdot (-1.3219) \\
&= 0.4422 + 0.5288 \\
&= 0.9710 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The root node has $\approx 0.971$ bits of uncertainty. This is the **upper bound** on the information we can extract by choosing a single split.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Conditional Entropy & Information Gain for Feature $X_1 = $ ChestPain

**Step 1: Partition $S$ by the three values of $X_1$.**

- **Typical** ($S_{Typ}$): P1, P2, P8, P9, P10 → 3 Yes, 2 No → $\vert S_{Typ} \vert = 5$
- **Atypical** ($S_{Aty}$): P3, P7 → 1 Yes, 1 No → $\vert S_{Aty} \vert = 2$
- **Asymptomatic** ($S_{Asy}$): P4, P5, P6 → 1 Yes, 2 No → $\vert S_{Asy} \vert = 3$

**Step 2: Compute entropy of each child node.**

For **$S_{Typ}$**: $p_{yes} = 3/5 = 0.6$, $p_{no} = 2/5 = 0.4$.

$$H(S_{Typ}) = -0.6 \log_2 0.6 - 0.4 \log_2 0.4 = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

For **$S_{Aty}$**: $p_{yes} = 1/2 = 0.5$, $p_{no} = 0.5$.

$$H(S_{Aty}) = -0.5 \log_2 0.5 - 0.5 \log_2 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

For **$S_{Asy}$**: $p_{yes} = 1/3 \approx 0.333$, $p_{no} = 2/3 \approx 0.667$.

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S_{Asy}) &= -\frac{1}{3} \log_2 \frac{1}{3} - \frac{2}{3} \log_2 \frac{2}{3} \\
&= \frac{1}{3} \cdot 1.585 + \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.585 \\
&\approx 0.5283 + 0.3900 \\
&\approx 0.9183 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute the weighted conditional entropy $H(S \vert X_1)$.**

$$H(S \vert X_1) = \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S \vert X_1) &= \frac{5}{10} \cdot 0.9710 + \frac{2}{10} \cdot 1.0 + \frac{3}{10} \cdot 0.9183 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 0.9710 + 0.2 \cdot 1.0 + 0.3 \cdot 0.9183 \\
&= 0.4855 + 0.2000 + 0.2755 \\
&= 0.9610 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Information Gain.**

$$IG(S, X_1) = H(S) - H(S \vert X_1) = 0.9710 - 0.9610 = 0.0100 \text{ bits}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The information gain from splitting on Chest Pain is only **0.01 bits** — a very small reduction in entropy. Chest Pain is therefore a *weak* splitter at the root. In a real ID3 run, the algorithm would check all features and pick the one with the *largest* $IG$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Gini Impurity Comparison for the Same Split

**Step 1: Compute root Gini.**

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - p_{yes}^2 - p_{no}^2 = 1 - 0.6^2 - 0.4^2 = 1 - 0.36 - 0.16 = 0.48$$

**Step 2: Compute child Gini for each subset.**

$$\text{Gini}(S_{Typ}) = 1 - (0.6)^2 - (0.4)^2 = 0.48$$

$$\text{Gini}(S_{Aty}) = 1 - (0.5)^2 - (0.5)^2 = 0.50$$

$$\text{Gini}(S_{Asy}) = 1 - \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = 1 - \frac{1}{9} - \frac{4}{9} = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.4444$$

**Step 3: Compute weighted child Gini.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gini}_{split}(X_1) &= \frac{5}{10} \cdot 0.48 + \frac{2}{10} \cdot 0.50 + \frac{3}{10} \cdot 0.4444 \\
&= 0.24 + 0.10 + 0.1333 \\
&= 0.4733
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Gini gain.**

$$\Delta \text{Gini}(S, X_1) = \text{Gini}(S) - \text{Gini}_{split}(X_1) = 0.48 - 0.4733 = 0.0067$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** Gini gain (0.0067) and Information Gain (0.01) rank the split the *same* way (both small). They will not always agree, but on average they correlate. The KTU exam accepts **either** criterion, provided the choice is **justified**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Closed-Form OLS for a 1-Feature Linear Model

Suppose we fit a linear model $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ to a 1-D regression dataset using OLS.

**Step 1: Set up the design matrix** $\mathbf{X}$ and target vector $\mathbf{y}$ for $N$ points:

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1 \\ 1 & x_2 \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_N \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_N \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Form the normal equations** $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} N & \sum x_i \\ \sum x_i & \sum x_i^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum y_i \\ \sum x_i y_i \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Solve by Cramer's rule** (the $2 \times 2$ inversion is explicit):

$$D = N \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2 = N \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$$

$$\beta_1 = \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2} = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

$$\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Result:** $\beta_1$ is the **sample covariance divided by the sample variance of $x$** — i.e., the standardized slope. This is the formula you reproduce by hand in the exam when asked to "derive the OLS estimator".

---

## 3.5 Derivation E — Rule Support, Confidence, and Lift

Suppose from the dataset above we want to evaluate the rule:

$$A \to C: \quad \text{ChestPain = Typical AND Age} \geq 50 \Rightarrow \text{HeartDisease = Yes}$$

**Step 1: Identify all rows satisfying antecedent $A$.**

Rows where ChestPain = Typical AND Age $\geq 50$: P1 (Yes, 55), P2 (Yes, 60), P10 (Yes, 50). So $\vert A \vert = 3$.

**Step 2: Identify rows satisfying $A \land C$.**

Among those 3, all have $C$ = Yes. So $\vert A \land C \vert = 3$.

**Step 3: Compute support and confidence (with $\vert \mathcal{D} \vert = 10$, $\vert C \vert = 6$).**

$$\text{supp}(A \to C) = \frac{3}{10} = 0.30$$

$$\text{conf}(A \to C) = \frac{3}{3} = 1.00 \quad (100\%)$$

$$\text{lift}(A \to C) = \frac{1.00}{0.6} \approx 1.667$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The rule has **30% support** and **100% confidence**. The lift of $1.67 > 1$ means that the combination of Typical chest pain + Age $\geq 50$ makes heart disease $1.67\times$ more likely than the base rate — a **statistically useful rule** for our example.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Model

| Criterion | Decision Tree | Linear Model | Rule-Based |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feature types** | Mixed (numeric + categorical) | Numeric mostly (one-hot for categorical) | Mixed |
| **Feature interactions** | Captured (deep trees) | Not captured (additive only) | Captured (in rule body) |
| **Linearity assumption** | None | Required (in feature space) | None |
| **Interpretability form** | Visual flowchart + path explanation | Equation + coefficient story | IF-THEN statutes |
| **Robustness to outliers** | Low (extreme values may dominate splits) | Medium (L1/Huber loss helps) | Low |
| **Scalability to high-dim** | Moderate (feature selection needed) | High (regularized) | Low (combinatorial explosion) |
| **Regulatory acceptability** | High (visual audit) | High (well-understood) | Very High (auditor-readable) |
| **Multicollinearity handling** | Inherent (selects one feature) | Needs Ridge/Elastic Net | Inherent (one condition per line) |
| **Probability calibration** | Poor (unless leaves store probs) | Excellent (logistic) | Poor (unless fuzzy rules) |
| **KTU example dataset** | Heart disease above | Boston Housing | Market basket, fraud |
