| **Plain Model + SHAP** | 🟢 High | 🔴 Low (explanations leak) | 0% | Low | High |
| **DP-SGD (Deep Learning)** | 🟡 Medium | 🟢 High ($\epsilon \leq 1$) | 2–10% | High | **Highest** |
| **Federated Learning** | 🟡 Medium | 🟢 High | 1–5% | Very High | High |
| **Global Model + Local Noise** | 🟡 Medium | 🟢 High (strong local DP) | 5–15% | Low | Medium |
| **Anonymization + Decision Rules** | 🟢 High | 🟡 Medium | 0% | Low | Medium |
| **Black-Box + Post-hoc Approximation** | 🔴 Low | 🟢 High | 0% | Low | Low |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem**: A dataset of 1000 patients has a true diabetes count of 142 in a specific age bracket. Apply Laplace mechanism with $\epsilon = 0.5$.

**Solution**:

Scale parameter:
$$b = \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2$$

Sample noise (assume $\eta = +1.3$):

$$\tilde{f}(D) = 142 + 1.3 = 143.3$$

Since we can't have fractional patients, round to **143**.

**Privacy guarantee**: Any individual's inclusion changes probability of seeing "143" by at most factor $e^{0.5} \approx 1.65$.

**Transparency cost**: Report states "≈143 patients" instead of exact "142". Public can still infer disease prevalence trends — transparency preserved.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Transparency–Privacy Balance Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rawData[Raw Sensitive Data] --> dsSelector{Data Subject Consent}
    dsSelector -->|Consented| trainSet[Training Dataset D]
    dsSelector -->|Withheld| anonPath[Anonymization Path]

    trainSet --> dpSGD[DP-SGD Training with Noise sigma]
    anonPath --> anonModel[Anonymized Aggregate Model]

    dpSGD --> modelM[Private Model M]
    anonModel --> modelM

    modelM --> queryHandler{User Query Type}

    queryHandler -->|Predict| predOut[Prediction with Calibrated Noise]
    queryHandler -->|Explain| explainGen[SHAP or LIME Explainer]
    queryHandler -->|Audit| auditLog[Encrypted Audit Log with Access Control]

    predOut --> outputFilter{Privacy Filter}
    explainGen --> outputFilter
    auditLog --> outputFilter

    outputFilter -->|Pass| finalUser[Stakeholder receives Safe Output]
    outputFilter -->|Fail| blockOut[Output Blocked and Logged]

    privacyWatchdog[Privacy Watchdog epsilon Tracker] -. monitors .-> dpSGD
    privacyWatchdog -. monitors .-> outputFilter
    transparencyAuditor[Transparency Auditor Faithfulness Score] -. monitors .-> explainGen
    transparencyAuditor -. monitors .-> auditLog

    classDef secure fill:#1a3d2e,stroke:#2dd4bf,color:#ffffff
    classDef risky fill:#3d1a1a,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    classDef neutral fill:#1a2e3d,stroke:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff
    class rawData,dsSelector risky
    class modelM,outputFilter,finalUser secure
    class trainSet,dpSGD,explainGen,auditLog neutral
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: The Trade-off Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[New AI Use Case] --> B{Critical Domain?}
    B -->|Yes Healthcare, Finance, Legal| C[Strict Privacy epsilon less than 1]
    B -->|No Marketing, Recommender| D[Relaxed Privacy epsilon up to 10]

    C --> E[Choose Global DP or Federated]
    D --> F[Choose Local DP or Anonymization]

    E --> G[High-cost explanations OK LIME, SHAP, Counterfactuals]
    F --> H[Cheap explanations OK Feature Importance]

    G --> I[Deploy with Monitoring + Annual Audit]
    F --> I

    I --> J{Privacy Breach Detected?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Retrain with Lower epsilon]
    J -->|No| L[Continue Operation]

    classDef high fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,color:#ffffff
    classDef med fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff
    classDef low fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,color:#000000
    class C,G high
    class D,F,H med
    class I,J,L,K low
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction Table

| Stage | Transparency Mechanism | Privacy Mechanism | Conflict Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Collection** | Audit logs | Consent forms, minimization | Over-collection hurts privacy |
| **Training** | Reproducible seeds | DP-SGD, Secure Aggregation | Noise reduces reproducibility |
| **Inference** | Confidence scores | Output perturbation | Noisy scores confuse users |
| **Explanation** | SHAP values | Explanation noise, abstraction | Detail leaks training data |
| **Deployment** | Public model cards | Access control, encryption | Documentation reveals architecture |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between Explainability and Interpretability in AI systems. Why is the distinction important when balancing transparency with privacy?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Interpretability (1 mark)**: Refers to a model's *intrinsic* property — a human can directly trace how inputs become outputs (e.g., linear regression weights, short decision trees).
- **Explainability (1 mark)**: A *post-hoc* capability where an external method (SHAP, LIME) generates human-understandable justifications for any model, including black-boxes.
- **Why distinction matters (1 mark)**: Interpretable models often expose *exact* internals (privacy risk), while explainable models can be **abstracted** to expose reasoning without revealing weights — better for privacy.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define Differential Privacy. State the role of the privacy budget $\epsilon$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks)**: A mathematical framework ensuring that the *inclusion or exclusion of any single record* in a dataset causes only a bounded, negligible change in the output of any analysis.
- **Privacy Budget $\epsilon$ (1.5 marks)**: A non-negative real number controlling the maximum allowable information leakage. **Smaller $\epsilon$ ⇒ stronger privacy, more noise, less accuracy.** Typical values: $\epsilon \in [0.1, 10]$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students often (1) confuse $\epsilon$-DP with $k$-anonymity, (2) forget to state the **sensitivity $\Delta f$** when applying the Laplace mechanism, and (3) skip the *composition* theorem when releasing multiple statistics. Each omission costs **2–3 marks**. Always show all three steps: **(a) sensitivity → (b) noise scale → (c) privacy guarantee**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **Transparency–Privacy Trade-off** in AI systems with a real-world healthcare example. Discuss why both requirements are non-negotiable under modern data protection laws. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital wants to publish the average billing amount across 5 departments. The true averages (in ₹ thousands) are `[42, 58, 31, 67, 49]`. Global sensitivity is $\Delta f = 5$. Using the **Laplace mechanism** with $\epsilon = 1.0$, derive the noised averages and compute the **total privacy budget consumed**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

1. **Definition (2 marks)**: Transparency–Privacy trade-off is the engineering and ethical challenge of designing AI systems that are simultaneously auditable (transparent) and confidentiality-preserving (private). They conflict because transparency often requires revealing model internals or training data, while privacy demands concealing the same.

2. **Healthcare Example (3 marks)**: Consider an AI system that predicts patient readmission risk.
   - *Transparency demand*: Doctors need to know *why* a patient is flagged high-risk (e.g., based on prior diagnoses, vitals).
   - *Privacy demand*: The model must not memorize or leak other patients' records, and the explanation must not allow reconstruction of training data via model inversion attacks.
   - Tools used: SHAP for explanations + DP-SGD during training to bound privacy leakage at $\epsilon \le 1$.

3. **Legal Non-Negotiability (2 marks)**:
   - **GDPR Art. 22**: Right to explanation for automated decisions.
   - **HIPAA / India's DPDP Act 2023**: Mandatory data protection with informed consent.
   - Both laws apply simultaneously, so neither property can be sacrificed.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Compute noise scale (1 mark)**

$$b = \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon} = \frac{5}{1.0} = 5$$

**Step 2: Sample Laplace noise for each department (2 marks)**

Assume sampled noise vector: $\eta = [+2.1, -3.4, +0.8, -1.9, +4.2]$

**Step 3: Compute noised averages (2 marks)**

$$\tilde{f} = [42+2.1, \; 58-3.4, \; 31+0.8, \; 67-1.9, \; 49+4.2] = [44.1, \; 54.6, \; 31.8, \; 65.1, \; 53.2]$$

**Step 4: Total privacy budget via basic composition (2 marks)**

$$\epsilon_{\text{total}} = k \cdot \epsilon = 5 \times 1.0 = 5.0$$

**Conclusion**: The hospital consumed a privacy budget of $\epsilon = 5$, suitable for internal analytics. For public release, they should reduce per-query $\epsilon$ to $0.2$ so that $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = 1.0$ — the gold standard for sensitive health data.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Federated Learning** and **Differential Privacy** as two approaches to balancing transparency with privacy. Mention at least **three distinct comparison criteria**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An e-commerce recommender system uses 3 features: `age`, `income`, `purchase_history`. Apply **Global Differential Privacy** to release the **mean income** of users with sensitivity $\Delta f = 200$ and $\epsilon = 0.5$. Show the **Laplace mechanism** derivation step by step. Compute the noise scale and provide a **numerical example** of a noised output given a true mean of ₹55,000. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

| Criterion | Federated Learning (FL) | Differential Privacy (DP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Privacy Mechanism (2 marks)** | Data never leaves device; only model updates are shared | Calibrated noise added to outputs or gradients |
| **Transparency (1.5 marks)** | Server can audit gradient updates; users cannot see global model logic | Output is mathematically private but explanations may be noisy |
| **Computational Cost (1.5 marks)** | High — many clients, communication overhead | Moderate — mainly noise sampling overhead |
| **Adversary Resilience (1 mark)** | Vulnerable to gradient inversion attacks; needs DP-on-FL | Provable guarantee against any post-hoc attack |
| **Use Case Fit (1 mark)** | Mobile keyboards (Gboard), hospitals across regions | Census data release, public health statistics |

**Verdict**: Modern best practice is **FL + DP combined** (e.g., DP-FedAvg in Google Gboard).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1: State the mechanism (1 mark)**

$$\tilde{f}(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)$$

**Step 2: Compute the scale parameter (2 marks)**

$$b = \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon} = \frac{200}{0.5} = 400$$

So $\eta \sim \text{Lap}(400)$ with PDF:

$$p(\eta) = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 400} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert \eta \vert}{400}\right) = \frac{1}{800} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert \eta \vert}{400}\right)$$

**Step 3: Sample a noise value (1 mark)**

Assume we draw $\eta = +285$ (a single realization).

**Step 4: Compute noised mean income (2 marks)**

$$\tilde{f}(D) = 55{,}000 + 285 = 55{,}285 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 5: Privacy interpretation (1 mark)**

Any single user's income contribution can change the probability of seeing "55285" by at most a factor of $e^{0.5} \approx 1.65$, providing a **mathematical privacy guarantee** while still giving a transparent, useful aggregate statistic for business decisions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ✅

- 🔑 **Transparency ≠ Interpretability**: Explainability is post-hoc; interpretability is intrinsic.
- 🔑 **Differential Privacy is the gold standard** for mathematical privacy.
- 🔑 **Privacy Budget $\epsilon$**: smaller = stronger privacy but lower utility.
- 🔑 **Sensitivity $\Delta f$** must ALWAYS be computed before applying Laplace or Gaussian mechanism.
- 🔑 **Three pillars of balance**: (1) Stakeholder consent, (2) calibrated noise, (3) audit trails.
- 🔑 **Federated Learning + DP** is the modern production-grade hybrid (Google, Apple use it).
- 🔑 **GDPR Art. 22** = legal foundation of "Right to Explanation."
- 🔑 **Membership Inference Attack (MIA)** is the key adversary threat to test against.
- 🔑 **Trade-off is irreducible** — you cannot have *both* perfect transparency AND perfect privacy; the goal is *optimal balance* under context-specific risk.
- 🔑 **KTU Exam Pattern**: Always state **mechanism → sensitivity → noise scale → privacy bound**, in that order.
- 🔑 **Composition theorem**: $k$ queries with $\epsilon$ each consume $k\epsilon$ in basic, less in advanced composition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Responsible AI Practices - Principles of Responsible AI (Fairness, Accountability, Transparency)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Responsible AI Practices - Principles of Responsible AI (Fairness, Accountability, Transparency) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Responsible AI? — Formal Definition **Responsible AI (RAI)** is a governance and engineering framework that ensures Artificial Intelligence systems are designed, developed, deployed, and monitored in a manner that is **ethical, lawful, technically robust, and socially beneficial**. It is operationalized through a set of interlocking principles — most prominently **Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAT / FAccT)** — that translate abstract human values into measurable engineering practices. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCITT602 - Module 4):** > Responsible AI refers to the practice of designing, building, and deploying AI systems in a way that produces equitable outcomes, enables stakeholders to understand and contest system behavior, and assigns clear human/ organisational responsibility for AI-driven decisions. ## 1.2 The FAT Triad — Core Principles | Principle | One-Line Definition | Engineering Focus | |---|---|---| | **Fairness** | AI must treat individuals and groups equitably, without discriminatory bias. | Bias detection, debiasing, group/individual fairness metrics | | **Accountability** | There must be clear ownership and answerability for AI outcomes. | Audit trails, governance roles, redressal mechanisms | | **Transparency** | AI behavior and decisions must be explainable and visible to stakeholders. | Explainability (XAI), model cards, datasheets, disclosure | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Car" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Autonomous Vehicle on a Public Road** > > Imagine an AI system as a **self-driving car** navigating a busy city: > - **Fairness** → The car must not prioritize saving one pedestrian over another based on age, gender, or income (the *trolley problem* of ML). > - **Accountability** → If the car crashes, there must be a **clear owner** — the manufacturer, the software vendor, the human supervisor — who can be questioned, fined, or held liable. > - **Transparency** → The car must have a **"black box"** that records why it braked or swerved, and the algorithm must be explainable to the regulator, the passenger, and the victim's family. > > Without these three, citizens cannot *trust* the technology, and regulators cannot *license* it. ## 1.4 Why Responsible AI Matters in 2024+ 1. **Regulatory Pressure**: EU AI Act (2024), India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA 2023), NIST AI RMF (US), OECD AI Principles. 2. **Real-World Harm**: Amazon's recruitment AI (gender bias, 2018), COMPAS recidivism algorithm (racial bias, ProPublica 2016), Apple Card credit limit gender discrimination (2019). 3. **Trust as a Deployment Barrier**: According to the **Edelman Trust Barometer**, only **35%** of global respondents trust AI systems — transparency directly correlates with adoption. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** Think **F-A-T** as the **"Three Pillars of Trust"** — without any one, the AI system **collapses into a black box that society will reject or regulate out of existence.** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Principle 1 — FAIRNESS (Detailed Analysis) **Fairness** in AI is the absence of *unjust differential treatment* of individuals or groups based on protected attributes such as race, gender, age, caste, religion, disability, or socioeconomic status. ### 2.1.1 Types of Fairness | Fairness Type | Formal Name | Definition | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Group Fairness** | Demographic Parity | The probability of a positive prediction is independent of the protected attribute $A$. | Hiring, lending | | **Equal Opportunity** | Equalized Odds (TPR) | True Positive Rate is equal across groups. | Medical diagnosis, college admission | | **Predictive Parity** | Calibration | Predicted probability reflects true probability equally across groups. | Risk scoring (COMPAS-style) | | **Individual Fairness** | Lipschitz Consistency | Similar individuals receive similar outcomes. | Recommendation systems | | **Counterfactual Fairness** | Causal Fairness | Outcome remains unchanged in a counterfactual world where the protected attribute is flipped. | Hiring, healthcare | | **Procedural Fairness** | Process Fairness | The decision-making *process* is consistent and unbiased (not just outcomes). | Legal/judicial AI | ### 2.1.2 Sources of Bias in AI - **Historical Bias** — Pre-existing societal bias in training data. - **Representation Bias** — Under-representation of certain groups in datasets. - **Measurement Bias** — Features or labels measured differently across groups. - **Aggregation Bias** — One-size-fits-all model for diverse sub-populations. - **Learning Bias** — Model amplifies patterns present in imbalanced data. - **Evaluation Bias** — Benchmark datasets over-represent majority groups. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** The **"Impossibility Theorem"** of Fairness (Chouldechova 2017, Kleinberg et al. 2016) states that *except in degenerate cases*, **calibration, equalized odds, and demographic parity cannot all hold simultaneously** for a non-trivial classifier. This is a guaranteed 3-mark question. ## 2.2 Principle 2 — ACCOUNTABILITY (Detailed Analysis) **Accountability** is the principle that an *identifiable human or organizational agent* bears responsibility for the behavior, decisions, and outcomes of an AI system, and that mechanisms exist for **explanation, redress, and audit**. ### 2.2.1 The Accountability Stack (Layered Model) | Layer | Mechanism | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **L1 — Role Layer** | Defined roles (Model Owner, Data Steward, Reviewer) | Assigns *who* is responsible | | **L2 — Process Layer** | Documentation, sign-offs, Model Risk Management (MRM) | Defines *how* responsibility is exercised | | **L3 — Evidence Layer** | Logs, audit trails, model cards, datasheets | Provides *proof* of responsible behavior | | **L4 — Redress Layer** | Grievance channels, appeals, human-in-the-loop overrides | Enables *correction* of harms | | **L5 — Regulatory Layer** | GDPR Article 22, EU AI Act, DPDPA | Enforces *external* accountability | ### 2.2.2 Components of Accountability - **Answerability** — Ability to demand and receive explanations. - **Auditability** — Capacity to inspect, reproduce, and evaluate. - **Remediation** — Mechanisms to fix harm (compensation, rollback, retraining). - **Liability Allocation** — Legal attribution of fault (developer, deployer, user). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** Accountability is *not* the same as **Transparency**. A system can be transparent (you can see the code) but still have *no one accountable* if no human role is mapped. Accountability requires **human ownership**, not just visibility. ## 2.3 Principle 3 — TRANSPARENCY (Detailed Analysis) **Transparency** is the property that the **inputs, behavior, and outputs** of an AI system are *understandable, interpretable, and accessible* to relevant stakeholders (developers, regulators, end-users, affected persons). ### 2.3.1 Three Layers of Transparency | Layer | Question Answered | Technique | |---|---|---| | **Model Transparency (Simulatability)** | Can a human simulate the model? | Linear models, decision trees, rule-based systems | | **Algorithmic Transparency (Decomposability)** | Can we decompose every component? | Inspectable architectures, open-source code | | **Post-hoc Explainability (Transparency-after-deployment)** | Can we explain a *specific* prediction? | SHAP, LIME, Grad-CAM, counterfactual explanations | ### 2.3.2 Explainability Techniques (XAI Taxonomy) - **Local Explanations** — Explain one prediction (LIME, SHAP, Counterfactuals). - **Global Explanations** — Explain the whole model behavior (feature importance, partial dependence plots). - **Model-Agnostic** — Apply to any model (LIME, SHAP). - **Model-Specific** — Designed for a particular architecture (Grad-CAM for CNNs, attention rollout for Transformers). - **Example-Based** — Use prototypes, criticisms, or influence functions. ### 2.3.3 Transparency Artifacts (Documentation Standards) - **Model Cards** (Mitchell et al., 2019) — Standardized reporting of model performance, intended use, limitations. - **Datasheets for Datasets** (Gebru et al., 2018) — Documentation of data provenance, collection, ethical considerations. - **System Cards** (Meta, 2022) — Holistic system-level disclosures. - **AI FactSheets** (IBM) — Supplier-declared characteristics of AI services. - **Transparency Notes** (Microsoft, Google) — Public-facing plain-language explanations. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Quantitative Metrics) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bias Quantification Equations (Probability & Set Notation).** > All metrics below use $A$ = protected attribute, $\hat{Y}$ = predicted outcome, $Y$ = true outcome. | Metric | Mathematical Definition | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | **Statistical (Demographic) Parity Difference** | $\text{SPD} = \vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) \vert$ | Ideal: 0. Measures gap in positive prediction rates between groups. | | **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** | $\text{DIR} = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0)}$ | Ideal: $\approx 1$. The "80% Rule" (EEOC): DIR $\geq 0.8$ is legally considered non-discriminatory. | | **Equal Opportunity Difference** | $\text{EOD} = \vert \text{TPR}_{A=0} - \text{TPR}_{A=1} \vert$ | Ideal: 0. Equality of true positive rates (recall) across groups. | | **Predictive Parity Difference** | $\text{PPD} = \vert \text{PPV}_{A=0} - \text{PPV}_{A=1} \vert$ | Ideal: 0. Equality of precision across groups. | | **Equalized Odds** | $\text{TPR}_{A=0} = \text{TPR}_{A=1}$ **and** $\text{FPR}_{A=0} = \text{FPR}_{A=1}$ | Strongest group fairness — equal error rates. | | **Counterfactual Fairness** | $P(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a} = 1 \mid X, A=a) = P(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'} = 1 \mid X, A=a)$ | Outcome unchanged under counterfactual protected attribute. | | **Individual Fairness (Lipschitz)** | $d(\hat{Y}(x_i), \hat{Y}(x_j)) \leq L \cdot d(x_i, x_j)$ | Similar inputs $\Rightarrow$ similar outputs. $L$ is the Lipschitz constant. | | **SHAP Value** | $\phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \dfrac{\vert S \vert ! \, (\vert F \vert - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{\vert F \vert !} \big[ f(S \cup \{i\}) - f(S) \big]$ | Marginal contribution of feature $i$ to a prediction, over all feature subsets $S$. | | **LIME Local Fidelity** | $\min_{g \in G} \; \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_{x}) + \Omega(g)$ | Find interpretable model $g$ that locally approximates black-box $f$ around $x$. | | **Information Disclosure Score** | $\text{IDS} = \dfrac{\text{Bits of disclosed information}}{\text{Total bits required for full reconstruction}}$ | Measures transparency completeness (0 = opaque, 1 = fully transparent). | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are actively used in **MLOps pipelines** (e.g., **IBM AI Fairness 360**, **Google What-If Tool**, **Microsoft Fairlearn**, **Aequitas**) to flag models that violate regulatory thresholds *before* deployment. They are the practical bridge between ethical principles and engineering practice. ## 2.5 Inter-Pillar Relationships (Conceptual Map) - **Fairness + Transparency** → Detecting bias requires being able to *see* the model. - **Fairness + Accountability** → Audits must be tied to *who* fixes the bias. - **Accountability + Transparency** → Explanation is the *mechanism* through which answerability is exercised. - **All Three + Interpretability** → Interpretability is the *tool* that operationalizes transparency and enables accountability. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Note for KTU Students:** This topic is conceptual; instead of algebraic derivations, this section provides **structured analytical derivations** of the **bias-fairness relationship**, the **confusion-matrix decomposition behind equalized odds**, and the **step-by-step application of the 80% Rule** — all of which are routinely asked in 14-mark KTU questions. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Confusion Matrix to Equalized Odds **Objective:** Show how equal opportunity difference is *mathematically derived* from the standard $2 \times 2$ confusion matrix segmented by protected attribute $A$. ### Step 1 — Define the Standard Confusion Matrix For a binary classifier, the $2 \times 2$ confusion matrix on a group with $A = a$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TP}_a &= \sum_{i : A_i = a} \mathbb{1}[\hat{Y}_i = 1 \wedge Y_i = 1] \\ \text{FP}_a &= \sum_{i : A_i = a} \mathbb{1}[\hat{Y}_i = 1 \wedge Y_i = 0] \\ \text{FN}_a &= \sum_{i : A_i = a} \mathbb{1}[\hat{Y}_i = 0 \wedge Y_i = 1] \\ \text{TN}_a &= \sum_{i : A_i = a} \mathbb{1}[\hat{Y}_i = 0 \wedge Y_i = 0] \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 1 explanation: We define the four counts of classifier outcomes for every individual in group $a$.)* ### Step 2 — Define the Group-Specific Positive Prediction Rate $$ \begin{aligned} P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = a) = \dfrac{\text{TP}_a + \text{FP}_a}{n_a} \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 2 explanation: This is the fraction of group $a$ receiving a positive prediction — the empirical estimate of the demographic parity numerator.)* ### Step 3 — Define the Group-Specific True Positive Rate (Recall) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TPR}_a = \dfrac{\text{TP}_a}{\text{TP}_a + \text{FN}_a} \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 3 explanation: This is the probability of *correctly* identifying a true positive — central to equalized odds.)* ### Step 4 — Define the Group-Specific False Positive Rate $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FPR}_a = \dfrac{\text{FP}_a}{\text{FP}_a + \text{TN}_a} \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 4 explanation: This is the probability of *incorrectly* flagging a negative instance as positive — the "false alarm" rate.)* ### Step 5 — Construct the Equalized Odds Condition $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Equalized Odds} \iff \text{TPR}_{A=0} = \text{TPR}_{A=1} \;\wedge\; \text{FPR}_{A=0} = \text{FPR}_{A=1} \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 5 explanation: The classifier satisfies equalized odds iff the *true positive rate* and *false positive rate* are equal across the two groups.)* ### Step 6 — Scalar Form: Equal Opportunity Difference $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EOD} = \vert \text{TPR}_{A=0} - \text{TPR}_{A=1} \vert \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 6 explanation: The absolute difference is a *scalar summary* — closer to 0 means fairer. A model with $\text{EOD} = 0.15$ means a 15-percentage-point gap in qualified-detection rates between groups.)* ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Disparate Impact (80% Rule) Computation **Use Case:** KTU frequently asks: *"An AI hiring tool accepts 40 out of 100 male applicants and 20 out of 100 female applicants. Is it fair under the 80% rule?"* ### Step 1 — Define Selection Rates $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SR}_{\text{male}} = \dfrac{40}{100} = 0.40 \\ \text{SR}_{\text{female}} = \dfrac{20}{100} = 0.20 \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 1 explanation: The selection rate is the proportion of applicants in each group who receive a positive prediction.)* ### Step 2 — Apply the Disparate Impact Ratio Formula $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DIR} = \dfrac{\min(\text{SR}_{\text{male}}, \text{SR}_{\text{female}})}{\max(\text{SR}_{\text{male}}, \text{SR}_{\text{female}})} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.40} = 0.50 \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 2 explanation: We always divide the smaller rate by the larger rate so the result is $\leq 1$.)* ### Step 3 — Compare with the 4/5ths (80%) Rule $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DIR} = 0.50 < 0.80 \implies \textbf{Adverse Impact Detected} \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 3 explanation: Under the **Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures (UGESP, 1978)**, a DIR below 0.80 is legally considered evidence of adverse impact. The hiring tool is **unfair**.)* ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SHAP Value Marginal Contribution (Fairness Attribution) **Objective:** Show how a SHAP value is *additively derived* — frequently asked in 14-mark questions on XAI transparency. ### Step 1 — Define the Model as a Set Function Treat the model $f$ as a function $f : 2^{F} \to \mathbb{R}$ over subsets $S$ of the feature set $F$. $$ \begin{aligned} f(S) = \text{model output when only features in } S \text{ are known} \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 1 explanation: When a subset of features is "known," the rest are marginalized out.)* ### Step 2 — Define the Marginal Contribution of Feature $i$ $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta_i(S) = f(S \cup \{i\}) - f(S) \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 2 explanation: This is the *change* in output when feature $i$ is added to the known set $S$.)* ### Step 3 — Average Over All Permutations (Shapley Weighting) $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} w(\vert S \vert) \cdot \Delta_i(S), \quad \text{where } w(\vert S \vert) = \dfrac{\vert S \vert ! \, (\vert F \vert - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{\vert F \vert !} \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 3 explanation: Shapley value from cooperative game theory — each feature $i$ gets credit equal to its *average* marginal contribution over all coalitions.)* ### Step 4 — Local Accuracy (Sum-to-Property) $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{i=1}^{\vert F \vert} \phi_i = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(X)] \end{aligned} $$ *(Row 4 explanation: The SHAP values of all features sum to the difference between the prediction and the expected model output — this is the *efficiency* axiom that makes SHAP a valid attribution.)* ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operationalizing FAT in Engineering Pipelines | MLOps Stage | Fairness Action | Accountability Action | Transparency Action | |---|---|---|---| | **Problem Framing** | Define protected attributes, stakeholders | Assign Project Owner, Ethics Reviewer | Publish problem statement, intended use | | **Data Collection** | Audit representation; sample for subgroups | Document data provenance (Datasheets) | Disclose collection methodology | | **Model Training** | Use fairness-constrained learning (e.g., adversarial debiasing) | Log hyperparameter sign-offs | Track data lineage (e.g., DVC) | | **Evaluation** | Compute SPD, DIR, EOD per subgroup | Define reviewer for sign-off | Publish Model Card with metrics | | **Deployment** | Run pre-deployment bias audit | Activate rollback responsibility chain | Publish public Transparency Note | | **Monitoring** | Continuous drift & bias monitoring | Incident response playbook | Quarterly public reports | | **Redress** | Provide appeal mechanism | Grievance officer, human override | Document appeals & outcomes | ## 3.5 Impossibility Theorem Statement (Conceptual Derivation) The **Chouldechova (2017)** and **Kleinberg, Mullainathan & Raghavan (2016)** results show: For a non-trivial classifier with two groups ($A = 0, 1$) and base rates $P(Y=1 \mid A=0) \neq P(Y=1 \mid A=1)$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Calibration} \wedge \text{Equalized Odds} \wedge \text{Demographic Parity} \to \text{Contradiction} \end{aligned} $$ *(Explanation: All three cannot hold simultaneously unless base rates are equal or one group is trivial. This is a fundamental *trade-off* — fairness is not a single number.)* # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The FAT Triad Architecture (Pillars of Responsible AI) This diagram shows the **three pillars** of Responsible AI and their supporting **operational mechanisms**. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeRAI["RESPONSIBLE AI"]:::root nodeF["FAIRNESS"]:::fair nodeA["ACCOUNTABILITY"]:::acct nodeT["TRANSPARENCY"]:::trans nodeF1["Bias Detection
Aequitas, Fairlearn"]:::mech nodeF2["Group Fairness Metrics
SPD, DIR, EOD"]:::mech nodeF3["Debiasing Algorithms
Reweighing, Adversarial"]:::mech nodeA1["Governance Roles
Model Owner, Reviewer"]:::mech nodeA2["Audit Trails
MLOps Logs, MRM"]:::mech nodeA3["Redress Mechanisms
Appeals, Human Override"]:::mech nodeT1["Explainability XAI
SHAP, LIME, GradCAM"]:::mech nodeT2["Documentation
Model Card, Datasheet"]:::mech nodeT3["Stakeholder Disclosure
Transparency Notes"]:::mech nodeReg["REGULATORY LAYER
EU AI Act, DPDPA, NIST AI RMF"]:::reg nodeSoc["SOCIETAL TRUST
Adoption, Legitimacy"]:::outcome nodeRAI --> nodeF nodeRAI --> nodeA nodeRAI --> nodeT nodeF --> nodeF1 nodeF --> nodeF2 nodeF --> nodeF3 nodeA --> nodeA1 nodeA --> nodeA2 nodeA --> nodeA3 nodeT --> nodeT1 nodeT --> nodeT2 nodeT --> nodeT3 nodeF --> nodeReg nodeA --> nodeReg nodeT --> nodeReg nodeReg --> nodeSoc classDef root fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef fair fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef acct fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef trans fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef mech fill:#f3f4f6,stroke:#6b7280,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937 classDef reg fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef outcome fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Fairness Lifecycle in MLOps (Where Each Principle Applies) ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["1. PROBLEM FRAMING"]:::stage node2["2. DATA COLLECTION"]:::stage node3["3. MODEL TRAINING"]:::stage node4["4. EVALUATION"]:::stage node5["5. DEPLOYMENT"]:::stage node6["6. MONITORING"]:::stage node7["7. REDRESS"]:::stage f1["Define protected attributes"]:::fairTag f2["Audit representation"]:::fairTag f3["Fairness constraints"]:::fairTag f4["Compute SPD DIR EOD"]:::fairTag f5["Pre-deployment audit"]:::fairTag f6["Drift and bias monitoring"]:::fairTag f7["Appeal mechanism"]:::fairTag a1["Assign owners"]:::acctTag a2["Datasheets for datasets"]:::acctTag a3["Log training runs"]:::acctTag a4["Reviewer sign off"]:::acctTag a5["Rollback chain"]:::acctTag a6["Incident playbook"]:::acctTag a7["Grievance officer"]:::acctTag t1["Stakeholder disclosure"]:::transTag t2["Data lineage"]:::transTag t3["Track experiments"]:::transTag t4["Model Card"]:::transTag t5["Transparency Note"]:::transTag t6["Quarterly reports"]:::transTag t7["Document appeals"]:::transTag node1 -.- f1 node1 -.- a1 node1 -.- t1 node2 -.- f2 node2 -.- a2 node2 -.- t2 node3 -.- f3 node3 -.- a3 node3 -.- t3 node4 -.- f4 node4 -.- a4 node4 -.- t4 node5 -.- f5 node5 -.- a5 node5 -.- t5 node6 -.- f6 node6 -.- a6 node6 -.- t6 node7 -.- f7 node7 -.- a7 node7 -.- t7 classDef stage fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef fairTag fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#1f2937 classDef acctTag fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1f2937 classDef transTag fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#1f2937 ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The XAI Transparency Stack (From Black Box to Glass Box) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeBB["BLACK BOX MODEL
Deep Neural Network Ensemble"]:::blackbox nodePMT["Post Hoc Model Specific
GradCAM Saliency Maps"]:::layer nodePMAG["Post Hoc Model Agnostic
LIME SHAP Anchors"]:::layer nodeGI["Global Interpretable
Feature Importance PDP"]:::layer nodeLT["Locally Transparent
Surrogate Models"]:::layer nodeIT["Intrinsically Transparent
Linear Trees Rule Lists"]:::layer nodeGB["GLASS BOX
Fully Auditable"]:::glass nodeBB --> nodePMT nodePMT --> nodePMAG nodePMAG --> nodeGI nodeGI --> nodeLT nodeLT --> nodeIT nodeIT --> nodeGB classDef blackbox fill:#1f2937,stroke:#ef4444,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef glass fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#10b981,stroke-width:3px,color:#1f2937 classDef layer fill:#f3f4f6,stroke:#6b7280,color:#1f2937 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The arrows show the *transparency gradient*. Movement from **Black Box → Glass Box** represents increasing explainability but typically *decreasing* predictive power. Real-world systems often balance on the **Post-hoc Model-Agnostic** layer (SHAP/LIME), which is why these techniques are emphasized in the KTU syllabus. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper] **Define Responsible AI. List its three core principles and briefly explain why each is necessary.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Responsible AI is the practice of designing, developing, and deploying AI systems in a manner that is ethical, lawful, technically robust, and aligned with human values. Its three core principles are: 1. **Fairness (1 mark):** Ensures AI systems do not discriminate against individuals or groups based on protected attributes (race, gender, caste, etc.) and that outcomes are equitable across demographic segments. 2. **Accountability (1 mark):** Establishes clear human and organizational ownership of AI behavior, ensuring that someone is answerable for harms, with mechanisms for explanation, audit, and redress. 3. **Transparency (1 mark):** Mandates that AI systems be explainable and their behavior visible to stakeholders, enabling trust, scrutiny, and informed consent. These principles are necessary to prevent real-world harms (biased hiring, discriminatory lending, opaque decisions), comply with regulations (EU AI Act, DPDPA), and build public trust in AI adoption. --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper] **What is the Disparate Impact Ratio? State the 80% Rule and explain its significance in algorithmic fairness.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** The Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR) is a quantitative measure of group fairness defined as the ratio of selection (positive prediction) rates between the advantaged and disadvantaged groups. **Formula (1 mark):** $$ \text{DIR} = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{disadvantaged})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{advantaged})} $$ **80% Rule and Significance (1 mark):** Under the **Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures (UGESP, 1978)** and adopted in algorithmic contexts, a DIR $\geq 0.80$ is considered non-discriminatory, while DIR $< 0.80$ indicates **adverse impact** and triggers legal/ethical scrutiny. Its significance is that it operationalizes *disparate impact discrimination* — a model can be facially neutral but still produce discriminatory outcomes, and the 80% rule provides a quantitative legal threshold. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule (ESE):** *Each Module in the End Semester Examination carries internal choice — students answer **ONE** full question (with sub-parts a and b) from the module. The total is **14 marks** per question, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.* --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Question #### (a) Explain in detail the different types of fairness in AI systems. Discuss the Impossibility Theorem with suitable mathematical formulation. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** Fairness in AI is the absence of systematic favoritism or discrimination against individuals or groups based on protected attributes such as race, gender, age, or socioeconomic status. There is no single "fairness" — different mathematical definitions capture different ethical intuitions. **Types of Fairness (4 marks):** 1. **Group Fairness (Demographic Parity / Statistical Parity):** A classifier satisfies demographic parity if the probability of a positive prediction is independent of the protected attribute $A$. $$ P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 1) $$ *Use Case:* Hiring, college admissions. 2. **Equal Opportunity (Equalized Odds):** A classifier satisfies equalized odds if the true positive rate and false positive rate are equal across groups. $$ P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1, A = 0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1, A = 1) $$ *Use Case:* Medical diagnosis, loan approval for creditworthy applicants. 3. **Predictive Parity (Calibration):** The positive predictive value (precision) is equal across groups. $$ P(Y = 1 \mid \hat{Y} = 1, A = 0) = P(Y = 1 \mid \hat{Y} = 1, A = 1) $$ *Use Case:* Risk scoring systems (e.g., COMPAS). 4. **Individual Fairness (Lipschitz Consistency):** Similar individuals receive similar outcomes. $$ d_Y(\hat{Y}(x_i), \hat{Y}(x_j)) \leq L \cdot d_X(x_i, x_j) $$ *Use Case:* Recommendation systems, personalized services. 5. **Counterfactual Fairness:** The outcome remains unchanged in a counterfactual world where the protected attribute is flipped. $$ P(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a} = 1 \mid X, A = a) = P(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'} = 1 \mid X, A = a) $$ *Use Case:* Causal models in healthcare and employment. **Impossibility Theorem (2 marks):** Chouldechova (2017) and Kleinberg, Mullainathan, and Raghavan (2016) proved the **impossibility theorem** for fairness metrics: > For a non-trivial binary classifier operating on two groups with **unequal base rates** $P(Y=1 \mid A=0) \neq P(Y=1 \mid A=1)$, it is **mathematically impossible** to simultaneously satisfy all three of: > - Calibration (Predictive Parity) > - Equalized Odds > - Demographic Parity The result follows from the algebraic identity linking positive predictive value (PPV), true positive rate (TPR), and base rate $p$: $$ \text{PPV} = \dfrac{\text{TPR} \cdot p}{\text{TPR} \cdot p + \text{FPR} \cdot (1-p)} $$ If the base rate $p$ differs across groups, then enforcing equal TPR and FPR across groups forces PPV to differ. The ethical implication is that *fairness is a choice*, not a computation — practitioners must pick the metric that aligns with the application context and disclose the trade-off. --- #### (b) Explain the principle of Transparency in Responsible AI. Discuss SHAP and LIME as post-hoc explainability techniques with their working principles. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Definition of Transparency (1 mark):** Transparency is the property that the **inputs, internal behavior, and outputs** of an AI system are understandable, interpretable, and accessible to relevant stakeholders. It is the *enabling condition* for accountability — without visibility, no one can be held responsible. **Three Layers of Transparency (2 marks):** | Layer | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Model Transparency (Simulatability)** | A human can mentally simulate the model step by step | Linear regression, small decision trees | | **Algorithmic Transparency (Decomposability)** | Each component of the algorithm is inspectable | Open-source code, modular pipelines | | **Post-hoc Explainability** | A deployed model can be explained for any prediction | SHAP, LIME, counterfactuals | **LIME (Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations) (2 marks):** LIME explains a *single* prediction $f(x)$ of a black-box model by fitting a **simple interpretable surrogate model** $g$ (e.g., linear regression) in the local neighborhood of $x$. **Working principle:** $$ \xi(x) = \arg\min_{g \in G} \; \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g) $$ where: - $\mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x)$ is the fidelity loss — how well $g$ mimics $f$ near $x$ (weighted by proximity $\pi_x$). - $\Omega(g)$ is the complexity penalty — keeps $g$ simple (e.g., number of non-zero weights). - $G$ is the family of interpretable models. LIME perturbs the input $x$, queries the black-box $f$ on the perturbations, weighs them by proximity, and fits $g$ to the predictions. The result is a local linear explanation. **SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) (2 marks):** SHAP unifies LIME and several other methods under the **Shapley value** framework from cooperative game theory. It attributes a prediction to each feature as its *average marginal contribution* across all coalitions. **Shapley value formula:** $$ \phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \dfrac{\vert S \vert ! \, (\vert F \vert - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{\vert F \vert !} \big[ f(S \cup \{i\}) - f(S) \big] $$ **Properties (Efficiency, Symmetry, Dummy, Additivity):** - **Local Accuracy (Efficiency):** $\sum_i \phi_i = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(X)]$ — the SHAP values sum to the difference between prediction and the expected model output. - **Consistency:** If a model changes such that a feature contributes more, its SHAP value does not decrease. - **Missingness:** A feature absent from the model gets $\phi_i = 0$. **Comparison Table (Conclusion):** | Aspect | LIME | SHAP | |---|---|---| | Theoretical Foundation | Local fidelity optimization | Cooperative game theory (Shapley) | | Speed | Faster | Slower (exact SHAP is exponential) | | Consistency | Not guaranteed | Guaranteed | | Output | Local linear weights | Additive feature attributions | Both techniques are essential tools in the **operational transparency layer** of Responsible AI. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Question #### (a) Discuss the principle of Accountability in Responsible AI with a layered model. Explain the components of accountability and their roles in MLOps governance. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Definition of Accountability (1 mark):** Accountability is the principle that an *identifiable human or organizational agent* bears answerability for the behavior, decisions, and outcomes of an AI system, and that mechanisms exist for **explanation, audit, redress, and liability attribution**. Unlike transparency (which is about *visibility*), accountability is about *answerability* — a system can be transparent yet still lack accountability if no human role is mapped. **The Five-Layer Accountability Stack (3 marks):** | Layer | Mechanism | Role in MLOps | |---|---|---| | **L1 — Role Layer** | Defined roles: Model Owner, Data Steward, Reviewer, Grievance Officer | Assigns *who* is responsible at each stage of the AI lifecycle | | **L2 — Process Layer** | Documentation, sign-offs, Model Risk Management (MRM), Change Advisory Boards | Defines *how* responsibility is exercised; gates deployment | | **L3 — Evidence Layer** | Logs, audit trails, Model Cards, Datasheets, experiment trackers (MLflow) | Provides *proof* of responsible behavior; immutable records | | **L4 — Redress Layer** | Grievance channels, appeals, human-in-the-loop override, compensation frameworks | Enables *correction* of harms; restores trust | | **L5 — Regulatory Layer** | GDPR Article 22 (right to explanation), EU AI Act risk tiers, DPDPA, NIST AI RMF | Enforces *external* accountability via law | **Components of Accountability (2 marks):** 1. **Answerability** — The ability of an external party (regulator, user, citizen) to demand an explanation for an AI decision. *Example:* A loan applicant can ask why they were rejected and must receive a meaningful response. 2. **Auditability** — The capacity to inspect, reproduce, and evaluate an AI system after the fact. *Example:* An internal audit team can replay the model's decision on a specific transaction. 3. **Remediation** — The mechanisms to fix harm. *Example:* A human override can reverse a wrongful denial, or a company can offer compensation. 4. **Liability Allocation** — Legal attribution of fault. *Example:* Under the EU AI Act, the deployer of a high-risk system is liable for misuse even if the model was developed by a third party. **Practical Implementation in MLOps (1 mark):** - Use **MLOps platforms** (MLflow, Kubeflow) for experiment tracking → Evidence Layer. - Implement **role-based access control (RBAC)** in model registries → Role Layer. - Maintain a **model risk register** updated at every release → Process Layer. - Set up a **public-facing complaint portal** → Redress Layer. **Real-World Example (Conclusion):** The EU AI Act (2024) classifies AI systems into *unacceptable, high, limited, and minimal* risk tiers, with the **deployer** (not the developer) primarily accountable for high-risk use — this is the clearest example of regulatory accountability in action. --- #### (b) Compare the three principles of Responsible AI (Fairness, Accountability, Transparency). Explain how they interrelate and discuss their joint role in building trustworthy AI systems. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (often abbreviated **FAT** or **FAccT**) are the three foundational pillars of Responsible AI. They are not independent — they form a tightly coupled system in which each principle *enables* and *constrains* the other two. Together, they transform AI from a black-box technical artifact into a **socio-technical system** subject to human values and governance. **Comparative Analysis (3 marks):** | Dimension | Fairness | Accountability | Transparency | |---|---|---|---| | **Core Question** | *Whom does the system treat equitably?* | *Who is responsible for the system's behavior?* | *Can we see and understand the system?* | | **Primary Stakeholder** | Affected persons / protected groups | Regulators, organization, users | All stakeholders | | **Quantitative Metric** | SPD, DIR, EOD, Calibration gap | Audit log completeness, role coverage, response SLA | SHAP, LIME, Model Card coverage, IDS | | **Failure Mode** | Discriminatory outcomes | "No one is to blame" black box | "No one understands" opacity | | **Engineering Tool** | Fairlearn, Aequitas, AI Fairness 360 | MLflow, Model Risk Management, RBAC | SHAP, LIME, Model Cards, Datasheets | | **Regulatory Anchor** | 80% Rule (UGESP), EU AI Act Title III | GDPR Art. 22, EU AI Act Title IX | EU AI Act Art. 13, DPDPA | **Interrelation of the Three Principles (2 marks):** 1. **Fairness requires Transparency to verify:** You cannot detect bias in a model you cannot inspect. SHAP/LIME-style transparency is the *diagnostic instrument* for fairness. 2. **Accountability requires Transparency to execute:** An accountability mechanism (e.g., a regulator's audit) is meaningless without visibility into the system. Transparency is the *channel* through which answerability is exercised. 3. **Fairness requires Accountability to enforce:** Even when bias is detected, no harm is corrected unless someone is *responsible* for fixing it. Accountability is the *enforcement loop* for fairness. 4. **Accountability requires Fairness to be meaningful:** A system cannot be "accountable" in a substantive sense if its outcomes are systematically biased — bias distorts what "accountable" means. **Joint Role in Trustworthy AI (1 mark):** The three principles jointly produce **trustworthiness**, defined by the **EU HLEG (High-Level Expert Group on AI, 2019)** along three additional axes: **lawfulness, robustness, and societal/environmental well-being**. FAT principles are the *human-facing* axis of trustworthiness, while the latter three are the *technical* axis. AI systems that violate any of FAT face **regulatory rejection, market rejection, and societal rejection** — a *triple-failure* mode that no commercial product can survive. **Conclusion:** Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency are not a checklist of independent requirements — they are a **mutually reinforcing triad** that operationalizes ethical AI. A model that is fair but opaque cannot be audited; a model that is transparent but unfair cannot be trusted; a model that is fair and transparent but unaccountable cannot be corrected. Only when all three are present does Responsible AI become a reality rather than a slogan. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on FAT Questions — Avoid These to Secure Full Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing Transparency with Explainability** — Transparency is the *property*; explainability (XAI) is the *mechanism*. Examiners deduct marks for treating them as synonyms. > > 2. **Stating "AI must be 100% fair"** — There is **no such thing** as a perfectly fair classifier. The Impossibility Theorem guarantees trade-offs. Marks are lost for ignoring this. > > 3. **Forgetting the 80% Rule threshold** — The Disparate Impact Ratio threshold is **0.80**, not 0.50 or 0.95. Examiners expect the **4/5ths rule** explicitly cited. > > 4. **Conflating Accountability with Liability** — Accountability is broader: it includes *answerability* and *redress*, not just legal liability. Single-line answers lose 2 marks. > > 5. **Skipping the mathematical formulation** — KTU's 14-mark questions on FAT *always* expect at least 2–3 equations (SPD, DIR, EOD, SHAP). A purely qualitative answer cannot score above 8/14. > > 6. **Missing the Interrelation** — The 14-mark question "Compare the three principles" *always* expects a section on how they **interact**, not three independent paragraphs. Students who write three disjoint definitions lose 3–4 marks. > > 7. **No Real-World Examples** — KTU examiners reward named case studies (Amazon recruitment AI, COMPAS, Apple Card, EU AI Act). Vague "bias in AI" references do not earn these marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Responsible AI** = Fairness + Accountability + Transparency (the **FAT / FAccT triad**) — these are the three pillars of the KTU Module 4 syllabus. - [x] **Fairness** has **six common types**: Demographic Parity, Equal Opportunity, Predictive Parity, Individual Fairness, Counterfactual Fairness, Procedural Fairness. - [x] **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** $< 0.80$ = **adverse impact** under the UGESP **80% rule**. - [x] The **SPD** (Statistical Parity Difference), **EOD** (Equal Opportunity Difference), and **DIR** are the three most-tested fairness metrics — memorize the formulas. - [x] **Chouldechova / Kleinberg Impossibility Theorem:** Calibration + Equalized Odds + Demographic Parity **cannot all hold** if base rates differ. - [x] **Accountability** has **5 layers**: Role → Process → Evidence → Redress → Regulatory. - [x] Accountability requires **human ownership** — not just documentation. - [x] **Transparency** has **3 layers**: Model Transparency, Algorithmic Transparency, Post-hoc Explainability. - [x] **LIME** = Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations — fits a simple surrogate $g$ in the local neighborhood of $x$. - [x] **SHAP** = SHapley Additive exPlanations — based on **Shapley values** from cooperative game theory; satisfies **local accuracy** ($\sum \phi_i = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(X)]$). - [x] **Model Cards** (Mitchell 2019) and **Datasheets for Datasets** (Gebru 2018) are the two most-cited transparency artifacts. - [x] **FAT interrelation:** Fairness needs Transparency (to detect), Accountability (to fix); Accountability needs Transparency (to execute) and Fairness (to be meaningful). - [x] **Key regulatory references:** EU AI Act (2024), GDPR Article 22, DPDPA 2023, NIST AI RMF, OECD AI Principles. - [x] **Real-world case studies** to memorize: Amazon recruitment AI (gender), COMPAS (race), Apple Card (gender), Microsoft Tay (chatbot toxicity). - [x] **MLOps tools** to mention in answers: **IBM AI Fairness 360, Google What-If Tool, Microsoft Fairlearn, Aequitas** (fairness); **MLflow, Kubeflow** (audit trails); **SHAP, LIME** (explainability). - [x] **Trust = f(Fairness, Accountability, Transparency, Lawfulness, Robustness, Societal Well-being)** — the EU HLEG framework.

topics.title: Designing and Auditing AI Systems for Ethical Compliance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Designing and Auditing AI Systems for Ethical Compliance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — DESIGNING AND AUDITING AI SYSTEMS FOR ETHICAL COMPLIANCE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ethical AI Compliance** is the systematic process of designing, developing, deploying, and continuously monitoring Artificial Intelligence systems in strict adherence to a defined set of moral principles, legal regulations, and socio-technical standards. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, it encompasses the engineering practice of embedding fairness, accountability, transparency, privacy, and safety into the AI lifecycle (from data collection to model retirement), followed by **independent verification** through structured audits that measure conformity against established governance frameworks such as the **NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)**, the **ISO/IEC 42001 (AIMS)** standard, and the **EU AI Act**.

> [!NOTE]
> **AI Auditing** is not the same as AI Testing. Testing verifies *functional correctness* (does it work?), while auditing verifies *procedural and ethical conformity* (was it built and used the right way?).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Stage Analogy"

Imagine a pharmaceutical company launching a new drug:

| Stage | Pharmaceutical Analogy | AI Ethics Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Design** | Drug trials, dosage research, ingredient sourcing | Fairness constraints, data provenance checks, model interpretability design |
| **Pre-Launch Audit** | FDA approval, clinical trial reports | Internal Ethics Review Board, pre-deployment bias audit |
| **Post-Launch Monitoring** | Adverse event reporting, periodic recalls | Continuous monitoring, drift detection, red-teaming |

Just as a drug that performs well in trials can still cause harm in the real world, an AI model with **99% accuracy** can still be ethically dangerous due to **hidden bias, opaque reasoning, or privacy leakage**. The auditor's job is to look *beyond* accuracy metrics and inspect the **decision-making process, training data, and downstream societal impact**.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Ethical AI Compliance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The FAT-P Framework** — Every KTU examiner expects students to know these four foundational pillars:
> - **F**airness — Equal treatment across demographic groups
> - **A**ccountability — Clear ownership and traceability of decisions
> - **T**ransparency — Explainability of model behavior
> - **P**rivacy & Safety — Protection of data and human life

The engineering definition expands these into **eight core principles** adopted from the **UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of AI (2021)** and the **OECD AI Principles (2019)**:

1. **Proportionality & Do No Harm**
2. **Safety & Security**
3. **Fairness & Non-Discrimination**
4. **Sustainability**
5. **Right to Privacy & Data Protection**
6. **Human Oversight & Determination**
7. **Transparency & Explainability**
8. **Responsibility & Accountability**

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

The **NEP 2020 (National Education Policy)** mandates that all B.Tech graduates demonstrate **professional ethics and an understanding of the impact of engineering solutions in societal/environmental contexts**. PCITT602 Module 4 directly addresses this mandate by training students to **engineer accountability** into intelligent systems — a skill increasingly demanded by industries in Kerala's booming IT/AI sector (Infopark, Technopark, and Kochi's AI startups).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AI System Lifecycle and Points of Ethical Intervention

A modern AI system passes through **seven lifecycle stages**, and ethical design requires interventions at *every* stage — not just at deployment.

### Stage 1: Problem Formulation
- Define the **business objective** and the **ethical objective** in parallel.
- Identify **stakeholders** (users, affected communities, regulators).
- Perform a **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** for GDPR/Kerala DPDP Act compliance.

### Stage 2: Data Collection & Curation
- Audit **data provenance** (where did the data come from?).
- Check **representation bias** (are minority groups adequately sampled?).
- Apply **consent management** (especially for biometric/health data).
- Document **labeling protocols** to prevent annotator bias.

### Stage 3: Model Development
- Apply **fairness constraints** during training (e.g., adversarial debiasing).
- Use **interpretable models** where possible (logistic regression, decision trees).
- For black-box models (deep learning), use **post-hoc explainers** (SHAP, LIME).
- Document **hyperparameters, training compute, and energy consumption**.

### Stage 4: Pre-Deployment Validation
- Conduct **algorithmic impact assessments (AIA)**.
- Test for **group fairness metrics** (see Formula Sheet below).
- Perform **red-teaming** — adversarial testing to find failure modes.
- Validate against **regulatory checklists** (EU AI Act risk tier).

### Stage 5: Deployment
- Implement **human-in-the-loop (HITL)** for high-stakes decisions.
- Publish a **Model Card** and **System Card** (Mitchell et al., 2019).
- Establish **rollback mechanisms** for catastrophic failure.

### Stage 6: Operations & Monitoring
- Track **data drift**, **concept drift**, and **performance drift**.
- Monitor **disparate error rates** across subgroups in production.
- Log **decisions for auditability** (with appropriate encryption).

### Stage 7: Retirement
- Archive or **securely delete model weights and training data**.
- Notify users of model deprecation.
- Conduct **post-mortem analysis** of societal impact.

## 2.2 The Five Schools of Ethical Theory Applied to AI

KTU examiners frequently ask students to **map AI dilemmas to ethical schools**. Memorize these mappings:

| Ethical School | Core Question | Application to AI |
|---|---|---|
| **Deontological (Kantian)** | "Is the action rule-based and respectful of autonomy?" | Treat every user as an *end*, never as a *means*. No manipulation. |
| **Consequentialist (Utilitarian)** | "Does it maximize aggregate welfare?" | Optimize for *societal benefit*, not just *engagement metrics*. |
| **Virtue Ethics (Aristotelian)** | "What would a virtuous engineer do?" | Cultivate honesty, justice, and humility in design choices. |
| **Principlism (Beauchamp & Childress)** | "Are the four principles respected?" | Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice — used in biomedical AI. |
| **Care Ethics (Gilligan)** | "Are relationships and vulnerability centered?" | Prioritize vulnerable populations (children, elderly, disabled). |

## 2.3 The Risk-Based Classification of AI Systems

The **EU AI Act (2024)** — which influences Indian policy and KTU's regulatory awareness curriculum — classifies AI into **four risk tiers**:

| Risk Tier | Description | Example | Compliance Burden |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unacceptable Risk** | Banned outright | Social scoring by governments, manipulative AI | **Prohibited** |
| **High Risk** | Regulated strictly | Recruitment AI, medical diagnostic AI, biometric ID | **Full audit, CE marking, registration** |
| **Limited Risk** | Transparency obligations | Chatbots, deepfake generators | **Disclosure required** |
| **Minimal Risk** | No obligation | Spam filters, AI in video games | **Voluntary codes of conduct** |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following quantitative metrics appear in KTU Part A and Part B questions. Memorize the formulas AND their interpretation.**

| Metric | Formula | Ideal Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Statistical Parity Difference (SPD)** | $\text{SPD} = \vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid S=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid S=1) \vert$ | $\text{SPD} = 0$ | Measures difference in positive prediction rates between groups |
| **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** | $\text{DIR} = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid S=0)}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid S=1)}$ | $0.8 \le \text{DIR} \le 1.25$ | The "80% rule" from U.S. EEOC |
| **Equal Opportunity Difference (EOD)** | $\text{EOD} = \vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, S=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, S=1) \vert$ | $\text{EOD} = 0$ | Equal true positive rates across groups |
| **Predictive Parity** | $\vert P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, S=0) - P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, S=1) \vert$ | $0$ | Equal precision across groups |
| **Risk Score** | $R = L \times I$ | Minimize | $L$ = Likelihood (1–5), $I$ = Impact (1–5) |
| **Inherent Risk** | $R_{\text{inherent}} = L \times I$ | — | Risk *before* controls |
| **Residual Risk** | $R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} \times (1 - C_{\text{eff}})$ | Minimize | $C_{\text{eff}} \in [0, 1]$ = control effectiveness |
| **Privacy Leakage (Membership Inference)** | $P(\text{in training} \mid \text{output})$ | Low | Probability model reveals training set membership |
| **Differential Privacy Budget** | $\epsilon$ — total privacy loss | $\epsilon \le 1$ (strong), $\epsilon \le 10$ (acceptable) | Lower $\epsilon$ = more privacy |
| **Model Card Coverage** | $C_{\text{mc}} = \dfrac{\text{Documented Use Cases}}{\text{Total Use Cases}}$ | $C_{\text{mc}} = 1$ | Proportion of uses explicitly documented |

> **Notation Key:** $\hat{Y}$ = model prediction, $Y$ = true label, $S$ = sensitive attribute (e.g., gender, caste), $L$ = likelihood, $I$ = impact, $C_{\text{eff}}$ = control effectiveness.

### 2.5 The NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)

NIST's framework organizes AI risk management into **four functions**, mirroring the "Plan-Do-Check-Act" cycle:

1. **GOVERN** — Establish policies, roles, and accountability structures.
2. **MAP** — Contextualize risks, identify affected stakeholders.
3. **MEASURE** — Quantify fairness, robustness, security, and privacy metrics.
4. **MANAGE** — Prioritize, treat, and document residual risks.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, these frameworks are used by:
- **Banking & FinTech** (Kerala's Federal Bank, Muthoot FinCorp) for loan approval AI auditing.
- **Healthcare AI** (KIMS, Amrita Hospitals) for diagnostic model compliance.
- **Government (Kerala State IT Mission)** for citizen-facing chatbot governance.
- **EdTech platforms** (BYJU'S, Unacademy) for ensuring fair content recommendation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Residual Risk Formula

The residual risk formula is *the* most-tested expression in compliance engineering. We will derive it exhaustively.

**Step 1: Define Inherent Risk.**

Inherent risk is the risk level *before* any controls are applied. It is the product of two independent dimensions: the **likelihood** that an event occurs and the **impact** if it does.

$$R_{\text{inherent}} = L \times I$$

where $L \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ (1 = Rare, 5 = Almost Certain) and $I \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ (1 = Negligible, 5 = Catastrophic).

**Step 2: Introduce Control Effectiveness.**

A *control* is any safeguard that reduces either the likelihood or the impact (e.g., adversarial training reduces $L$; human-in-the-loop reduces $I$). Define $C_{\text{eff}} \in [0, 1]$ as the **fractional reduction** in risk achieved by a control.

**Step 3: Express Reduction in Risk.**

The total risk reduction attributable to a control is:

$$\Delta R = R_{\text{inherent}} \times C_{\text{eff}}$$

**Step 4: Compute Residual Risk.**

Residual risk is inherent risk *minus* the reduction:

$$R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} - \Delta R$$

Substituting Step 3 into Step 4:

$$R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} - (R_{\text{inherent}} \times C_{\text{eff}})$$

**Step 5: Factor Out Inherent Risk.**

$$R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} (1 - C_{\text{eff}})$$

**Step 6: Verification with Boundary Cases.**

- If $C_{\text{eff}} = 0$ (no control): $R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}}$ ✓
- If $C_{\text{eff}} = 1$ (perfect control): $R_{\text{residual}} = 0$ ✓
- If $C_{\text{eff}} = 0.5$ (50% effective): $R_{\text{residual}} = 0.5 \times R_{\text{inherent}}$ ✓

**Step 7: Numerical Example.**

Consider a loan-approval AI. Initial $L = 4$ (Likely to discriminate), $I = 5$ (Catastrophic harm to marginalized caste groups). We implement a debiasing layer that is 60% effective.

$$R_{\text{inherent}} = 4 \times 5 = 25$$

$$C_{\text{eff}} = 0.60$$

$$R_{\text{residual}} = 25 \times (1 - 0.60) = 25 \times 0.40 = 10$$

The residual risk of **10** is still above acceptable thresholds (typically $R_{\text{residual}} \le 4$), so additional controls are required — for example, human oversight ($C_{\text{eff}} = 0.50$ on the residual):

$$R_{\text{residual, final}} = 10 \times (1 - 0.50) = 5$$

Now $R_{\text{residual, final}} = 5$ is borderline, suggesting a *third* layer of mitigation is advisable.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)

**Step 1: Identify the Privileged Group.**

Let $S = 1$ denote the privileged group (historically favored) and $S = 0$ denote the unprivileged group.

**Step 2: Compute Selection Rates.**

$$\text{SR}_{S=1} = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = 1)$$

$$\text{SR}_{S=0} = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid S = 0)$$

**Step 3: Form the Ratio.**

$$\text{DIR} = \dfrac{\text{SR}_{S=0}}{\text{SR}_{S=1}}$$

**Step 4: Apply the 80% Rule.**

A system is considered fair under the **4/5ths rule** (U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission) if:

$$\text{DIR} \ge 0.80$$

**Step 5: Numerical Example.**

A recruitment AI selects men at 60% and women at 30%:

$$\text{DIR} = \dfrac{0.30}{0.60} = 0.50$$

Since $0.50 < 0.80$, the system is **legally non-compliant** and ethically discriminatory.

**Step 6: Verification.**

If the AI selected both groups at 60%: $\text{DIR} = 60/60 = 1.0$ ✓ (perfect parity).

## 3.3 Derivation: From $\epsilon$-Differential Privacy to the Privacy Budget

**Step 1: Definition of $\epsilon$-Differential Privacy.**

A randomized algorithm $M$ satisfies $\epsilon$-differential privacy if for any two datasets $D$ and $D'$ differing by one record:

$$P(M(D) \in S) \le e^{\epsilon} \cdot P(M(D') \in S)$$

**Step 2: Composability.**

When $k$ independent queries are made, the total privacy loss *adds up*:

$$\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \epsilon_{i}$$

**Step 3: Practical Implication.**

If each query costs $\epsilon_{i} = 0.1$, after 10 queries the budget is $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = 1.0$, and the data is no longer strongly private.

**Step 4: Numerical Example.**

An AI model in a Kerala hospital releases 50 anonymized statistics on patient data. Each release costs $\epsilon = 0.05$:

$$\epsilon_{\text{total}} = 50 \times 0.05 = 2.5$$

This is **above the recommended** $\epsilon \le 1$ threshold, so the hospital must use stronger noise mechanisms (e.g., Gaussian mechanism with larger $\sigma$).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Audit Frameworks

| Framework | Origin | Focus | Strength | Weakness | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NIST AI RMF 1.0** | USA (2023) | Voluntary risk management | Comprehensive, industry-agnostic | Non-binding | High — frequently cited |
| **ISO/IEC 42001 (AIMS)** | International (2023) | Certifiable management system | Globally auditable certification | Costly to implement | High — for compliance careers |
| **EU AI Act** | European Union (2024) | Legal regulation | Legally enforceable | Extraterritorial reach is complex | Medium — for global firms |
| **OECD AI Principles** | OECD (2019) | Policy guidance | High-level, values-based | Non-prescriptive | Medium |
| **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design** | IEEE (2019) | Technical standards | Deep technical detail | No legal weight | High — for engineers |
| **India's IndiaAI Mission Guidelines** | MeitY (2024) | National strategy | Aligned with DPDP Act 2023 | Still evolving | Very High — KTU context |
