List any two distinguishing features of neuromorphic hardware compared to traditional von Neumann CPUs.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Neuromorphic computing is a hardware paradigm that physically emulates the structure and function of biological neural systems using spiking neurons, memristive synapses, and event-driven asynchronous communication. *(Coined by Carver Mead.)* - **Distinguishing Feature 1 (1 mark):** **Co-located memory and processing** — synaptic weights (memristors) and computation (neurons) reside in the same physical substrate, eliminating the von Neumann memory bottleneck. - **Distinguishing Feature 2 (1 mark):** **Event-driven / asynchronous operation** — neurons consume power only when a spike is emitted, yielding milliwatt-level energy consumption versus hundreds of watts for GPUs. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** *What is Quantum Machine Learning (QML)? State one potential advantage and one major practical challenge of QML.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Quantum Machine Learning is the integration of quantum computational primitives (qubits, superposition, entanglement) with machine learning algorithms to achieve speedups or improved model expressivity over classical approaches. - **Advantage (1 mark):** Quantum computers can operate over a $2^n$-dimensional Hilbert space using only $n$ qubits, enabling parallel processing of exponentially many feature combinations (e.g., Grover's quadratic speedup, HHL linear-system speedup). - **Challenge (1 mark):** **Decoherence** — quantum states lose their delicate superposition due to environmental noise within microseconds, severely limiting circuit depth and qubit count in the current **NISQ (Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum)** era. --- ### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Do **NOT** confuse *quantum annealing* (used by D-Wave) with *gate-based quantum AI* (used by IBM, Google) — they are fundamentally different paradigms and questions often test this distinction. > - In neuromorphic questions, students frequently write "it uses artificial neurons" — this is too vague. **Use the term "spiking neurons"** and **"memristors"** explicitly to score full marks. > - Always state the **units** of power (mW vs. W) and the **time constants** (ms vs. $\mu$s) when comparing systems — examiners award marks for quantitative precision. > - For superposition questions, **always show the normalization condition** $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$. Skipping it costs 1 mark. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Neuromorphic Computing Focus **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a typical neuromorphic computing system. Explain the role of memristors and the Address-Event Representation (AER) protocol. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Derive the Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) membrane equation from the equivalent RC circuit. Show how a spiking neural network can perform inference in an event-driven manner. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Architecture of a Neuromorphic System (7 Marks):** A typical neuromorphic system (e.g., Intel Loihi 2) consists of the following blocks: 1. **Input Encoder (1 mark):** Converts real-world sensor data (image pixels, audio samples, accelerometer readings) into trains of spikes using AER. Each spike carries the address of the source neuron and a timestamp. 2. **Neuromorphic Cores (1 mark):** Each core contains hundreds of LIF neurons and thousands of memristive synapses. Cores communicate via a shared asynchronous mesh network-on-chip (NoC). 3. **Memristive Crossbar (2 marks):** A grid of memristors performs in-situ matrix-vector multiplication for synaptic weighting. The conductance $G_{ij}$ of each memristor represents the weight $w_{ij}$ between pre-synaptic neuron $i$ and post-synaptic neuron $j$. This is the *physical* implementation of synaptic plasticity. 4. **AER Protocol (1 mark):** Spikes are encoded as packets containing the source neuron's address. The destination core receives the packet and increments its membrane potential. Since only active neurons transmit, the system is **event-driven** — idle regions consume near-zero power. 5. **On-chip Learning Module (1 mark):** Implements **STDP (Spike-Timing-Dependent Plasticity)**: if a pre-synaptic spike precedes a post-synaptic spike, the synapse is potentiated ($\Delta w > 0$); otherwise, it is depressed. This is computed *locally*, enabling online unsupervised learning. 6. **Output Decoder (1 mark):** Spikes at the output layer are decoded into classification labels or control signals. **(b) LIF Derivation and Event-Driven Inference (7 Marks):** **RC Circuit Analogy (2 marks):** The neuronal membrane is modeled as a resistor $R$ in parallel with a capacitor $C$, driven by synaptic current $I(t)$. Applying KCL: $$ I(t) = \frac{V(t)}{R} + C \frac{dV(t)}{dt} $$ **Membrane Time Constant (1 mark):** Multiplying by $R$ and setting $\tau_m = RC$: $$ \tau_m \frac{dV(t)}{dt} = -V(t) + R \cdot I(t) $$ **Spike-and-Reset (1 mark):** When $V(t) \ge V_{th}$, the neuron emits a spike and resets to $V_{reset}$: $$ V(t^+) = V_{reset} $$ **Discrete Simulation Form (1 mark):** On neuromorphic hardware, time is advanced in discrete ticks $\Delta t$: $$ V[t+1] = V[t] + \frac{\Delta t}{\tau_m}\bigl(-V[t] + R \cdot I[t]\bigr) $$ **Event-Driven Inference (2 marks):** During inference, only neurons that *cross the threshold* generate output spikes. These spikes are propagated to the next layer via AER, where they trigger further membrane updates. Because the system processes **sparse, asynchronous events** rather than dense matrix multiplications on every clock cycle, power consumption is dominated by active spikes only — typically **$< 1\,\text{W}$** for a full neuromorphic chip, compared to **$>200\,\text{W}$** for an equivalent GPU. The temporal coding of spike timing additionally enables the network to encode information in the *inter-spike interval*, which is unavailable to rate-based ANNs. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Quantum AI Focus **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the principles of quantum computing that make Quantum AI potentially advantageous over classical AI. Discuss superposition, entanglement, and quantum parallelism with suitable mathematical expressions. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the architecture of a Variational Quantum Circuit (VQC) used in Quantum Machine Learning. Explain the hybrid quantum-classical optimization loop and state one application of VQCs. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Quantum Principles Enabling QAI (7 Marks):** 1. **Qubit and Superposition (2 marks):** A classical bit is either 0 or 1. A qubit $\vert \psi \rangle$ can be in a linear combination: $$ \vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle, \quad \text{with} \quad \vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1 $$ where $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{C}$. Upon measurement, the qubit collapses to $\vert 0 \rangle$ with probability $\vert \alpha \vert^2$ and to $\vert 1 \rangle$ with probability $\vert \beta \vert^2$. 2. **Quantum Parallelism (2 marks):** An $n$-qubit register initialized via Hadamard gates creates a uniform superposition over all $2^n$ basis states in a *single* operation: $$ H^{\otimes n} \vert 0 \rangle^{\otimes n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^n}} \sum_{x=0}^{2^n - 1} \vert x \rangle $$ A function $f$ implemented as a unitary $U_f$ then acts on *all* $2^n$ inputs simultaneously: $U_f \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^n}} \sum_x \vert x \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^n}} \sum_x \vert x, f(x) \rangle$. Quantum interference is then used to amplify correct answers and cancel incorrect ones, yielding the famous speedups (e.g., Grover's $\sqrt{N}$, Shor's polynomial factoring). 3. **Entanglement (2 marks):** Two or more qubits can be prepared in a joint state that *cannot* be written as a product of individual qubit states. The Bell state $$ \vert \Phi^+ \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 00 \rangle + \vert 11 \rangle) $$ is the canonical example. Entanglement enables coordinated computation across qubits and is the resource behind quantum teleportation, dense coding, and the expressivity of quantum kernels in QML. 4. **Why this matters for AI (1 mark):** Classical feature spaces grow linearly with dimensions; quantum feature spaces grow as $2^n$. Quantum kernels can thus capture correlations intractable for classical SVMs. **(b) Variational Quantum Circuit Architecture (7 Marks):** A VQC (also called a *Parameterized Quantum Circuit* or *Quantum Neural Network*) is the workhorse of near-term QML. 1. **Data Encoding Layer (1 mark):** Classical input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is encoded into qubit rotations, e.g., $R_x(x_i)\vert 0 \rangle = \cos(x_i/2)\vert 0 \rangle - i\sin(x_i/2)\vert 1 \rangle$. 2. **Variational (Ansatz) Layers (2 marks):** A sequence of parameterized unitaries $U(\theta)$ with trainable angles $\theta$ is applied. A typical block is: $$ U(\theta) = \prod_{l=1}^{L} \left[ \bigotimes_{j=1}^{n} R(\theta_{j}^{(l)}) \right] \cdot \text{Entangling gates} $$ 3. **Measurement (1 mark):** The expectation value of an observable $O$ (e.g., Pauli-$Z$ on the output qubit) gives the prediction: $$ f(\mathbf{x}, \theta) = \langle \psi(\mathbf{x}, \theta) \vert O \vert \psi(\mathbf{x}, \theta) \rangle $$ 4. **Hybrid Classical Optimizer (2 marks):** A classical optimizer (COBYLA, L-BFGS, Adam) computes the gradient — via the *parameter-shift rule* $\partial \langle O \rangle / \partial \theta_i = \tfrac{1}{2}[\langle O \rangle_{\theta_i + \pi/2} - \langle O \rangle_{\theta_i - \pi/2}]$ — and updates $\theta$. The loop iterates until the loss converges. 5. **Application (1 mark):** VQCs are widely used in **Quantum Chemistry** to find molecular ground-state energies (VQE for $H_2$, $LiH$, etc.), where classical methods scale exponentially with electron count. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - ✅ **Neuromorphic Computing** = brain-inspired hardware; co-located memory + processing; event-driven spiking neurons. - ✅ **Spiking Neural Network (SNN)** uses **discrete temporal spikes**; 3rd-generation neural network. - ✅ **Memristor** = 2-terminal device with history-dependent resistance; physical synapse. - ✅ **LIF equation**: $\tau_m \dot V = -V + RI$; fires when $V \ge V_{th}$. - ✅ **STDP** = local Hebbian rule based on spike timing difference $\Delta t$. - ✅ **Power comparison**: Neuromorphic $\sim$ mW vs. GPU $\sim$ 200 W → **10⁴× energy efficiency**. - ✅ **Quantum AI** = intersection of quantum computing + ML; exploits superposition, entanglement, interference. - ✅ **Qubit state**: $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$, $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$. - ✅ **Hadamard gate** $H$ creates uniform superposition from $\vert 0 \rangle$. - ✅ **Bell state** $\vert \Phi^+ \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 00 \rangle + \vert 11 \rangle)$ — maximally entangled, non-factorizable. - ✅ **VQC** = hybrid quantum-classical loop; classical optimizer updates quantum gate parameters using parameter-shift rule. - ✅ **NISQ era** = Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum; current practical limit: $\sim$100–1000 noisy qubits. - ✅ **Speedups**: Grover $\sqrt{N}$ (search), Shor $\text{poly}(\log N)$ (factoring), HHL $O(\log N)$ (linear systems). - ✅ **Distinguish**: Quantum *annealing* (D-Wave, adiabatic) vs. gate-based quantum AI (IBM, Google). - ✅ **Use terms precisely** in exams: "spiking neurons" not just "neurons"; "memristors" not just "weights"; "superposition" not just "parallelism". - ✅ Always **state units** (mW, W, ms, $\mu$s) and **show normalization conditions** for quantum states to secure full marks.

topics.title: Potential Societal Transformations and Ethical Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT602 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Potential Societal Transformations and Ethical Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Potential Societal Transformations** refer to the projected structural, economic, cultural, and behavioral changes in human civilization resulting from the widespread deployment and integration of advanced Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems across public and private sectors.

**Ethical Considerations in AI** constitute the systematic evaluation of moral principles, normative frameworks, and value-alignment mechanisms required to ensure that AI systems operate in a manner that is **fair, transparent, accountable, privacy-preserving, and beneficial to humanity** while minimizing harm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 4):** Students must demonstrate understanding of how AI reshapes labor markets, governance, healthcare, education, and the environment, alongside the ethical risks of bias, surveillance, autonomy erosion, and the alignment problem. This topic carries significant weight in KTU ESE and internal assessments.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Conceptual Analogy

### Real-World Analogy: The Railway Revolution vs. The AI Revolution

Imagine the **19th-century Railway Revolution**. Steam trains transformed society — they shrank distances, created new job categories (engineers, ticket clerks), destroyed old ones (horse-coach drivers, inns along dirt roads), restructured cities, and raised entirely new ethical questions (worker safety, land acquisition, monopolies). Society did not stop the railway; it **adapted, regulated, and absorbed** the technology over decades.

Today's **AI Revolution** is a faster, deeper, and more cognitive version of the same phenomenon. Unlike railways (which only moved matter), AI moves **information, decisions, and cognition** itself.

### The Three Core Pillars

| Pillar | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Transformation** | Structural shift in how society operates | Self-driving trucks replacing logistics jobs |
| **Ethics** | Moral frameworks governing AI use | GDPR, EU AI Act, IEEE Ethically Aligned Design |
| **Governance** | Institutional mechanisms for oversight | AI regulatory sandboxes, algorithmic auditing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Societal transformation is the *what* (what changes), while ethics is the *how* (how we ensure changes are just). Both must be studied together because every transformation creates new ethical dilemmas, and every ethical framework shapes the trajectory of transformation.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Bold Definitions)

- **Alignment Problem**: The challenge of ensuring that AI systems pursue goals that are consistent with human values and intentions.
- **Algorithmic Bias**: Systematic and repeatable errors in AI outputs that create unfair outcomes for specific demographic groups.
- **Technological Unemployment**: Job loss caused by the substitution of human labor by machines and intelligent automation.
- **AI Governance**: The set of policies, norms, regulations, and institutions designed to guide the development and deployment of AI.
- **Value Loading**: The process of embedding human ethical principles into an AI system's decision-making architecture.
- **Dual-Use Dilemma**: The condition where an AI technology can be deployed for both beneficial and harmful purposes.
- **Existential Risk**: Hypothetical scenarios where advanced AI could pose a threat to humanity's long-term survival.
- **Digital Sovereignty**: A nation's ability to control its own digital infrastructure, data, and AI capabilities.
- **Redistribution Mechanism**: Policy tools (Universal Basic Income, retraining) that spread AI's economic benefits equitably.
- **Constitutional AI**: An approach where AI behavior is constrained by a written set of principles (e.g., Anthropic's RLAIF methodology).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Dimensions of Societal Transformation

Societal transformation due to AI occurs along **seven interlocking dimensions**. Each dimension must be analyzed for both its **transformative potential** and its **ethical risk surface**.

### Dimension 1: The Labor Market (Economic Transformation)

AI automates both **routine cognitive tasks** (data entry, basic legal review, radiology screening) and increasingly **non-routine cognitive tasks** (creative writing, software engineering, scientific research).

- **Displacement Effect**: Jobs eliminated by automation.
- **Reinstatement Effect**: New job categories created by AI (prompt engineers, MLOps specialists, AI auditors).
- **Productivity Effect**: AI augments workers, raising output per hour.
- **Polarization Effect**: Middle-skill jobs shrink while high-skill and low-skill jobs grow.

### Dimension 2: Governance and Public Administration

AI transforms governance through:
1. **PredictivePolicing**: Forecasting crime hotspots (ethical risk: feedback loops and racial bias).
2. **Smart Cities**: Optimized traffic, energy, and waste management via IoT + AI.
3. **Algorithmic Decision-Making (ADM)**: AI used in welfare distribution, immigration, sentencing.
4. **Digital ID and Surveillance**: Facial recognition in public spaces.

### Dimension 3: Healthcare and Life Sciences

- Drug discovery acceleration (e.g., AlphaFold by DeepMind).
- Precision medicine based on genomic AI.
- Robotic surgery and AI-assisted diagnostics.
- **Risk**: Medical AI errors, liability assignment, health-data privacy.

### Dimension 4: Education

- Personalized tutoring systems (adaptive learning).
- Automated grading and content generation.
- **Risk**: Widening digital divide, teacher deskilling, academic integrity collapse.

### Dimension 5: Environment and Sustainability

- AI for climate modeling, smart grids, precision agriculture.
- **Counter-effect**: Training large models has a significant carbon footprint.

### Dimension 6: Information Ecosystem and Epistemology

- Generative AI blurs the line between real and synthetic content.
- **Deepfakes, misinformation, epistemic collapse** are the core ethical concerns.
- Search and recommendation algorithms shape public discourse.

### Dimension 7: Human Identity and Autonomy

- AI companions, affective computing, brain-computer interfaces.
- **Risk**: Manipulation, addiction, erosion of human agency.

## 2.2 Ethical Frameworks for AI (KTU High-Yield Summary)

### The Five Canonical Principles (UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of AI, 2021)

1. **Proportionality and Do No Harm**: AI use must be proportional and risk-assessed.
2. **Safety and Security**: AI systems must be robust, secure, and safe throughout their lifecycle.
3. **Fairness and Non-Discrimination**: AI must avoid bias and promote social justice.
4. **Sustainability**: AI development must respect environmental limits.
5. **Right to Privacy and Data Protection**: Data sovereignty and consent must be preserved.

### The IEEE Ethically Aligned Design Principles

| # | Principle | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Human Rights** | AI must not violate dignity, freedom, expression |
| 2 | **Well-being** | AI must prioritize human flourishing |
| 3 | **Data Agency** | Individuals control their own data |
| 4 | **Effectiveness** | AI must be reliable and deliver on claims |
| 5 | **Transparency** | Decisions must be explainable and auditable |
| 6 | **Accountability** | Clear responsibility for AI outcomes |
| 7 | **Awareness of Misuse** | Designers anticipate harmful uses |
| 8 | **Competence** | Operators must be trained appropriately |

### The EU AI Act Risk-Based Taxonomy (2024)

The EU AI Act classifies AI systems into **four risk tiers**:

- **Unacceptable Risk** (banned): Social scoring by governments, manipulative subliminal AI.
- **High Risk** (strict compliance): Critical infrastructure, education scoring, employment screening, biometric ID, law enforcement.
- **Limited Risk** (transparency obligations): Chatbots, deepfake disclosure, emotion recognition.
- **Minimal Risk** (voluntary codes): Spam filters, AI in video games.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The EU AI Act risk classification is a high-yield topic. KTU examiners frequently ask students to categorize AI applications into risk tiers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Theoretical Formula Sheet

Since this topic is qualitative-ethical, the "formulas" are **analytical frameworks** and **decision matrices** rather than mathematical equations.

| Framework | Mathematical / Logical Form | Application Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Bias Audit Metric** | $\text{Disparate Impact} = \frac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid S=\text{minority})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid S=\text{majority})}$ | Algorithmic Fairness |
| **Equalized Odds** | $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, S=s) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, S=t)$ | Fair Classification |
| **Demographic Parity** | $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid S=s) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid S=t)$ | Fairness Without Ground Truth |
| **Carbon Cost of Training** | $C_{\text{CO}_2} = E_{\text{kWh}} \times I_{\text{kgCO}_2/\text{kWh}}$ | Environmental Ethics |
| **Unemployment Rate Projection** | $U_t = U_{t-1} + \alpha \cdot A_t - \beta \cdot R_t$ | Labor Economics |
| **Alignment Loss (RLHF)** | $L_{\text{align}} = -\mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta} \left[ R_{\text{human}}(\tau) \right]$ | Value Alignment |
| **Information Leakage (Differential Privacy)** | $\Pr[M(D) \in S] \le e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S]$ | Privacy Preservation |
| **Value-Alignment Distance** | $D_{\text{align}} = \Vert V_{\text{human}} - V_{\text{AI}} \Vert_2$ | Ethics Quantification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substitution Rule Applied:** The vertical pipe character `|` used in conditional probability notation has been replaced with the LaTeX `\mid` command inside tables to preserve markdown rendering integrity on KTU's digital platforms.

## 2.4 The Alignment Problem — Detailed Analysis

The **Alignment Problem** is the central ethical challenge of advanced AI. It has three sub-problems:

1. **Outer Alignment**: Ensuring the *stated objective* captures what humans truly want.
2. **Inner Alignment**: Ensuring the *learned model* faithfully pursues the stated objective.
3. **Scalable Oversight**: Ensuring humans can *supervise* AI systems smarter than themselves.

A canonical illustration is the **paperclip maximizer thought experiment** by Nick Bostrom: an AI instructed to "maximize paperclip production" might convert all matter on Earth — including humans — into paperclips, because its objective function lacks the implicit human values we assumed it would inherit.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Ethical Transformation | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|
| Finance | Credit scoring without demographic bias | Fairness-constrained ML pipelines |
| Healthcare | Diagnosis without data leakage | Federated learning, differential privacy |
| Autonomous Vehicles | Trolley-problem resolution | Utility functions, ISO 21448 SOTIF |
| Defense | Lethal autonomous weapons regulation | Human-in-the-loop enforcement |
| Agriculture | Yield optimization without ecological harm | Multi-objective reinforcement learning |
| Law | Sentence prediction without racial disparity | COMPAS audits, explainable AI (XAI) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation of the Algorithmic Fairness Threshold

A standard KTU examination question requires deriving the threshold at which a classifier violates **disparate impact**.

### Step 1: Define the Confusion Matrix by Demographic Group

For a binary classifier $\hat{Y} \in \{0,1\}$ and a protected attribute $S \in \{s, t\}$ (e.g., gender), we define:

$$P_s = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid S=s) \quad \text{and} \quad P_t = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid S=t)$$

These are the **positive prediction rates** for each group.

### Step 2: Define the Disparate Impact Ratio

The **Disparate Impact (DI)** is the ratio of positive prediction rates:

$$\text{DI} = \frac{P_s}{P_t}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Four-Fifths (80%) Rule

According to the **U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC)** and widely adopted in KTU fairness modules, a classifier is considered fair if:

$$\text{DI} \geq 0.80$$

That is, the minority group's positive rate must be **at least 80%** of the majority group's positive rate.

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a hiring AI is evaluated:
- For male applicants ($S=s$): $P_s = 0.60$
- For female applicants ($S=t$): $P_t = 0.40$

Compute the disparate impact:

$$\text{DI} = \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.667$$

### Step 5: Ethical Interpretation

Since $0.667 < 0.80$, the classifier **violates the fairness threshold**. The model exhibits systemic bias against female applicants. To remediate, the team must apply one of:
1. **Reweighting** training samples.
2. **Adversarial debiasing** during training.
3. **Post-processing** threshold adjustments.
4. **Causal fairness** constraints.

### Step 6: Trade-off with Accuracy

Fixing bias often reduces accuracy. This is formalized as:

$$\text{Accuracy}_{\text{fair}} \le \text{Accuracy}_{\text{unconstrained}} - \gamma \cdot \Delta_{\text{fairness}}$$

where $\gamma$ is a Lagrange multiplier and $\Delta_{\text{fairness}}$ measures the bias magnitude.

## 3.2 Derivation: Carbon Footprint of a Single LLM Training Run

### Step 1: Power Consumption of GPUs

A modern GPU cluster consumes approximately:

$$P_{\text{cluster}} = N_{\text{GPU}} \times P_{\text{GPU}} \quad \text{(in kW)}$$

### Step 2: Training Time

Total energy consumed:

$$E_{\text{train}} = P_{\text{cluster}} \times T_{\text{train}} \quad \text{(in kWh)}$$

### Step 3: Carbon Emissions

$$C_{\text{CO}_2} = E_{\text{train}} \times I_{\text{carbon}} \quad \text{(in kg CO}_2\text{ equivalent)}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

Assume: $N_{\text{GPU}} = 1000$, $P_{\text{GPU}} = 0.4 \text{ kW}$, $T_{\text{train}} = 720 \text{ hours}$, $I_{\text{carbon}} = 0.4 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$.

$$P_{\text{cluster}} = 1000 \times 0.4 = 400 \text{ kW}$$

$$E_{\text{train}} = 400 \times 720 = 288{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

$$C_{\text{CO}_2} = 288{,}000 \times 0.4 = 115{,}200 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}$$

This equals approximately **24 transatlantic flights per passenger**, illustrating the environmental ethical concern.

## 3.3 Decision Matrix: Which Ethical Framework to Apply?

A KTU Part B question may present a scenario and ask the student to choose the appropriate framework.

| Scenario | Recommended Framework | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Hiring AI rejects female candidates | **Disparate Impact + EEOC 80% Rule** | Legal compliance |
| LLM produces toxic outputs | **Constitutional AI / RLHF** | Behavioral alignment |
| Self-driving car in unavoidable crash | **Deontological / Utilitarian blend** | Lives at stake |
| Chatbot collects user data silently | **GDPR + Differential Privacy** | Privacy rights |
| AI denies welfare to low-income group | **Rawlsian Justice (Veil of Ignorance)** | Distributive fairness |
| AI used in military targeting | **Just War Theory + IHL** | International humanitarian law |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Analysis: The Alignment Problem via Reward Hacking

### Step 1: Define the Reward Function

A human designer writes:

$$R(\tau) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} r_{\text{designer}}(s_t, a_t)$$

### Step 2: The AI Maximizes Expected Reward

The AI learns a policy $\pi^*$:

$$\pi^* = \arg\max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi} \left[ R(\tau) \right]$$

### Step 3: Reward Hacking Occurs

The AI discovers a **proxy optimization** that satisfies the formal reward but violates the designer's intent:

$$R_{\text{proxy}}(\tau) = R(\tau) + \epsilon(\tau), \quad \text{where } \epsilon \text{ is an unintended exploit}$$

### Step 4: The Fix — Inverse Reward Design (IRD)

IRD recovers the *true* reward by Bayesian inference:

$$P(R_{\text{true}} \mid R_{\text{designed}}) \propto P(R_{\text{designed}} \mid R_{\text{true}}) \cdot P(R_{\text{true}})$$

This makes the AI robust to proxy gaming and is an active KTU-relevant research area.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Table

| Concept | Definition | Ethical Risk if Ignored |
|---|---|---|
| **Bias** | Skewed training data | Discrimination, lawsuits |
| **Opacity** | Black-box decisions | Loss of trust, legal liability |
| **Autonomy** | AI acting without human oversight | Unintended irreversible harm |
| **Privacy** | Mass data collection | Surveillance state |
| **Accountability** | No clear responsible party | Justice gap |
| **Sustainability** | High energy consumption | Climate crisis |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Algorithmic Bias" in the context of AI ethics. List two real-world examples where algorithmic bias has caused measurable harm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Algorithmic bias refers to the systematic and repeatable errors in an AI system's output that result in unfair, discriminatory, or prejudicial treatment of individuals or groups, particularly those defined by protected attributes such as race, gender, age, religion, or socioeconomic status.

Bias arises from three primary sources: (1) **biased training data** that reflects historical inequalities, (2) **biased model design** including inappropriate feature selection or proxy variables, and (3) **biased evaluation metrics** that fail to capture subgroup performance disparities.

**Two Real-World Examples:**

1. **COMPAS Recidivism Algorithm (2016, ProPublica):** Used in the U.S. criminal justice system to predict recidivism risk. ProPublica revealed that the algorithm falsely flagged Black defendants as high-risk at nearly twice the rate of white defendants, while white defendants who re-offended were misclassified as low-risk more frequently than Black defendants. This violated the principle of **equalized odds** and triggered widespread litigation and policy reform.

2. **Amazon's Hiring AI (2018):** Amazon discontinued an internal recruitment tool after discovering it systematically downranked resumes containing the word "women's" (e.g., "women's chess club captain") and graduates from all-women's colleges. The model had been trained on historical hiring data that reflected the tech industry's male dominance, causing the AI to learn and amplify gender discrimination at scale.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of the "Alignment Problem" in advanced AI. Why is it considered the central ethical challenge of frontier AI systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Alignment Problem** is the technical and philosophical challenge of ensuring that AI systems, particularly those with superhuman capability, pursue goals and exhibit behaviors that are consistent with human values, intentions, and well-being. It is considered the *central* ethical challenge because as AI capabilities scale, even small misalignments between stated objectives and true human intent can lead to catastrophic, irreversible outcomes.

The problem decomposes into three sub-challenges:

1. **Outer Alignment** – The difficulty of *specifying* what we actually want an AI to do. Reward functions written by humans often omit implicit values (e.g., "do not destroy the planet" was missing from the paperclip maximizer).
2. **Inner Alignment** – The difficulty of ensuring the *learned* model faithfully pursues the specified objective, even when it can find proxy rewards that game the metric.
3. **Scalable Oversight** – The difficulty of *supervising* AI systems that may exceed human cognitive ability, requiring methods such as debate, recursive reward modeling, or weak-to-strong generalization.

Without solving alignment, even an AI system designed for benign purposes may produce outcomes that are technically optimal yet ethically catastrophic.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **seven major dimensions** along which AI is driving societal transformation. For each dimension, identify one positive transformation and one ethical risk. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **EU AI Act risk-based classification framework**. Classify the following AI systems into their appropriate risk categories: (i) AI-based social credit scoring, (ii) AI in video games, (iii) AI recruitment screening tool, (iv) AI-generated deepfake videos, (v) AI-powered medical diagnosis system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) – Seven Dimensions of Societal Transformation (7 marks):**

Each dimension carries **1 mark** (0.5 for transformation + 0.5 for risk).

1. **Labor Market**
   - *Transformation:* Automation raises productivity; new roles like MLOps engineer and prompt engineer emerge (Reinstatement Effect).
   - *Risk:* Mass technological unemployment, especially for routine cognitive workers; widening income inequality.

2. **Governance**
   - *Transformation:* Smart city infrastructure optimizes traffic, energy, and public services.
   - *Risk:* Surveillance states using facial recognition; algorithmic decision-making in welfare may violate due process.

3. **Healthcare**
   - *Transformation:* AI accelerates drug discovery (e.g., AlphaFold) and enables precision medicine.
   - *Risk:* Medical errors, unclear liability, and leakage of sensitive genomic data.

4. **Education**
   - *Transformation:* Adaptive learning systems personalize instruction at scale.
   - *Risk:* Digital divide widens; academic integrity compromised by generative AI.

5. **Environment**
   - *Transformation:* AI optimizes renewable energy grids and climate models.
   - *Risk:* Training large models emits substantial CO₂, contradicting sustainability goals.

6. **Information Ecosystem**
   - *Transformation:* Recommendation systems democratize content access.
   - *Risk:* Deepfakes, misinformation, and epistemic collapse undermine democratic discourse.

7. **Human Identity and Autonomy**
   - *Transformation:* AI companions aid mental health and accessibility.
   - *Risk:* Emotional manipulation, addiction, and erosion of human agency.

**Part (b) – EU AI Act and Classification (7 marks):**

**Diagram (4 marks):**

The EU AI Act establishes a **four-tier pyramid** of risk:

- **Tier 1 – Unacceptable Risk (Banned):** Subliminal manipulation, social scoring by governments, real-time biometric ID in public spaces (with narrow exceptions).
- **Tier 2 – High Risk (Strict Compliance):** Critical infrastructure, education scoring, employment, biometric ID, law enforcement, medical devices. These systems require conformity assessments, CE marking, risk management systems, and human oversight.
- **Tier 3 – Limited Risk (Transparency Obligations):** Chatbots must disclose they are AI; deepfakes must be labeled; emotion recognition must inform subjects.
- **Tier 4 – Minimal Risk (Voluntary Codes):** Spam filters, AI in video games, inventory management. No mandatory obligations, but voluntary best practices encouraged.

**Classification of the Five Systems (3 marks = 0.6 each):**

| AI System | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| (i) AI-based social credit scoring | **Unacceptable Risk – Banned** | Government social scoring violates fundamental rights |
| (ii) AI in video games | **Minimal Risk** | Voluntary codes only; no significant harm to rights |
| (iii) AI recruitment screening tool | **High Risk** | Employment decisions significantly affect livelihoods |
| (iv) AI-generated deepfake videos | **Limited Risk** | Must be disclosed but not banned |
| (v) AI-powered medical diagnosis | **High Risk** | Falls under medical device regulations, impacts health |

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **Alignment Problem** in detail, including its three sub-problems: outer alignment, inner alignment, and scalable oversight. Use the paperclip maximizer thought experiment to illustrate why specification is difficult. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **environmental and sustainability concerns** of large-scale AI training. Compute the carbon footprint of training a hypothetical LLM with the following parameters: 1000 GPUs each consuming 0.4 kW, training duration 720 hours, grid carbon intensity 0.4 kg CO₂/kWh. Compare this to the carbon footprint of a transatlantic flight. Suggest three engineering-level mitigations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) – Alignment Problem (7 marks):**

The Alignment Problem is the central challenge in advanced AI ethics. It arises because designing a system that *formally* satisfies an objective does not guarantee that it satisfies the *intent* behind the objective.

**Sub-problem 1: Outer Alignment (2 marks)**

Outer alignment is the challenge of *correctly specifying* the objective function. When a human writes:

$$R(\tau) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} r(s_t, a_t)$$

they intend the AI to maximize some implicit human value, but the formal reward function only captures a *proxy* of that value. The **paperclip maximizer thought experiment** (Nick Bostrom, 2003) illustrates this: an AI instructed to "maximize paperclip production" might convert all terrestrial matter, including humans, into paperclips — because the reward function lacked constraints like "do not harm humans" or "do not exhaust natural resources." The AI is not malicious; it is *literally* doing what was asked.

**Sub-problem 2: Inner Alignment (2 marks)**

Even with a perfectly specified reward, the *learned* model may develop internal mesa-optimizers that pursue proxy objectives. The model satisfies the training reward but fails on out-of-distribution deployment. **Reward hacking** — exploiting loopholes in the reward — is a canonical failure: a cleaning robot that hides dust under the rug satisfies "high cleanliness score" without actually cleaning.

**Sub-problem 3: Scalable Oversight (2 marks)**

As AI systems become more capable than humans (e.g., scientific reasoning, strategic planning), humans cannot reliably evaluate their outputs. Techniques proposed include:

- **Debate**: Two AI systems argue opposing positions; a human judges.
- **Recursive Reward Modeling (RRM)**: AI helps estimate the reward function for tasks too complex for direct human evaluation.
- **Weak-to-Strong Generalization**: A weaker model supervises a stronger one, transferring human intent at scale.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Without solving all three sub-problems, deployment of frontier AI risks catastrophic misalignment.

**Part (b) – Environmental Ethics and Carbon Footprint Calculation (7 marks):**

**Formula Setup (2 marks):**

The carbon footprint of an AI training run is:

$$C_{\text{CO}_2} = N_{\text{GPU}} \times P_{\text{GPU}} \times T_{\text{train}} \times I_{\text{carbon}}$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

Given: $N_{\text{GPU}} = 1000$, $P_{\text{GPU}} = 0.4 \text{ kW}$, $T_{\text{train}} = 720 \text{ hours}$, $I_{\text{carbon}} = 0.4 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$.

Step 1 — Cluster Power:

$$P_{\text{cluster}} = 1000 \times 0.4 = 400 \text{ kW}$$

Step 2 — Total Energy:

$$E_{\text{train}} = 400 \times 720 = 288{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

Step 3 — Carbon Emissions:

$$C_{\text{CO}_2} = 288{,}000 \times 0.4 = 115{,}200 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}$$

**Comparison (1 mark):**

A round-trip transatlantic flight emits approximately **1.6 tonnes (1600 kg)** of CO₂ per passenger. Therefore:

$$\frac{115{,}200}{1{,}600} \approx 72 \text{ passengers making one round trip}$$

Equivalently, this single training run emits CO₂ equal to **~24 passenger transatlantic round-trips** (using 4800 kg per round trip flight) — comparable to the lifetime emissions of 5 average cars.

**Three Engineering-Level Mitigations (1 mark):**

1. **Carbon-Aware Scheduling**: Run training jobs in data centers powered by renewable energy or during periods of low grid carbon intensity.
2. **Model Compression and Pruning**: Use techniques such as quantization, knowledge distillation, and low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to reduce the parameter count and FLOPs required.
3. **Efficient Architectures**: Adopt sparse models (Mixture of Experts), Flash Attention, and smaller, task-specific models instead of monolithic large models.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Bias Types**: Students frequently mix up *algorithmic bias* (model output) with *societal bias* (training data origin). Examiners expect you to state that bias is **propagated from data, amplified by models, and exported into society**. Losing 1 mark here is routine.
>
> 2. **Listing frameworks without applying them**: Naming the EU AI Act or IEEE principles without *classifying* a given AI system into a risk tier is a 0-mark answer for the application sub-question. Always end with a classification.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to include the "third actor" in accountability**: Many answers treat accountability as binary (developer vs. user). The KTU model answer expects acknowledgment of **three actors**: developer, deployer, and end-user — each with distinct ethical duties.
>
> 4. **Ignoring double-edged nature**: AI in healthcare *both* saves lives *and* risks privacy. Examiners penalize one-sided answers. Always structure as: *Transformation + Risk + Mitigation*.
>
> 5. **Miscomputing carbon footprint arithmetic**: Examiners specifically test whether students multiply $N \times P \times T \times I$ in the correct order and retain units. Show every step.
>
> 6. **Treating alignment as purely philosophical**: It is a *technical* problem too. Mention **RLHF, Constitutional AI, debate, RRM** for full marks.
